When a P-type and N-type semiconductor are brought into intimate contact, a **PN junction** is formed. Three distinct regions emerge:

1. **Depletion Region**: A narrow zone near the junction devoid of mobile charge carriers, containing only **immobile ionized dopants** (positive ions on N-side, negative ions on P-side).
2. **Space Charge / Junction Capacitance**: Behaves like a tiny parallel-plate capacitor $C_j = \dfrac{\epsilon_s A}{W}$, where $W$ is depletion width.
3. **Built-in Potential (Barrier Voltage)**:
$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$
For Si at 300 K, typical $V_{bi} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$. For Ge, $V_{bi} \approx 0.3\ \text{V}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the depletion region forms:** Electrons from N-side diffuse into P-side and recombine with holes. This leaves behind a layer of **positive donor ions** on the N-side and **negative acceptor ions** on the P-side, creating an internal electric field $\vec{E}$ pointing from N → P that opposes further diffusion — equilibrium is reached.

## 2.2 The PN Junction Diode — Operating Modes

| Operating Mode | Applied Voltage | Current Flow | Depletion Region |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Forward Bias** | $V > 0$ (P +, N −) | Large (exponential) | Narrows drastically |
| **Reverse Bias** | $V < 0$ (P −, N +) | Tiny ($\mu A$, leakage) | Widens |
| **Breakdown** | $V < -V_{BR}$ | Large (avalanche/Zener) | Destroyed if not limited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always check whether the diode is **Forward Biased** ($V_{AK} > 0.7\ \text{V}$ for Si) or **Reverse Biased** before applying any current formula.

## 2.3 The Ideal Diode Equation — Shockley's Equation

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / (nV_T)} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $I_D$ = Diode current (A)
- $I_S$ = Reverse saturation current (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}\ \text{A}$)
- $V_D$ = Voltage across diode (V)
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 for ideal, ~1–2 for real)
- $V_T$ = Thermal voltage = $\dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85\ \text{mV}}$ at 300 K

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Forward Bias Form:** When $V_D \gg V_T$, the "-1" becomes negligible:
> $$I_D \approx I_S \cdot e^{V_D / (nV_T)}$$
> This means **every additional 60 mV** ($= 2.3 \times V_T$ for $n=1$) of forward voltage **multiplies the diode current by a factor of 10** — a critical relationship for understanding diode sensitivity.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Exam Importance |
|:-:|:-------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage | V (≈ 25.85 mV @ 300 K) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $I_D = I_S\left(e^{V_D/nV_T}-1\right)$ | Shockley equation | A | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}$ | Built-in potential | V | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ | Mass action law | cm⁻⁶ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$ | Conductivity | S/m | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $W \propto \sqrt{V_{bi} - V_D}$ | Depletion width | m | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $C_j = \dfrac{\epsilon_s A}{W}$ | Junction capacitance | F | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $r_d = \dfrac{nV_T}{I_D}$ | Dynamic (AC) resistance | Ω | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $V_D = V_T \ln\dfrac{I_D}{I_S}$ | Diode voltage (large signal) | V | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $P_D = V_D \cdot I_D$ | Diode power dissipation | W | ⭐⭐⭐ |

**Physical Constants Table (KEEP IN YOUR FORMULA SHEET):**

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:--------:|:------:|:-----:|:----:|
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| Electron charge | $q$ | $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Permittivity of free space | $\epsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Relative permittivity of Si | $\epsilon_r$ | 11.7 | — |
| Relative permittivity of Ge | $\epsilon_r$ | 16.0 | — |

## 2.5 Special-Purpose Diodes — Engineering Quick-Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — These four special diodes are almost always asked as short questions in KTU exams:**

### (a) Zener Diode
- Operates in **reverse breakdown region** as a **voltage regulator**.
- Breakdown voltage $V_Z$ is precisely controlled by doping level.
- **Application**: Voltage reference, over-voltage protection, simple regulator.

### (b) Light Emitting Diode (LED)
- Recombination of electrons and holes at the junction releases energy as **photons**.
- Wavelength of emitted light: $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$.
- Forward voltage $V_F \approx 1.8$ to $3.2\ \text{V}$ (color dependent).
- **Application**: Indicators, displays, optocouplers, optical encoders (mechatronic feedback!).

### (c) Photodiode
- Operates in **reverse bias**. Incident light creates electron-hole pairs, increasing reverse current.
- **Application**: Light sensors, line-following robots, optical encoders, speed measurement.

### (d) Schottky Diode
- Metal-semiconductor junction (not PN). Very low forward drop ($0.2 - 0.4\ \text{V}$), very fast switching.
- **Application**: High-frequency rectifiers, fast switching in SMPS, clamping in logic circuits.

## 2.6 The BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor) — Mechatronic Workhorse

> [!NOTE]
> **BJT Structure:** Two PN junctions back-to-back, forming either **NPN** or **PNP**. Three terminals: **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, **Collector (C)**. The base is extremely thin and lightly doped.

### Modes of Operation
- **Active (Forward Active)**: BE junction forward-biased, BC junction reverse-biased → **Amplification**.
- **Saturation**: Both junctions forward-biased → Fully ON, $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ → **Switch CLOSED**.
- **Cutoff**: Both junctions reverse-biased → Fully OFF, $I_C \approx 0$ → **Switch OPEN**.
- **Reverse Active**: BE reverse, BC forward — rarely used (analogous to reversed diode).

> [!TIP]
> **Mechatronics Tip:** A BJT driven between **Cutoff and Saturation** acts as a perfect electronic switch — ideal for driving relays, solenoids, and DC motor coils. Use a **flyback diode** across inductive loads!

### Key BJT Equations (KTU Must-Know)

$$I_E = I_B + I_C$$

$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = \alpha \cdot I_E$$

$$\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}, \quad \alpha = \frac{\beta}{1+\beta}$$

$$I_{CEO} = (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$$

$$\text{Early Effect: } I_C = I_S \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}\left(1 + \frac{V_{CE}}{V_A}\right)$$

Where $V_A$ = Early voltage, $\beta$ = common-emitter current gain (typically 50–300).

## 2.7 The MOSFET — Modern Mechatronics King

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MOSFETs dominate mechatronics:** They are **voltage-controlled** (almost zero gate current), have extremely **high input impedance**, switch very fast, and can handle high currents — perfect for PWM motor control, H-bridges, and power electronics.

### Types of MOSFETs
- **N-channel Enhancement (E-MOS)** — Most common in mechatronic drivers.
- **P-channel Enhancement** — Used in high-side switches.
- **N-channel Depletion** — Normally ON, less common.

### Key MOSFET Equations

$$I_D = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } V_{GS} < V_{th} \text{ (cutoff)} \\ k_n \left[2(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2\right] & \text{(triode)} \\ \dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS}) & \text{(saturation)} \end{cases}$$

Where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ is the **transconductance parameter**.

**Switching Applications** — When $V_{GS} \gg V_{th}$, MOSFET is in **triode** → behaves as a resistor $R_{DS(on)}$ — essentially a closed switch.

## 2.8 Real-World Mechatronic Utility

| Mechatronic Subsystem | Semiconductor Device Used |
|----------------------|---------------------------|
| DC Motor speed control | Power MOSFET / IGBT in PWM H-bridge |
| Optical encoder feedback | Photodiode + comparator IC |
| Temperature sensing | Thermistor + op-amp conditioning |
| Solenoid/Relay driving | BJT/MOSFET switch + flyback diode |
| Battery charging | Schottky diode bridge rectifier |
| Status indication | LED |
| Electrical isolation | Optocoupler (LED + phototransistor) |
| Logic-level decision making | BJT in saturation/cutoff (switching) |
| Signal amplification | BJT/MOSFET in active region |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation of the Diode Current Equation (Shockley's Equation)

### Starting Point: The Continuity Equation
The net current in a semiconductor arises from the **diffusion** of charge carriers across the junction. Under forward bias, minority carrier concentrations at the edges of the depletion region are governed by the **law of the junction**:

$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \cdot e^{V_D / V_T}, \quad n_p(0) = n_{p0} \cdot e^{V_D / V_T}$$

### Step 1: Diffusion Currents at the Edges
The diffusion current of minority carriers at the depletion edge is proportional to the excess concentration gradient:

$$I_{p,\text{diff}} = -q A D_p \frac{dp}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0_n}, \quad I_{n,\text{diff}} = q A D_n \frac{dn}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0_p}$$

### Step 2: Integrating the Diffusion Equation
Assume minority carriers decay exponentially into the neutral regions with characteristic diffusion lengths $L_p$ and $L_n$:

$$\frac{dp}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0_n} = -\frac{p_n(0) - p_{n0}}{L_p} = -\frac{p_{n0}}{L_p}\left(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1\right)$$

### Step 3: Total Diode Current (Sum of Electron and Hole Diffusion Currents)
$$I_D = I_{p,\text{diff}} + I_{n,\text{diff}} = qA\left(\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)\left(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1\right)$$

### Step 4: Define Reverse Saturation Current
$$I_S \equiv qA\left(\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)$$

Using Einstein relations ($D_p = \mu_p V_T$, $D_n = \mu_n V_T$) and mass action law ($n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$, $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$):

$$\boxed{\,I_D = I_S\left(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1\right)\,}$$

### Step 5: Inclusion of Ideality Factor (Empirical Correction)
For real diodes, carrier recombination in the depletion region modifies the equation:

$$\boxed{\,I_D = I_S\left(e^{V_D/(nV_T)} - 1\right)\,}$$

where $n$ ranges from 1 (ideal) to 2 (heavy recombination).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Forward Voltage from a Given Current

**Problem:** A silicon diode has $I_S = 10^{-14}\ \text{A}$ and ideality factor $n = 1.5$. Find the forward voltage $V_D$ required to produce a current of $I_D = 5\ \text{mA}$ at 300 K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write down the Shockley equation:**
$$I_D = I_S \left(e^{V_D/(nV_T)} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $V_D$:**
$$V_D = nV_T \ln\left(\frac{I_D}{I_S} + 1\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
- $n = 1.5$
- $V_T = 25.85 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$
- $I_D = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$
- $I_S = 10^{-14}\ \text{A}$

$$\frac{I_D}{I_S} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-14}} = 5 \times 10^{11}$$

Since this is enormous, the "+1" is negligible:
$$\frac{I_D}{I_S} + 1 \approx 5 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the natural logarithm:**
$$\ln(5 \times 10^{11}) = \ln 5 + 11 \ln 10 = 1.609 + 11 \times 2.303 = 1.609 + 25.333 = 26.94$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**
$$V_D = 1.5 \times 0.02585 \times 26.94 = 0.03878 \times 26.94 \approx 1.045\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_D \approx 1.045\ \text{V}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A current of 5 mA requires about 1.05 V across the diode — a bit higher than the textbook "0.7 V" because the ideality factor is 1.5 (slightly non-ideal).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: AC Dynamic Resistance of a Diode

**Problem:** For the diode in Example 1 at $I_D = 5\ \text{mA}$, calculate the **small-signal dynamic resistance** $r_d$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** The small-signal resistance is the inverse slope of the I-V curve at the operating point:
$$r_d = \left(\frac{dI_D}{dV_D}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate Shockley's equation:**
$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_D} = \frac{I_S}{nV_T} \cdot e^{V_D/(nV_T)} = \frac{I_D + I_S}{nV_T} \approx \frac{I_D}{nV_T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**
$$r_d = \frac{nV_T}{I_D} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.02585}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.03878}{0.005} = 7.756\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\,r_d \approx 7.76\ \Omega\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mechatronics Insight:** At low currents, $r_d$ is large (e.g., at 1 mA, $r_d \approx 38.8\ \Omega$). At high currents (100 mA), $r_d \approx 0.39\ \Omega$. This is why **diode drop becomes more stable at higher currents** — useful in precision rectifier design.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: BJT Base Resistor Design (Switching Application)

**Problem:** Design the base resistor $R_B$ for an NPN BJT switch driving a 24 V, 100 mA DC relay coil. Given: $V_{CC} = 24\ \text{V}$, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $\beta_{forced} = 20$ (to ensure saturation), microcontroller output $V_{OH} = 5\ \text{V}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine required base current for saturation:**
$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{L}} \approx \frac{24 - 0.2}{V_{CC}/I_{coil}}$$

Assume $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ and the relay coil draws $100\ \text{mA}$:
$$I_{C(sat)} \approx 100\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Required base current (using forced $\beta$):**
$$I_{B} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{20} = 5\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL across base loop:**
$$V_{OH} = I_B R_B + V_{BE(on)}$$
$$5 = (5 \times 10^{-3}) R_B + 0.7$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_B$:**
$$R_B = \frac{5 - 0.7}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.3}{0.005} = 860\ \Omega$$

Use the nearest standard value: $R_B = \mathbf{820\ \Omega}$.

$$\boxed{\,R_B \approx 820\ \Omega\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always include a **flyback diode** (1N4007 or similar) anti-parallel with the relay coil, cathode at $+V_{CC}$, anode at collector. This protects the BJT from the inductive kickback of $V_L = -L\,dI/dt$ when the relay is switched off.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Diode Quick-Decision Table

| If you are given... | And you need... | Use this formula... |
|:-------------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|
| $I_D$, $I_S$ | $V_D$ | $V_D = nV_T \ln(I_D/I_S + 1)$ |
| $V_D$, $I_S$ | $I_D$ | $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ |
| $I_D$ at Q-point | $r_d$ | $r_d = nV_T / I_D$ |
| $N_A$, $N_D$, $n_i$ | $V_{bi}$ | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ |
| $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | $\beta = \alpha / (1-\alpha)$ |
| $\beta$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \beta / (1+\beta)$ |
| $I_C$, $\beta$ | $I_B$ | $I_B = I_C / \beta$ |
| $I_{CBO}$, $\beta$ | $I_{CEO}$ | $I_{CEO} = (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Functional Schematics

## 4.1 Semiconductor Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Semiconductor Materials"]:::root --> B["Elemental: Si, Ge"]
    A --> C["Compound: GaAs, SiC, GaN"]
    B --> D["Intrinsic Pure"]
    B --> E["Extrinsic Doped"]
    D --> D1["Equal e and h carriers"]
    D --> D2["Fermi level at mid-gap"]
    E --> F["N-type Pentavalent Dopant"]
    E --> G["P-type Trivalent Dopant"]
    F --> F1["Donor: P As Sb"]
    F --> F2["Majority: electrons"]
    F --> F3["Fermi level near CB"]
    G --> G1["Acceptor: B Ga In"]
    G --> G2["Majority: holes"]
    G --> G3["Fermi level near VB"]
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ntype fill:#065f46,stroke:#047857,color:#fff
    classDef ptype fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#c2410c,color:#fff
    classDef intrinsic fill:#581c87,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#fff
    class F,F1,F2,F3 ntype
    class G,G1,G2,G3 ptype
    class D,D1,D2 intrinsic
```

## 4.2 PN Junction Formation — Energy Band Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[P-type Region]:::p --> J["Junction\nDepletion Zone"]:::j
    J --> N[N-type Region]:::n
    P2[Valence Band P-side]:::pb --> EF1["Fermi Level P"]:::ef
    EF1 --> CB1["Conduction Band P"]:::cb
    N2["Conduction Band N"]:::cb --> EF2["Fermi Level N"]:::ef
    EF2 --> VB2["Valence Band N"]:::pb
    J -.->|"Built-in field E"| FIELD[Internal E-field opposes diffusion]:::warn
    classDef p fill:#7c2d12,color:#fff,stroke:#c2410c
    classDef n fill:#065f46,color:#fff,stroke:#047857
    classDef j fill:#374151,color:#fff,stroke:#1f2937
    classDef pb fill:#92400e,color:#fff
    classDef cb fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff
    classDef ef fill:#fbbf24,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#b91c1c,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Diode I-V Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    AX["X-axis: V_D Voltage"] --> O[Origin 0,0]:::o
    AY["Y-axis: I_D Current"] --> O
    O --> Q1["Forward Bias Region V_D greater than 0.7V"]:::fwd
    O --> Q3["Reverse Bias Region V_D less than 0"]:::rev
    O --> Q4["Breakdown V_D less than V_BR"]:::brk
    Q1 --> Q1a["Exponential rise of current"]:::fwd
    Q1 --> Q1b["Knee voltage 0.7V for Si, 0.3V for Ge"]:::fwd
    Q3 --> Q3a["Tiny reverse saturation I_S in microampere"]:::rev
    Q4 --> Q4a["Avalanche or Zener breakdown"]:::brk
    Q4 --> Q4b["Controlled by external current limit"]:::brk
    classDef o fill:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fwd fill:#065f46,color:#fff
    classDef rev fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff
    classDef brk fill:#7c2d12,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mechatronic Functional Architecture — Semiconductor Devices in a System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Sensors Layer"]
        S1["Temperature Sensor Thermistor"]:::sensor
        S2["Light Sensor Photodiode"]:::sensor
        S3["Position Sensor Encoder"]:::sensor
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL["Signal Conditioning"]
        SC1["Amplifier BJT Active"]:::amp
        SC2["Filter RC Network"]:::filter
        SC3["Rectifier Diode Bridge"]:::rect
    end
    subgraph LOGIC["Decision and Control"]
        L1["Microcontroller"]:::mcu
        L2["Comparator Op-amp"]:::comp
    end
    subgraph DRIVER["Actuator Driver Power"]
        D1["MOSFET Switch"]:::mos
        D2["BJT Switch"]:::bjt
        D3["H-Bridge Driver"]:::hb
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Actuators and Indicators"]
        O1["DC Motor"]:::act
        O2["Solenoid Valve"]:::act
        O3["LED Indicator"]:::led
```

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### Q1. Differentiate between intrinsic, N-type, and P-type semiconductors. Mention the majority and minority carriers in each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured):**

- **Intrinsic Semiconductor** (1 Mark): A pure semiconductor crystal (Si or Ge) with no impurity doping. At thermal equilibrium, the number of free electrons equals the number of holes: $n_i = p_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ for Si at 300 K. The Fermi level lies at the middle of the band gap.
- **N-type Semiconductor** (1 Mark): Doped with pentavalent impurities (P, As, Sb) that donate free electrons. **Majority carriers = electrons**, **minority carriers = holes**. Fermi level shifts closer to the conduction band.
- **P-type Semiconductor** (1 Mark): Doped with trivalent impurities (B, Ga, In) that create holes. **Majority carriers = holes**, **minority carriers = electrons**. Fermi level shifts closer to the valence band.

> The mass action law $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ holds in all three cases.

---

### Q2. With a neat sketch, explain the V-I characteristics of a PN junction diode and mark the knee voltage and breakdown regions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **PN junction diode** is a two-terminal semiconductor device that conducts current easily in one direction (forward bias) and blocks it in the other (reverse bias).

**Forward Bias (1 Mark):** When the P-side is connected to the positive terminal of the battery, the depletion region narrows. Current rises exponentially with voltage following $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$. For Si, conduction begins appreciably after the **knee voltage** of $0.7\ \text{V}$.

**Reverse Bias (1 Mark):** When the P-side is connected to the negative terminal, the depletion region widens. Only a tiny **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ (in $\mu$A) flows due to thermally generated minority carriers.

**Breakdown Region (1 Mark):** If the reverse voltage exceeds the **breakdown voltage** $V_{BR}$, the diode enters avalanche or Zener breakdown, with a sharp increase in reverse current. This region is exploited in Zener diodes for voltage regulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *Students answer ONE full question (either Q-A or Q-B). Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the Shockley diode equation starting from the law of the junction. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — State the Law of the Junction:**
At the edge of the depletion region, the minority carrier concentration under forward bias $V_D$ is:
$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \cdot e^{V_D/V_T}, \quad n_p(0) = n_{p0} \cdot e^{V_D/V_T}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Assumptions:**
1. One-dimensional current flow.
2. Steady-state (no time variation).
3. Depletion region is narrow compared to diffusion lengths $L_p, L_n$.
4. No generation/recombination in the depletion region.
5. Low-level injection.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Minority Carrier Diffusion:**
In the neutral N-region, excess holes decay exponentially with diffusion length $L_p$:
$$p_n(x) = p_{n0} + (p_n(0) - p_{n0}) e^{-x/L_p}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Hole Diffusion Current at $x = 0_n$:**
$$I_p = -qA D_p \frac{dp}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0_n} = \frac{qA D_p p_{n0}}{L_p}\left(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Total Diode Current:**
Summing hole diffusion (N-side) and electron diffusion (P-side):
$$I_D = qA\left(\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)\left(e^{V_D/V_T} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Final Form:**
Defining $I_S = qA\left(\dfrac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \dfrac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right)$ and including ideality factor $n$:
$$\boxed{\,I_D = I_S\left(e^{V_D/(nV_T)} - 1\right)\,}$$

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] For a silicon diode with $I_S = 10^{-13}\ \text{A}$ and $n = 1.2$, calculate: (i) the forward voltage at $I_D = 10\ \text{mA}$, and (ii) the small-signal dynamic resistance at this operating point. Take $V_T = 25\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Forward Voltage (4 Marks):**

Starting from the Shockley equation:
$$I_D = I_S\left(e^{V_D/(nV_T)} - 1\right)$$

Solving for $V_D$:
$$V_D = nV_T \ln\left(\frac{I_D}{I_S} + 1\right)$$

**Numerical substitution:**
- $nV_T = 1.2 \times 0.025 = 0.030\ \text{V}$
- $\dfrac{I_D}{I_S} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-13}} = 10^{11}$
- Since $10^{11} \gg 1$, the "+1" is negligible.

$$V_D = 0.030 \times \ln(10^{11}) = 0.030 \times (11 \times 2.3026) = 0.030 \times 25.33$$
$$\boxed{\,V_D \approx 0.760\ \text{V}\,}$$

**(ii) Dynamic Resistance (3 Marks):**

$$r_d = \frac{nV_T}{I_D} = \frac{0.030}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.030}{0.010}$$
$$\boxed{\,r_d = 3.0\ \Omega\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert mA to A when substituting. Always write $10\ \text{mA} = 10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$ in your working.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $V_T = 26\ \text{mV}$ (rounded) when the question specifies $25\ \text{mV}$ — read the question carefully!
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Dropping the ideality factor $n$. Always check whether $n = 1$ is given; if not, include it.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to apply $\ln$ properly — use $\ln(10) = 2.3026$, not $\log_{10}$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a circuit diagram and V-I characteristics, explain the operation of a Zener diode as a voltage regulator. A Zener diode has $V_Z = 5.6\ \text{V}$, $P_{ZM} = 500\ \text{mW}$. Find the maximum current it can handle and the series resistance needed to regulate an input of $V_{in} = 12\ \text{V}$ supplying a load of $R_L = 1\ \text{k\Omega}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Theoretical Explanation (3 Marks):**
A Zener diode is a heavily doped PN junction diode designed to operate reliably in the **reverse breakdown region**. Once the reverse voltage exceeds the **Zener voltage** $V_Z$, the voltage across the diode remains nearly constant at $V_Z$ over a wide range of currents.

**Voltage Regulator Circuit:** The Zener is connected in **reverse bias** with a **series resistor** $R_S$ between $V_{in}$ and the load. The Zener is connected in parallel with the load. When $V_{in}$ varies (within limits), the excess voltage drops across $R_S$, keeping $V_L = V_Z$ constant.

**Numerical Solution (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Maximum Zener current:**
$$I_{Z(max)} = \frac{P_{ZM}}{V_Z} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{5.6} = 89.3\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Load current:**
$$I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L} = \frac{5.6}{1000} = 5.6\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across $R_S$:**
$$V_{R_S} = V_{in} - V_Z = 12 - 5.6 = 6.4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Total current through $R_S$ (which equals $I_Z + I_L$):**
$$I_{total} = I_{Z(max)} + I_L = 89.3 + 5.6 = 94.9\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Series resistance:**
$$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_{total}} = \frac{6.4}{94.9 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 67.4\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{\,R_S \approx 68\ \Omega \text{ (standard value)}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always design the regulator so that $I_{Z(min)} > 0$ (typically 5–10% of $I_{Z(max)}$) to keep the Zener in its breakdown region. This guarantees stable regulation.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Compare BJT and MOSFET as switching devices for mechatronic applications. Justify why MOSFETs are preferred for low-power motor control.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | BJT | MOSFET |
|:---------:|:---:|:------:|
| Control type | Current-controlled ($I_B$) | Voltage-controlled ($V_{GS}$) |
| Input impedance | Low (~kΩ) | Very high (~10¹² Ω) |
| Switching speed | Slower (storage time) | Very fast (ns range) |
| Drive power | Significant base current needed | Negligible gate current |
| Thermal stability | Poor (thermal runaway) | Better (positive temp coeff of $R_{DS(on)}$) |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Saturation voltage | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ | $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_D$ (typ. 0.05 V) |
| Paralleling | Difficult (negative temp coeff) | Easy (positive temp coeff) |
| Best suited for | Analog amplification, low-side switches | PWM motor control, high-frequency SMPS |

**Why MOSFETs are preferred for low-power motor control (3 Marks):**

1. **PWM Compatibility**: MOSFETs can switch at high frequencies (50 kHz to 1 MHz) needed for smooth PWM motor control. BJT storage time limits them to a few kHz.
2. **Low Drive Power**: Microcontroller GPIO pins can directly drive a logic-level MOSFET (e.g., IRLZ44N) without needing a base driver circuit.
3. **Low On-Resistance**: Modern MOSFETs have $R_{DS(on)} < 0.05\ \Omega$, leading to very low conduction loss — important for battery-powered mechatronic systems.
4. **Easy Paralleling**: The positive temperature coefficient of $R_{DS(on)}$ means MOSFETs share current equally when paralleled, which is useful in high-current motor drivers.
5. **Less Heat**: With low on-resistance and fast switching, MOSFETs dissipate less heat, allowing compact mechatronic designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **BJT vs MOSFET Confusion:** Students often mix up "current-controlled" and "voltage-controlled." Remember: BJT = Base current controls collector current; MOSFET = Gate voltage controls drain current.
> - **MOSFET Static Damage:** Never handle a MOSFET without grounding yourself — the high input impedance makes the gate susceptible to ESD damage.
> - **BJT Saturation Confusion:** Saturation does NOT mean the transistor is "saturated with charge" — it means both junctions are forward-biased and the collector current is limited by external circuit, not by $\beta$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Frequently Asked Conceptual Short Notes

| # | Quick Concept | One-Line Exam-Ready Answer |
|:-:|:-------------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | Drift current | Due to electric field applied across semiconductor |
| 2 | Diffusion current | Due to carrier concentration gradient |
| 3 | Einstein relation | $D/\mu = V_T = kT/q$ |
| 4 | Fermi level | Energy level at which probability of occupation is 50% |
| 5 | Depletion region | Region near junction with no mobile carriers |
| 6 | Knee voltage | Minimum forward voltage for significant conduction (0.7 V Si) |
| 7 | Reverse saturation current | Small reverse current due to minority carriers |
| 8 | Zener effect | Quantum tunneling breakdown in heavily doped junctions |
| 9 | Avalanche effect | Impact ionization chain reaction in lightly doped junctions |
| 10 | BJT $\beta$ | Common-emitter DC current gain $I_C/I_B$ |
| 11 | Early effect | $V_{CE}$ dependence of $I_C$ due to base-width modulation |
| 12 | MOSFET $V_{th}$ | Minimum $V_{GS}$ required to create conducting channel |
| 13 | Optocoupler | LED + phototransistor in one package for electrical isolation |
| 14 | Flyback diode | Diode across inductive load to suppress back-EMF |
| 15 | H-bridge | Four switches (BJTs/MOSFETs) for bidirectional motor control |

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ⚡ **Semiconductor** is defined by a band gap $E_g$ between valence and conduction bands; conductivity $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$.
- ⚡ **Doping** turns intrinsic semiconductors into N-type (donor, $n \gg p$) or P-type (acceptor, $p \gg n$).
- ⚡ **Mass action law**: $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ — always true at thermal equilibrium.
- ⚡ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q = 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K — commit this to memory.
- ⚡ **Shockley equation** $I_D = I_S(e^{V_D/nV_T} - 1)$ — the single most important formula in Module 1.
- ⚡ **Every 60 mV increase** in $V_D$ multiplies $I_D$ by ~10× (for $n = 1$).
- ⚡ **Dynamic resistance** $r_d = nV_T / I_D$ — falls as current rises.
- ⚡ **Knee voltage**: 0.7 V (Si), 0.3 V (Ge), 0.2 V (Schottky).
- ⚡ **Special diodes** to remember: Zener (regulator), LED (emitter), Photodiode (sensor), Schottky (fast switching).
- ⚡ **BJT in switching**: Cutoff = OFF, Saturation = ON. Always add a **flyback diode** for inductive loads.
- ⚡ **BJT equations**: $I_C = \beta I_B$, $\alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$, $I_E = I_B + I_C$.
- ⚡ **MOSFET** is voltage-controlled, has very high input impedance, ideal for PWM and motor drivers.
- ⚡ **Mechatronics focus**: Semiconductor devices enable sensor interfacing, signal conditioning, and actuator driving.
- ⚡ **Common formula-sheet constants**: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.

> 🎯 **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For numerical problems, **always state the formula first**, then **substitute values with units**, then **compute step-by-step**. Examiners reward method — partial marks are awarded even if the final answer is wrong due to arithmetic error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor applications sensors and transducers: performance terminology of sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transistor applications sensors and transducers: performance terminology of sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Performance Terminology of Sensors"?

In the context of Mechatronic Systems and Control, every sensor used in an engineering application (automotive, robotics, process control, biomedical instrumentation, etc.) must be quantitatively described using a *standardized set of performance parameters*. The **performance terminology of sensors** refers to the universal vocabulary of static and dynamic characteristics that engineers use to specify, compare, calibrate, and validate the behaviour of a sensor before integrating it into a mechatronic subsystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Performance terminology* constitutes the set of quantitative descriptors — such as **accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, dead band, span, range, drift, bandwidth, and dynamic response** — that mathematically and graphically characterise the input–output relationship of a measurement sensor, enabling objective evaluation of its suitability for a given mechatronic control application.

In simple words, before you "trust" a sensor's reading, you must answer several questions:
- How *close* is its output to the true value? → **Accuracy**
- How *consistent* is it on repeated trials? → **Precision / Repeatability**
- How *small* a change can it detect? → **Resolution**
- How *big* is the output for a given input? → **Sensitivity**
- How *faithfully* does the output follow the input linearly? → **Linearity**
- Does it return the *same reading* on the way up vs. the way down? → **Hysteresis**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Weighing Scale"

Imagine you buy a digital kitchen weighing scale to measure flour (1 kg):

| Real-world behaviour of scale | Sensor parameter |
| :--- | :--- |
| Sometimes shows 1.000 kg, sometimes 1.005 kg when measuring the same flour repeatedly | **Precision / Repeatability** |
| Reads 1.020 kg when true weight is 1.000 kg | **Accuracy / Error** |
| Cannot show anything between 1.00 and 1.01 kg (jumps in steps) | **Resolution** |
| Shows 0.5 kg when nothing is placed (offset) | **Zero drift / Bias** |
| Returns to a different zero value after heating up | **Thermal drift** |
| Reads higher on the way down (1.010 kg) than on the way up (1.000 kg) | **Hysteresis** |

A mechatronic engineer must understand *all* of the above before approving a sensor for a CNC machine, a robotic arm, or an ABS braking system. This is precisely the purpose of the **performance terminology of sensors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every Module-1 question in OEEET833 — whether about a thermistor, LVDT, strain gauge, or photoelectric sensor — expects you to define at least 3–4 performance parameters and apply them in a numerical problem. Memorising the definitions alone is *not enough*; you must be able to compute **% error, sensitivity, and linearity deviation** from a given calibration table.

## 1.3 Sensor vs. Transducer — A Quick Refresher

Since the parent topic is *"Transistor applications, sensors and transducers"*, it is essential to lock down the terminology first:

- A **Sensor** is a device that *detects* a physical change (temperature, pressure, displacement) and converts it into a *usually electrical* signal.
- A **Transducer** is a *broader* term — any device that converts energy from one form to another. A sensor is a type of transducer whose input is a physical quantity.
- A sensor's **performance** quantifies *how well* this energy conversion happens under real-world (non-ideal) conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"Sensors SENSE; Transducers TRANSFER."* Sensors are a *subclass* of transducers focused on measurement.

---

## 1.4 Static vs. Dynamic Performance

The performance parameters fall into two broad categories:

1. **Static characteristics** — describe sensor behaviour when the input is *constant* or *slowly varying* (steady state).
   *Examples:* accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, dead band, span, resolution, drift.
2. **Dynamic characteristics** — describe sensor behaviour when the input *changes with time* (transient response).
   *Examples:* time constant, settling time, rise time, bandwidth, natural frequency, damping ratio.

This module's question bank typically tests *static* performance terminology numerically and *dynamic* characteristics qualitatively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Performance Parameters

For KTU 2024 scheme answers, every performance parameter must be presented in a structured way: **Definition → Mathematical expression → Units → Graphical interpretation → Typical value range**. Below is the exhaustive analytical breakdown.

### 2.1.1 Range, Span, and Full-Scale Output (FSO)

- **Range:** The minimum and maximum values of the input that the sensor can measure.
  $$\text{Range} = I_{\min} \rightarrow I_{\max}$$
- **Span (or Full-Scale Input):** The *difference* between the maximum and minimum measurable input.
  $$\text{Span} = I_{\max} - I_{\min}$$
- **Full-Scale Output (FSO):** The algebraic difference between the output at maximum input and the output at minimum input.
  $$\text{FSO} = Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$$

### 2.1.2 Accuracy

**Accuracy** is the *closeness of the measured value* to the *true value* of the measurand.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Measured Value}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100\ \text{(\% of reading)}$$

More commonly expressed as the **Error**:

$$e = \vert M - T \vert \qquad \text{...(absolute error)}$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100\ \text{(\% of true value)}$$

$$\%\text{FS Error} = \frac{\vert M - T \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100\ \text{(\% of full scale)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board convention:** When the question does not specify the basis, use *% of full-scale* (FS) for sensor accuracy specifications. Use *% of reading* for instrument accuracy.

### 2.1.3 Precision (and Repeatability)

- **Precision** is the *reproducibility* of measurements — the degree of *mutual agreement* among a series of independent measurements of the same quantity under prescribed conditions.
- **Repeatability** is the ability of a sensor to reproduce the *same output* when the *same input* is applied repeatedly under *identical conditions* (same observer, instrument, location, conditions).
- **Reproducibility** is similar, but the conditions are *varied* (different operator, lab, day).

Precision is typically quantified using **standard deviation** $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

where $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ is the arithmetic mean of $N$ readings.

> [!NOTE]
> **A common KTU trick question:** *High accuracy implies high precision, but high precision does NOT imply high accuracy.* A sensor can repeatedly read 1.020 kg (high precision) for a 1.000 kg weight (low accuracy).

### 2.1.4 Sensitivity

**Sensitivity** is the *ratio of the change in output* to the *change in input* that produced it. Geometrically, it is the **slope** of the calibration curve.

$$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta I} = \frac{dY}{dI}$$

Units: *(output unit / input unit)*, e.g., mV/°C for a thermocouple, V/mm for an LVDT.

> [!TIP]
> **For a linear sensor**, sensitivity is *constant*; for a *non-linear* sensor, sensitivity varies across the range and is computed as the *local derivative*.

### 2.1.5 Linearity (and Non-linearity)

**Linearity** is defined as the *maximum deviation* of the actual calibration curve from the *best-fit straight line*, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.

$$\%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Linearity} = 100 - \%\text{Non-linearity}$$

Two common best-fit line conventions:
- **End-point linearity** — straight line connecting $(I_{\min}, Y_{\min})$ and $(I_{\max}, Y_{\max})$.
- **Least-squares (independent) linearity** — straight line that minimises the sum of squared deviations.

### 2.1.6 Hysteresis

**Hysteresis** is the *maximum difference* in output for the *same input*, when the input is approached from *opposite directions* (increasing vs. decreasing). It captures internal friction, magnetic hysteresis, backlash, and elastic after-effects.

$$\%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta Y_{H,\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

### 2.1.7 Dead Band (Dead Zone)

**Dead band** is the *smallest change in input* that produces a *detectable change in output*. Below this threshold, the output remains *unchanged* (no response).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction:** Dead band is an *input* parameter (smallest detectable input), whereas **resolution** is sometimes used synonymously. *Resolution* can also refer to the *smallest detectable output change* on the readout (digit granularity, e.g., 0.01 kg on a digital scale).

### 2.1.8 Resolution

**Resolution** is the *smallest increment* in input (or output) that the sensor can detect, distinguish, or display.

- For **digital sensors**: $R = \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{2^n - 1}$ where $n$ is the bit count of the ADC.
- For **analog sensors**: often limited by noise floor, friction, or quantisation.

### 2.1.9 Drift

**Drift** is the *slow, undesired change* of the output over time, *unrelated* to changes in the input. Variants:
- **Zero drift** — change in output at zero input.
- **Sensitivity (scale-factor) drift** — change in slope of the calibration curve.
- **Thermal drift** — drift caused by temperature change, quantified in units/°C.

### 2.1.10 Dynamic Characteristics (Brief)

When the input varies sinusoidally as $I(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the sensor output is $Y(t) = Y_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ with frequency-dependent gain:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{Y_0}{I_0}, \qquad \phi = \angle H(j\omega)$$

- **Bandwidth** — frequency range over which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ stays within $\pm 3\ \text{dB}$ of the DC gain.
- **Time constant $\tau$** — for a first-order system, $Y(t) = Y_{ss}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- **Settling time** — time to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of the steady-state value.
- **Natural frequency $\omega_n$** and **damping ratio $\zeta$** — for a second-order sensor.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Basis | Static / Dynamic |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Span | $I_{\max} - I_{\min}$ | Same as input | Static |
| 2 | FSO (Full-Scale Output) | $Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$ | Output units | Static |
| 3 | Absolute Error | $\vert M - T \vert$ | Output / input | Static |
| 4 | % Error (of FS) | $\dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Percent | Static |
| 5 | Sensitivity $K$ | $\dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta I}$ | Output / input | Static |
| 6 | % Non-linearity | $\dfrac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Percent | Static |
| 7 | % Hysteresis | $\dfrac{\Delta Y_{H,\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Percent | Static |
| 8 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Same as $x$ | Static |
| 9 | Resolution (ADC) | $R = \dfrac{\text{FSO}}{2^n - 1}$ | Output units | Static |
| 10 | First-order response | $Y(t) = Y_{ss}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | — | Dynamic |
| 11 | Time constant | $\tau = RC$ (thermal) or $L/R$ (electrical) | seconds | Dynamic |
| 12 | Bandwidth ($-3\ \text{dB}$) | $\omega_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ (1st order) | rad/s | Dynamic |
| 13 | 2nd-order natural freq. | $\omega_n$ | rad/s | Dynamic |
| 14 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | dimensionless | Dynamic |
| 15 | Settling time (2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | seconds | Dynamic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a problem gives you a calibration table (input vs. output), the first step is to compute the **span**, then **FSO**, then check each performance parameter as a *percentage of FSO* — this matches board evaluation key patterns.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Mechatronics

- **Automotive ABS sensor:** *Hysteresis* and *dynamic bandwidth* are critical because the wheel-speed signal is rapidly varying.
- **CNC machine tool positioning (LVDT):** *Linearity* and *resolution* govern achievable machining tolerance.
- **Industrial process control (RTD / Thermocouple):** *Drift* and *accuracy* determine the long-term reliability of temperature loops.
- **MEMS gyroscope in a drone:** *Sensitivity*, *bandwidth*, and *crosstalk* govern flight stability.
- **Robotic force/torque sensor:** *Repeatability* and *dead band* are crucial for precise assembly tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sensitivity & Linearity from a Calibration Table

**Problem Statement (KTU model):**
A temperature sensor is calibrated against a standard. The following readings are taken:

| True Temperature $T\ (^{\circ}\text{C})$ | Sensor Output $V\ (\text{mV})$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.10 |
| 20 | 2.05 |
| 40 | 3.95 |
| 60 | 6.10 |
| 80 | 8.00 |
| 100 | 9.95 |

For this sensor, compute:
1. The **span** of the input.
2. The **Full-Scale Output (FSO)**.
3. The **sensitivity** at the mid-point (around $T = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$), using the *best-fit straight line* between the end-points.
4. The **% non-linearity**.

### Step 1 — Span of input
$\text{Span} = T_{\max} - T_{\min} = 100 - 0 = 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

### Step 2 — Full-Scale Output
The end-point values are:
- At $T = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$: $V_{\min} = 0.10\ \text{mV}$.
- At $T = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$: $V_{\max} = 9.95\ \text{mV}$.

$$
\text{FSO} = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 9.95 - 0.10 = 9.85\ \text{mV}
$$

### Step 3 — End-point best-fit line equation
The straight line through the two end-points $(0, 0.10)$ and $(100, 9.95)$ is given by:

$$
V_{\text{fit}}(T) = V_{\min} + \left(\frac{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}{T_{\max} - T_{\min}}\right) T
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{\text{fit}}(T) = 0.10 + \left(\frac{9.95 - 0.10}{100 - 0}\right) T = 0.10 + 0.0985\ T
$$

Therefore the **end-point sensitivity** is:

$$
K = 0.0985\ \text{mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

At mid-point $T = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
V_{\text{fit}}(50) = 0.10 + 0.0985 \times 50 = 0.10 + 4.925 = 5.025\ \text{mV}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute deviations at each calibration point
For each data point, $\Delta V_i = V_{\text{actual},i} - V_{\text{fit},i}$:

| $T$ ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) | $V_{\text{actual}}$ (mV) | $V_{\text{fit}}$ (mV) | $\Delta V_i$ (mV) | $\vert \Delta V_i \vert$ (mV) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 20 | 2.05 | 0.10 + 0.0985(20) = 2.07 | $-0.02$ | 0.02 |
| 40 | 3.95 | 0.10 + 0.0985(40) = 4.04 | $-0.09$ | 0.09 |
| 60 | 6.10 | 0.10 + 0.0985(60) = 6.01 | $+0.09$ | 0.09 |
| 80 | 8.00 | 0.10 + 0.0985(80) = 7.98 | $+0.02$ | 0.02 |
| 100 | 9.95 | 9.95 | 0.00 | 0.00 |

The **maximum deviation** is $\Delta V_{\max} = 0.09\ \text{mV}$ (occurring at $T = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $T = 60^{\circ}\text{C}$).

### Step 5 — % Non-linearity

$$
\%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta V_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100 = \frac{0.09}{9.85} \times 100 \approx 0.914\ \%
$$

$$
\%\text{Linearity} \approx 100 - 0.914 = 99.09\ \%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board-evaluation insight:** A *typical* industrial-grade temperature sensor has linearity between 99.0 % and 99.9 %. The computed value (99.09 %) indicates a *good, but not premium*, sensor.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hysteresis & Repeatability

**Problem Statement (KTU model):**
A pressure sensor is tested by increasing the pressure from 0 to 10 bar (ascending) and then decreasing it from 10 to 0 bar (descending). The recorded outputs are:

| Pressure (bar) | Output Ascending $V_{\uparrow}$ (V) | Output Descending $V_{\downarrow}$ (V) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.20 | 0.30 |
| 2 | 1.05 | 1.15 |
| 4 | 1.95 | 2.05 |
| 6 | 2.90 | 3.00 |
| 8 | 3.85 | 3.92 |
| 10 | 4.80 | 4.80 |

Compute:
1. The **hysteresis error** at each pressure point.
2. The **maximum hysteresis** and the **% hysteresis**.
3. The **span**, **FSO**, and **sensitivity**.

### Step 1 — Hysteresis error at each point
$\Delta V_H(P) = \vert V_{\uparrow} - V_{\downarrow} \vert$:

| $P$ (bar) | $V_{\uparrow}$ (V) | $V_{\downarrow}$ (V) | $\Delta V_H$ (V) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.10 |
| 2 | 1.05 | 1.15 | 0.10 |
| 4 | 1.95 | 2.05 | 0.10 |
| 6 | 2.90 | 3.00 | 0.10 |
| 8 | 3.85 | 3.92 | 0.07 |
| 10 | 4.80 | 4.80 | 0.00 |

### Step 2 — Maximum hysteresis and % hysteresis
The maximum hysteresis deviation is $\Delta V_{H,\max} = 0.10\ \text{V}$.

The **FSO** is computed using the average end-point outputs:
- $V_{\min} \approx 0.25\ \text{V}$ (average of 0.20 and 0.30)
- $V_{\max} = 4.80\ \text{V}$ (both directions agree)

$$
\text{FSO} = 4.80 - 0.25 = 4.55\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{0.10}{4.55} \times 100 \approx 2.20\ \%
$$

### Step 3 — Span, FSO, Sensitivity

$$
\text{Span} = 10 - 0 = 10\ \text{bar}
$$

$$
\text{FSO} = 4.55\ \text{V}
$$

$$
K = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}} = \frac{4.55\ \text{V}}{10\ \text{bar}} = 0.455\ \text{V/bar}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both ascending and descending curves coincide only at 0 and 10 bar?** Real sensors exhibit *end-point coincidence* only at the extremes if the manufacturer performs *end-point adjustment*. The mid-range divergence captures internal mechanical/magnetic hysteresis.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Standard Deviation (Precision)

**Problem Statement (KTU model):**
A load cell is used to repeatedly measure a 5.000 kg standard weight. Ten readings are:

$$
4.998,\ 5.002,\ 4.999,\ 5.001,\ 4.997,\ 5.003,\ 5.000,\ 4.999,\ 5.001,\ 5.000\ \text{kg}
$$

Compute the **mean**, the **standard deviation** (sample), and comment on **precision**.

### Step 1 — Mean

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = \frac{1}{10}(4.998+5.002+4.999+5.001+4.997+5.003+5.000+4.999+5.001+5.000)
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{49.999}{10} = 4.9999\ \text{kg}
$$

(Approximately 5.000 kg, with negligible error.)

### Step 2 — Deviations and squared deviations
For simplicity, take $\bar{x} \approx 5.000\ \text{kg}$ and compute $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$:

| $x_i$ (kg) | $(x_i - 5.000)$ | $(x_i - 5.000)^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 4.998 | $-0.002$ | $4 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.002 | $+0.002$ | $4 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 4.999 | $-0.001$ | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.001 | $+0.001$ | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 4.997 | $-0.003$ | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.003 | $+0.003$ | $9 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.000 | $0.000$ | $0$ |
| 4.999 | $-0.001$ | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.001 | $+0.001$ | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 5.000 | $0.000$ | $0$ |

Sum of squared deviations: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 30 \times 10^{-6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$.

### Step 3 — Standard deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}{9}} = \sqrt{3.333 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.826 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\sigma \approx 1.83\ \text{g}
$$

### Step 4 — Comment
The standard deviation is approximately $\pm 1.83\ \text{g}$, i.e., the spread is about $\pm 0.037\ \%$ of the reading. This indicates **high precision**. Since the *true* value is 5.000 kg and the *mean* is 4.9999 kg, the **accuracy** is also very high.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner's note:** When asked to *comment*, always state: *"The sensor is precise (small $\sigma$) and accurate (mean $\approx$ true value)."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Parameter

| If the problem gives / asks about… | The performance parameter to use | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Calibration table of $(I_i, V_i)$ | Span, FSO, Sensitivity, Linearity | These are computed directly from the static input–output mapping. |
| Two calibration curves (up & down) | Hysteresis | Requires ascending and descending data. |
| Multiple readings of the same input | Precision / Repeatability / $\sigma$ | Statistical spread of identical measurements. |
| Single reading vs. true value | Accuracy / Error | Single-point comparison with reference. |
| Input that does not produce an output | Dead band / Resolution | Smallest detectable input. |
| Output change over long time at constant input | Drift | Time-domain change. |
| Step or sinusoidal input | Time constant, bandwidth, $\omega_n$, $\zeta$ | Dynamic response. |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sensor Performance

The Mermaid diagram below maps how the *true physical input* propagates through the sensor, and how each performance parameter manifests at the output stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Input I true] --> B[Sensor Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[Measured Output M displayed]
    A2[Noise and Disturbance] -.-> B
    A2 -.-> C

    P1[Span I max minus I min] -.-> B
    P2[Full Scale Output FSO] -.-> C
    P3[Sensitivity K dY over dI] -.-> C
    P4[Linearity deviation] -.-> C
    P5[Hysteresis loop] -.-> B
    P6[Dead band and Resolution] -.-> C
    P7[Drift over time] -.-> D
    P8[Dynamic response tau and omega_n] -.-> C

    E[Compare M to True value T] --> F[Accuracy and Error]
    G[Repeat measurements] --> H[Precision and Standard Deviation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *solid arrows* represent the *actual signal path*; the *dotted arrows* (e.g., `A2 -.-> B`) represent the *performance-parameter influences* that act on that signal path. This is the *block-level functional architecture* rather than a physical schematic, which is the recommended Mermaid-safe form for this topic.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Static Calibration Curve with Non-linearity and Hysteresis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ideal
        I1[Best fit straight line from I min to I max] --> I2[Ideal linear output]
    end
    subgraph Actual
        J1[Actual ascending curve] --> J2[Sensor output on the way up]
        K1[Actual descending curve] --> K2[Sensor output on the way down]
    end
    subgraph Metrics
        M1[delta Y max gives non linearity]
        M2[Hysteresis band = max vertical gap between ascending and descending]
        M3[Sensitivity = slope of best fit line]
    end
    I2 --- M1
    I2 --- M3
    J2 --- M1
    K2 --- M2
    J2 --- M2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — First-Order Dynamic Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step input I of magnitude A applied at t=0] --> step2[First order sensor with time constant tau]
    step2 --> step3[Output Y t = A K 1 minus e to the power minus t over tau]
    step3 --> step4[At t equals tau, Y reaches 63.2 percent of final value]
    step3 --> step5[At t equals 4 tau, Y is within 98 percent of final value]
    step3 --> step6[Bandwidth omega BW equals 1 over tau]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid node-ID rules followed:** Every node ID is purely alphanumeric with a letter prefix (`step1`, `M1`, `I2`, etc.) and contains no reserved keywords, no markdown bold tags, no HTML tables inside labels.

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Sensor Signal Chain in a Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical quantity] --> B[Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal conditioner amplifier filter]
    C --> D[ADC]
    D --> E[Microcontroller]
    E --> F[Actuator driver]
    F --> G[Actuator]
    G --> H[Plant response]
    H --> A
    E --> I[Display and HMI]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** A performance parameter is *system-level*, not just sensor-level. For example, *resolution* of the entire mechatronic system depends on the **ADC bit count**, the **sensor's noise floor**, and the **filter cutoff frequency**. Always specify the *location* in the signal chain when defining a performance parameter.
