# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the following performance characteristics of a sensor. *(3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) Sensitivity (1 mark):**
The ratio of the change in sensor output to the change in the input that produced it, expressed as the *slope of the calibration curve*.
$$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta I}$$

**(b) Hysteresis (1 mark):**
The maximum difference in output for the *same input* when the input is approached from *opposite directions* (increasing vs. decreasing), expressed as a percentage of full-scale output.

**(c) Dead band (1 mark):**
The *smallest change in input* that produces a *detectable change in output*; below this threshold, the sensor does not respond at all.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between **accuracy** and **precision** of a sensor. State which is quantified by *standard deviation*. *(3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Closeness of the *mean measured value* to the *true value* | Closeness of *repeated measurements* to each other |
| Affected by | Calibration error, bias, offset, linearity | Random noise, resolution, repeatability |
| Quantified by | % Error $= \frac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100$ | Standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ |
| Graphical analogy | Dart near bull's-eye (but spread out) | Darts clustered (but away from centre) |

> Standard deviation quantifies *precision*, **not** accuracy. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### (a) Define the following performance parameters of a sensor and state the corresponding mathematical expression: **span, full-scale output, sensitivity, % non-linearity, and % hysteresis.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Span (1 mark):** The difference between the maximum and minimum values of the input that the sensor is designed to measure.
   $$\text{Span} = I_{\max} - I_{\min}$$

2. **Full-Scale Output (FSO) (1 mark):** The algebraic difference between the output at $I_{\max}$ and the output at $I_{\min}$.
   $$\text{FSO} = Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$$

3. **Sensitivity $K$ (2 marks):** The ratio of a small change in output to the corresponding small change in input; geometrically, the slope of the calibration curve.
   $$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta I} = \frac{dY}{dI}$$

4. **% Non-linearity (1.5 marks):** The maximum deviation of the actual calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of FSO.
   $$\%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

5. **% Hysteresis (1.5 marks):** The maximum difference in output for the same input approached from increasing and decreasing directions, expressed as a percentage of FSO.
   $$\%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta V_{H,\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

#### (b) A thermistor is calibrated against a standard thermometer. The data are:

| Standard $T$ ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Thermistor $R$ ($\text{k}\Omega$) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 20 | 13.50 |
| 40 | 8.40 |
| 60 | 5.20 |
| 80 | 3.30 |
| 100 | 2.10 |

**Compute:** (i) span, (ii) FSO, (iii) end-point sensitivity at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, and (iv) % non-linearity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Span (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Span} = 100 - 20 = 80\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**(ii) FSO (1 mark):**
$$
\text{FSO} = 2.10 - 13.50 = -11.40\ \text{k}\Omega
$$
Magnitude of FSO = $11.40\ \text{k}\Omega$ (negative indicates inverse characteristic). (1 mark)

**(iii) End-point best-fit line (3 marks):**
The end-point line through $(20, 13.50)$ and $(100, 2.10)$ has slope:

$$
K = \frac{2.10 - 13.50}{100 - 20} = \frac{-11.40}{80} = -0.1425\ \text{k}\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

The intercept is:
$$
b = 13.50 - (-0.1425)(20) = 13.50 + 2.85 = 16.35\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Best-fit line: $R_{\text{fit}}(T) = 16.35 - 0.1425\ T$.

At $T = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$:
$$
R_{\text{fit}}(50) = 16.35 - 0.1425 \times 50 = 16.35 - 7.125 = 9.225\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**(iv) % Non-linearity (2 marks):**
Compute deviations $\Delta R_i = R_{\text{actual},i} - R_{\text{fit},i}$:

| $T$ ($^{\circ}\text{C}$) | $R_{\text{actual}}$ (kΩ) | $R_{\text{fit}}$ (kΩ) | $\Delta R$ (kΩ) | $\vert \Delta R \vert$ (kΩ) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 20 | 13.50 | $16.35 - 0.1425(20) = 13.50$ | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 40 | 8.40 | $16.35 - 0.1425(40) = 10.65$ | $-2.25$ | 2.25 |
| 60 | 5.20 | $16.35 - 0.1425(60) = 7.80$ | $-2.60$ | 2.60 |
| 80 | 3.30 | $16.35 - 0.1425(80) = 4.95$ | $-1.65$ | 1.65 |
| 100 | 2.10 | $16.35 - 0.1425(100) = 2.10$ | 0.00 | 0.00 |

Maximum deviation: $\Delta R_{\max} = 2.60\ \text{k}\Omega$.

$$
\%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{2.60}{11.40} \times 100 \approx 22.81\ \%
$$

$$
\%\text{Linearity} \approx 77.19\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The thermistor is *strongly non-linear* (an expected behaviour of NTC thermistors). In practice, a *polynomial fit* or *Steinhart–Hart equation* is used to linearise the response.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) With neat sketches, distinguish between **systematic error** and **random error**. Define the following performance parameters in terms of these errors: **accuracy, precision, bias, and repeatability.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Systematic error (1 mark):** A *deterministic*, repeatable error that consistently shifts the measured value away from the true value. Examples: miscalibration, zero offset, drift, loading error, environmental bias. It *reduces accuracy* but not precision.

**Random error (1 mark):** A *stochastic*, non-repeatable error arising from noise, electrical interference, vibration, human operator variation, or quantisation. It *reduces precision* but, in the limit of a large sample, the *mean* remains close to the true value.

**Sketch (2 marks):** The classic *target diagram* —
- Systematic error: dart cluster displaced in *one* direction from the bull's-eye.
- Random error: dart cluster *spread* around the centre.
- Combined systematic + random: dart cluster *spread* and *displaced*.

**Definitions in terms of errors (3 marks):**

1. **Accuracy** — quantified using the *systematic error*; $e_{\text{sys}} = \bar{x} - T$.
2. **Precision** — quantified using the *random error*; expressed as $\sigma$ (standard deviation) of repeated measurements.
3. **Bias** — the *systematic* deviation of the *mean* of measurements from the true value; mathematically identical to systematic error.
4. **Repeatability** — the *closeness of agreement* of successive measurements under *identical* conditions; quantified by the *spread* (range or standard deviation) of the readings.

#### (b) A load cell is subjected to a 500 N standard load ten times. The readings in N are:

$$
500.5,\ 501.0,\ 499.5,\ 500.0,\ 499.0,\ 500.5,\ 499.0,\ 500.0,\ 501.0,\ 500.5
$$

**Compute the mean, the standard deviation, the bias, the % error of full scale (assuming FSO = 1000 N), and comment on the precision of the sensor.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mean (1 mark):**
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{500.5+501.0+499.5+500.0+499.0+500.5+499.0+500.0+501.0+500.5}{10} = \frac{5001.0}{10} = 500.1\ \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Standard deviation (2.5 marks):**
Deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and their squares:

| $x_i$ (N) | $x_i - 500.1$ | $(x_i - 500.1)^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 500.5 | $+0.4$ | 0.16 |
| 501.0 | $+0.9$ | 0.81 |
| 499.5 | $-0.6$ | 0.36 |
| 500.0 | $-0.1$ | 0.01 |
| 499.0 | $-1.1$ | 1.21 |
| 500.5 | $+0.4$ | 0.16 |
| 499.0 | $-1.1$ | 1.21 |
| 500.0 | $-0.1$ | 0.01 |
| 501.0 | $+0.9$ | 0.81 |
| 500.5 | $+0.4$ | 0.16 |

Sum $= 4.90$.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{4.90}{10-1}} = \sqrt{0.5444} \approx 0.738\ \text{N}
$$

**(iii) Bias (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Bias} = \bar{x} - T = 500.1 - 500.0 = +0.1\ \text{N}
$$

**(iv) % Error of full scale (1 mark):**
$$
\%\text{Error (FS)} = \frac{\vert \bar{x} - T \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100 = \frac{0.1}{1000} \times 100 = 0.01\ \%
$$

**(v) Comment on precision (1.5 marks):**
The standard deviation is $\sigma \approx 0.738\ \text{N}$, which is about $\pm 0.148\ \%$ of the reading. This represents *good precision* for a 500 N load cell. The bias is small (0.1 N) and the % FS error is 0.01 %, indicating *high accuracy* as well. The sensor is therefore both *accurate* and *precise*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks in KTU board evaluations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to express error as a % of FSO.** Many students compute absolute error and stop. The KTU key *always* expects a percentage basis — usually "% of full scale" for sensor specifications.
> 2. **Confusing precision and accuracy.** Examiners explicitly test this with a target-diagram question. *Precision* is *spread*; *accuracy* is *closeness to the centre*.
> 3. **Wrong formula for sample standard deviation.** Use $N-1$ (Bessel's correction) in the denominator, *not* $N$. Many students lose a mark for using $N$.
> 4. **Confusing *resolution* with *sensitivity*.** *Resolution* is the *smallest detectable increment* (lower bound on input), while *sensitivity* is the *gain* (output per unit input).
> 5. **Forgetting the negative sign in % non-linearity for inverse sensors** (NTC thermistor, negative-slope LVDT region). Take the *magnitude* of the maximum deviation in such cases.
> 6. **Mismatching the basis of "full scale."** FSO refers to *output* span, while span refers to *input* span. Do not interchange them in the denominator of a % formula.
> 7. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always state the units of sensitivity (e.g., mV/°C, V/bar, kΩ/°C). Loss of 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 8. **Forgetting that "high precision ≠ high accuracy."** A sensor with $\sigma = 0$ can still be inaccurate (constant bias).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Span** = $I_{\max} - I_{\min}$; **FSO** = $Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$ — *always* state units.
- **Sensitivity** $K = \Delta Y / \Delta I$ — slope of calibration curve; units are *output / input*.
- **Accuracy** is closeness to *true value*; **Precision** is closeness *among* repeated measurements.
- **Linearity** is computed using a *best-fit straight line* — end-point or least-squares; *always* report as % of FSO.
- **Hysteresis** requires *both* ascending and descending data; it is the *maximum vertical gap* between the two curves, expressed as % of FSO.
- **Dead band / Resolution** — the *smallest input* the sensor can detect; for digital sensors, $R = \text{FSO}/(2^n - 1)$.
- **Drift** is *time-dependent* change in output at constant input; differentiate zero-drift, sensitivity-drift, and thermal-drift.
- **Dynamic parameters:** $\tau$ (time constant), $\omega_n$ (natural frequency), $\zeta$ (damping ratio), bandwidth = $1/\tau$ (first-order).
- **Standard deviation** formula: use $N-1$ in the denominator for sample standard deviation.
- **% Error (FS)** = $\frac{\vert M - T \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ is the *default* KTU convention.
- **High precision does NOT imply high accuracy** — a classic viva and 2-mark question.
- The **performance of a sensor is system-dependent** — resolution depends on ADC, noise depends on environment, drift depends on temperature.
- **Static characteristics** describe *constant* inputs; **dynamic characteristics** describe *time-varying* inputs — keep this distinction clear in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Displacement (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Displacement** is a *primary measurand* in mechatronic systems, defined as the *change in position* of a body or point with respect to a fixed reference frame, measured along a straight line (linear displacement) or along an arc (angular/rotary displacement). The SI unit of displacement is the **metre (m)** for linear motion and the **radian (rad)** for angular motion.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET833)**, displacement is treated as one of the foundational *non-electrical physical quantities* that must be transduced into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or phase shift) for use in feedback control loops, CNC machines, robotic arms, and automation systems.

**Classification of Displacement (As per KTU Module 1):**

| Class | Type | Typical Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Translational | $\mu m$ to $m$ | Position control, LVDTs |
| **Angular** | Rotary | deg or rad | Servo motors, encoders |
| **Absolute** | From zero reference | $0$ to full scale | Machine tool slides |
| **Incremental** | From last position | $\Delta x$ step size | CNC, robotics |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Garage Parking Sensor":**
> Imagine you are slowly reversing your car into a garage. A small device on the wall measures how far the bumper is from the wall and beeps faster as the gap shrinks. That gap — the *change in distance* between the car and the wall — **is displacement**. The sensor inside is doing exactly what every mechatronic displacement transducer does: it converts a *mechanical quantity* (a distance) into an *electrical quantity* (a voltage, frequency, or current) that the controller can read.

- **Linear displacement** = how far a robotic gripper has moved along a slide.
- **Angular displacement** = how much a motor shaft has rotated (e.g., $30^{\circ}$ from home).

### 1.3 Why Displacement Measurement is Central to Mechatronics

A mechatronic system is the **synergistic integration** of mechanical, electronic, control, and computer engineering. Without an accurate knowledge of *where* an actuator is, *how far* it has travelled, and *how close* it is to its target, no closed-loop control is possible. **Position feedback = displacement measurement**.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference benchmarks in modern industry: measurement resolution of **0.1 µm** in CNC machines, accuracy of **±1 µm** in coordinate measuring machines (CMMs), and bandwidth of up to **20 kHz** in high-speed servo systems.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit (linear):** metre (m); sub-units mm, µm, nm.
- **SI Unit (angular):** radian (rad); also degree ($^{\circ}$) where $1\ rad = 57.2958^{\circ}$.
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ m/s$ (used in optical displacement sensors).
- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81\ m/s^{2}$ (for inertial-type sensors).
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m$ (used in capacitive sensors).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Displacement Transducers

Displacement sensors are classified by the **physical principle** of transduction. The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus typically expects students to be conversant with the following families:

**(A) Resistive / Potentiometric**
- A uniform resistance element with a sliding wiper. Movement of the wiper changes the tapped resistance fraction.

**(B) Inductive**
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** — the most frequently examined sensor in KTU papers.
- **Eddy-current probes** — for non-contact measurement.
- **Variable reluctance transducers** — based on air-gap changes.

**(C) Capacitive**
- Two parallel plates; displacement changes the gap or overlap area, altering capacitance.

**(D) Piezoelectric / Piezoresistive**
- Strain-induced charge generation — useful for *dynamic* displacement only.

**(E) Optical**
- **Laser interferometers**, **optical encoders** (absolute and incremental), **fiber-optic sensors**.

**(F) Ultrasonic and Magnetic**
- Time-of-flight, Hall-effect, magnetostrictive sensors.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Mechanical Coupling:** The target whose displacement is to be measured physically engages the sensor (through a probe tip, plunger, or non-contact gap).

**Step 2 — Primary Sensing Element Converts Geometry to an Electrical Parameter:** A geometric change (gap, area, length, angle) modulates $R$, $L$, $C$, phase, or frequency.

**Step 3 — Signal Conditioning:** The raw signal is amplified, demodulated, linearised, and converted to a standard output (e.g., $0$ to $10\ V$ or $4$ to $20\ mA$).

**Step 4 — Controller Interface:** The conditioned signal is fed to the ADC of a microcontroller/PLC, where it becomes a digital word.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 numerical and short-answer question.

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Output Quantity | Linearity Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Voltage | Linear (ideal) |
| **LVDT (r.m.s. output)** | $V_{o} = K_{L} \cdot x$ (small $x$) | A.C. Voltage | Linear in mid-range |
| **LVDT (general)** | $V_{o} = V_{p} \cdot \dfrac{N_{s2}-N_{s1}}{N_{p}} \propto x$ | Differential Voltage | $\pm$5 mm typical |
| **Capacitive (gap)** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d}$ | Capacitance | Non-linear (1/d) |
| **Capacitive (differential)** | $\Delta C = 2 C_{0} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_{0}}$ | $\Delta$ Capacitance | Linear (small $\Delta d$) |
| **Piezoelectric** | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot x$ | Charge | Dynamic only |
| **Strain-Gauge (linear)** | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = G \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Resistance change | Linear |
| **Optical Encoder (incremental)** | $\theta = \dfrac{p}{N} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{pulses}}$ | Pulses | Quantised |
| **Magnetic (Hall)** | $V_{H} = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n_{e} \cdot e \cdot t}$ | Hall Voltage | Linear in B |

> **NOTATION:** $x$ = displacement, $L$ = full stroke, $K_{L}$ = LVDT sensitivity (V/mm), $V_{p}$ = primary excitation, $G$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain, $N_{pulses}$ = pulses per revolution, $p$ = pulse count.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining:** Closed-loop linear position control on each axis.
- **Robotics:** Joint-angle feedback for trajectory tracking.
- **Automotive:** Throttle-position sensing, suspension travel measurement.
- **Aerospace:** Flight-control surface deflection (LVDTs are standard).
- **Civil Engineering:** Crack-movement monitoring in bridges and dams.
- **Biomedical:** Catheter-tip position tracking, prosthetic limb feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potentiometric Displacement Sensor

Consider a uniform resistance wire of total length $L$ and total end-to-end resistance $R_{tot}$, excited by a stable d.c. source $V_{ex}$. A wiper taps a fraction $x/L$ of the total length, where $x$ is the displacement from the lower end.

**Step 1 — Resistance per unit length:**
Since the wire is uniform, the resistance per unit length is constant.

$$
r = \frac{R_{tot}}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Resistance of the tapped portion:**
The portion between the wiper and the lower end has length $x$, so its resistance is

$$
R_{x} = r \cdot x = \frac{R_{tot}}{L} \cdot x
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage-divider rule:**
The output voltage is the voltage across the tapped portion, given by the standard voltage-divider equation.

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{tot}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_{x}$:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\frac{R_{tot}}{L} \cdot x}{R_{tot}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $R_{tot}$:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The output voltage is **directly proportional** to the displacement $x$. The transfer function of an ideal potentiometric sensor is a pure gain:

$$
K_{pot} = \frac{V_{ex}}{L} \quad \text{(V per m)}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) Output

The LVDT is a passive inductive transducer with one primary coil ($P$) and two identical secondaries ($S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$) wound on a cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core is coupled to the displacement target.

**Step 1 — Mutual inductance model:**
When the core is at the *null* (central) position, the mutual inductances to $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are equal: $M_{1} = M_{2} = M_{0}$. The induced e.m.f.s in the two secondaries are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase, so the differential output is

$$
V_{o} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Displacement of core by $x$ to the left:**
The coupling to $S_{1}$ increases and to $S_{2}$ decreases.

$$
M_{1} = M_{0} + \Delta M(x), \qquad M_{2} = M_{0} - \Delta M(x)
$$

**Step 3 — Induced e.m.f. in each secondary (assuming a sinusoidal primary current $I_{p}\sin\omega t$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{S1} &= -j\omega (M_{0} + \Delta M) \, I_{p} \\
V_{S2} &= -j\omega (M_{0} - \Delta M) \, I_{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Differential output voltage:**

$$
V_{o} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = -j\omega \, I_{p} \left[ (M_{0} + \Delta M) - (M_{0} - \Delta M) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
V_{o} = -j\omega \, I_{p} \cdot 2 \, \Delta M
$$

**Step 6 — Recognise that $\Delta M$ is a linear function of $x$ for small displacements:**

$$
\Delta M = k \cdot x
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and write the r.m.s. magnitude:**

$$
\vert V_{o} \vert = 2 \omega I_{p} k \cdot \vert x \vert = K_{L} \cdot \vert x \vert
$$

where the LVDT sensitivity is

$$
K_{L} = 2 \, \omega \, I_{p} \, k \quad (\text{V/m})
$$

> **Conclusion:** The r.m.s. magnitude of the differential output voltage is *linearly proportional* to the magnitude of the core displacement, with the *phase* indicating the direction of motion. This phase-sensitive detection gives the LVDT its excellent bidirectional linearity over a typical stroke of **±5 mm** to **±25 mm**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitive Displacement Sensor (Differential Configuration)

**Step 1 — Nominal capacitance (parallel plate, gap $d_{0}$, area $A$, dielectric $\varepsilon_{r}$):**

$$
C_{0} = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d_{0}}
$$

**Step 2 — Place a second identical plate at $d_{0}$ on the other side of the diaphragm:**
Let the central diaphragm move by $\Delta d$ to the right. The right gap becomes $d_{0} - \Delta d$ and the left gap becomes $d_{0} + \Delta d$.

$$
C_{1} = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d_{0} - \Delta d}, \qquad C_{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d_{0} + \Delta d}
$$

**Step 3 — Differential change in capacitance:**

$$
\Delta C = C_{1} - C_{2} = \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A \left( \frac{1}{d_{0} - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_{0} + \Delta d} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the fractions:**

$$
\Delta C = \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A \cdot \frac{(d_{0} + \Delta d) - (d_{0} - \Delta d)}{d_{0}^{2} - (\Delta d)^{2}}
$$

$$
\Delta C = \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A \cdot \frac{2 \Delta d}{d_{0}^{2} - (\Delta d)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — For $\Delta d \ll d_{0}$, the term $(\Delta d)^{2}$ is negligible:**

$$
\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A \cdot \Delta d}{d_{0}^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $C_{0}$:**

$$
\Delta C = \frac{2 C_{0}}{d_{0}} \cdot \Delta d
$$

> **Conclusion:** In a *differential* capacitive configuration, the change in capacitance is **linearly proportional** to the displacement $\Delta d$. The sensitivity in $\text{F/m}$ is

$$
K_{c} = \frac{2 C_{0}}{d_{0}} \quad (\text{F/m})
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Transfer Function of a First-Order Displacement-Measurement Channel

Modelling the sensor, signal conditioner, and ADC as a first-order low-pass filter of gain $K$ and time constant $\tau$, the governing differential equation is

$$
\tau \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + V_{out}(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$
\tau s \, V_{out}(s) + V_{out}(s) = K \, X(s)
$$

Therefore the transfer function is

$$
\frac{V_{out}(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

For a sinusoidal input $x(t) = X_{m} \sin \omega t$ at steady state, the magnitude and phase are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert G(j\omega) \vert &= \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^{2}}} \\
\phi(\omega) &= -\tan^{-1}(\omega \tau)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **$-3\ \text{dB}$ bandwidth** is $\omega_{c} = 1/\tau$ rad/s. This is critical when selecting a displacement sensor for a high-speed servo system.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A potentiometric displacement sensor has total resistance $R_{tot} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, total length $L = 100\ \text{mm}$, and is excited by $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$ d.c. Calculate (a) the output voltage when the wiper is at $x = 35\ \text{mm}$, and (b) the resolution of the sensor if the output is digitised by a 12-bit ADC over the full $0$ to $10\ \text{V}$ range.

**Solution (a):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \cdot \frac{35}{100} = 3.5\ \text{V}
$$

**Solution (b):**
ADC resolution in volts:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} = 2.442\ \text{mV/LSB}
$$

Equivalent displacement resolution:

$$
x_{LSB} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{K_{pot}} = \frac{2.442 \times 10^{-3}}{10 / 0.1} = 2.442 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 24.42\ \mu m
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} = 3.5\ \text{V}$ and $x_{LSB} \approx 24.4\ \mu m$ per LSB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Displacement Measurement Channel

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below maps the *signal-flow architecture* of a generic mechatronic displacement-measurement channel — from mechanical input to digital controller. It captures all functional modules typically examined in KTU Module 1 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Displacement x] --> B[Primary Sensing Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Stage]
    C --> D[Amplifier and Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Converter]
    E --> F[Microcontroller or PLC]
    F --> G[Display or HMI]
    F --> H[Closed Loop Controller]
    H --> I[Actuator Command]
    I --> J[Mechanical Motion]
    J -->|Feedback| A

    subgraph SENSOR[Sensor Front End]
    A
    B
    end

    subgraph SIGNALCHAIN[Analog Signal Chain]
    C
    D
    E
    end

    subgraph DIGITAL[Digital Control Domain]
    F
    G
    H
    I
    J
    end
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Displacement Transducers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Displacement Transducers] --> C1[Contact Type]
    Root --> C2[Non Contact Type]

    C1 --> P1[Potentiometric]
    C1 --> P2[Strain Gauge]
    C1 --> P3[LVDT]

    C2 --> P4[Capacitive]
    C2 --> P5[Eddy Current]
    C2 --> P6[Optical Encoder]
    C2 --> P7[Laser Interferometer]
    C2 --> P8[Ultrasonic]
    C2 --> P9[Hall Effect or Magnetic]
```

### 4.3 LVDT Internal Architecture and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXC[AC Excitation Source] --> PRI[Primary Coil P]
    PRI --> CORE[Movable Ferromagnetic Core]
    CORE --> SEC1[Secondary Coil S1]
    CORE --> SEC2[Secondary Coil S2]
    SEC1 --> DEMOD[Differential Demodulator]
    SEC2 --> DEMOD
    DEMOD --> PHASE[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    PHASE --> OUT[Conditioned DC Output]
    OUT --> CTRL[Controller or PLC]
```

### 4.4 Potentiometric Sensor — Wiper Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Terminal A Vex] --> RES[Uniform Resistance Element Rtot]
    RES --> V2[Terminal B Ground]
    RES --> WIP[Wiper Contact]
    WIP --> VOUT[Output Terminal Vout]
    X[Displacement x] -->|Mechanical Input| WIP
```

### 4.5 Differential Capacitive Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TARGET[Moving Target or Diaphragm] --> SHIFT[Change in Gap delta d]
    SHIFT --> C1[Capacitor C1 Left Plate]
    SHIFT --> C2[Capacitor C2 Right Plate]
    C1 --> BRG[Bridge Circuit]
    C2 --> BRG
    BRG --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> DC[Linear DC Output]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define displacement. Distinguish clearly between **linear displacement** and **angular displacement**. State **two** industrial applications of displacement measurement in mechatronic systems.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Displacement is defined as the change in position of a body or point with respect to a fixed reference frame, measured along a defined path. It is a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction.

**Distinction:**

| Parameter | Linear Displacement | Angular Displacement |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Straight line | Arc of a circle |
| SI Unit | metre (m) | radian (rad) |
| Measured by | LVDT, potentiometer, capacitive probe | Rotary encoder, resolver |
| Range | $\mu m$ to $m$ | $0$ to $2\pi$ rad (or $0^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$) |

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. Position feedback in CNC machine tool slides (linear LVDT).
2. Joint-angle sensing in robotic manipulators (rotary encoder).

*(Word count target: 80–100 words for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the **working principle of an LVDT**. Why is it called a "differential" transformer? State its sensitivity expression.*

**Model Answer:**

The LVDT consists of one primary coil (P) and two identical secondary coils ($S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$) wound symmetrically on a hollow cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core, coupled to the displacement target, slides inside the former. The primary is excited by an a.c. voltage $V_{p}\sin\omega t$.

When the core is at the *null* (central) position, the e.m.f.s induced in $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase, so the differential output is zero. When the core displaces by $x$, the coupling to one secondary increases and to the other decreases, producing a net differential output

$$
V_{o} = K_{L} \cdot x
$$

where $K_{L} = 2 \omega I_{p} k$ is the LVDT sensitivity in V/m.

It is called "differential" because the output is the *difference* of the two secondary voltages, not their sum. This arrangement cancels common-mode errors, harmonics, and temperature drifts, giving excellent linearity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Missing the null-position concept** in LVDT answers (loses 2 marks).
> - **Writing $C = \varepsilon_{0} A / d$** without specifying *which variable changes* (loses 1 mark on linearity).
> - **Forgetting the phase directionality** of the LVDT output (loses 1 mark).
> - **Failing to state the assumption $\Delta d \ll d_{0}$** in capacitive derivation (loses 2 marks).
> - **Mixing up gauge factor $G$** with Poisson's ratio in strain-gauge answers (loses 2 marks).
> - **Not specifying units** in final numerical answers (loses 0.5–1 mark per sub-part).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Potentiometric + LVDT Combination

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the transfer-function relation $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (x / L)$ for a linear potentiometric displacement sensor. A $10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $50\ \text{mm}$ potentiometer is excited by $5\ \text{V}$ d.c. Determine the output voltage and the wiper resistance when the displacement is $x = 20\ \text{mm}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Show that the differential output voltage is proportional to the core displacement $x$, and state the significance of phase reversal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
As shown in Section 3.1, for a uniform resistance wire of total length $L$ and total resistance $R_{tot}$ excited by $V_{ex}$:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**
Given: $R_{tot} = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $L = 50\ \text{mm}$, $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$, $x = 20\ \text{mm}$.

Step 1 — Output voltage:

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{20}{50} = 2\ \text{V}
$$

Step 2 — Wiper resistance (resistance per unit length times the wiper position):

$$
R_{x} = \frac{R_{tot}}{L} \cdot x = \frac{10\,000}{50} \cdot 20 = 4\,000\ \Omega = 4\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Final Answer (a):** $V_{out} = 2\ \text{V}$ and $R_{x} = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Construction (2 marks):** The LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical former on which three coils are wound — one primary (P) in the centre and two identical secondaries ($S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$) on either side, connected in series opposition. A movable ferromagnetic core, attached to the displacement target, slides inside the former.

**Working (3 marks):** The primary is energised by an a.c. source $V_{p}\sin\omega t$. When the core is at the *null* (central) position, the e.m.f.s in $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are equal and opposite, giving zero differential output. When the core moves by $x$ towards $S_{1}$, the mutual inductance with $S_{1}$ rises and with $S_{2}$ falls. The differential output is, as derived in Section 3.2:

$$
V_{o} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 2 \omega I_{p} k \cdot x = K_{L} \cdot x
$$

**Significance of Phase Reversal (2 marks):** The phase of the output voltage reverses when the core crosses the null position. This phase reversal enables *phase-sensitive demodulation*, which gives:
- Unambiguous sign of displacement (direction of motion).
- Cancellation of residual null voltage and harmonics.
- Excellent zero-crossing stability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Capacitive + Piezoelectric Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the relationship between capacitance and gap displacement for a parallel-plate capacitive sensor. Show how a *differential* arrangement linearises the response, and obtain an expression for sensitivity. A capacitive sensor with $A = 100\ \text{mm}^{2}$, $d_{0} = 1\ \text{mm}$, and $\varepsilon_{r} = 1$ is connected in a differential bridge. Find the differential change in capacitance for $\Delta d = 10\ \mu m$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric displacement sensor. Why is it unsuitable for measuring *static* displacement? A piezoelectric crystal has $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$ and is loaded with a force $F = 10\ \text{N}$. Calculate the generated charge and the equivalent open-circuit voltage if the crystal capacitance is $C_{p} = 1\ \text{nF}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** From Section 3.3, for a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{r} A}{d}
$$

A single-ended capacitive sensor is non-linear in $1/d$. Placing two identical capacitors on either side of a moving diaphragm gives a *differential* arrangement in which

$$
\Delta C = \frac{2 C_{0}}{d_{0}} \cdot \Delta d
$$

which is **linear** in $\Delta d$ for $\Delta d \ll d_{0}$.

**Sensitivity (1 mark):** $K_{c} = 2 C_{0} / d_{0}$ in F/m.

**Numerical (3 marks):**
Given: $A = 100\ \text{mm}^{2} = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{2}$, $d_{0} = 1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_{r} = 1$, $\Delta d = 10\ \mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$.

Step 1 — Nominal capacitance:

$$
C_{0} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(100 \times 10^{-6})}{10^{-3}} = 8.854 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F} \approx 0.885\ \text{pF}
$$

Step 2 — Differential change:

$$
\Delta C = \frac{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-3}} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 1.771 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F} \approx 17.7\ \text{fF}
$$

**Final Answer (a):** $\Delta C \approx 17.7\ \text{fF}$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):** Certain crystals (quartz, PZT, barium titanate) develop an electric charge on their faces when subjected to mechanical stress. The charge $Q$ is proportional to the applied force $F$ through the piezoelectric charge coefficient $d_{33}$:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

The applied force $F = k \cdot x$ where $k$ is the stiffness of the crystal, so $Q$ is also proportional to the mechanical displacement $x$.

**Why Unsuitable for Static Displacement (2 marks):** The generated charge leaks away through the input resistance of the measuring circuit and through the internal resistance of the crystal itself. Only *dynamic* (time-varying) displacements maintain a measurable charge; static displacement produces a charge that decays exponentially with the time constant $\tau = R C_{p}$, making static measurement impractical without a charge amplifier.

**Numerical (2 marks):**
Given: $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$, $F = 10\ \text{N}$, $C_{p} = 1\ \text{nF} = 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$.

Step 1 — Generated charge:

$$
Q = (400 \times 10^{-12}) \times 10 = 4 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} = 4\ \text{nC}
$$

Step 2 — Open-circuit voltage:

$$
V = \frac{Q}{C_{p}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-9}} = 4\ \text{V}
$$

**Final Answer (b):** $Q = 4\ \text{nC}$ and $V = 4\ \text{V}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Displacement** = change in position w.r.t. a fixed reference. **Linear** (m) and **angular** (rad).
- **Potentiometer:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot x/L$ — simple, low cost, but contact wear and limited resolution.
- **LVDT:** $V_{o} = K_{L} \cdot x$ with phase indicating direction — high accuracy ($\mu m$ level), infinite resolution, robust.
- **Capacitive (differential):** $\Delta C = (2 C_{0}/d_{0}) \cdot \Delta d$ — non-contact, very high resolution, suitable for tiny displacements.
- **Piezoelectric:** $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ — **dynamic only**; never use for static displacement.
- **Strain Gauge:** $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon = G \cdot x / L$ — gauge factor $G \approx 2$ for constantan.
- **Optical Encoders:** Quantised digital output — incremental (pulses) vs absolute (Gray code).
- **Bandwidth:** Sensor–signal-chain modelled as first-order low-pass with $G(s) = K / (\tau s + 1)$ and $-3\ \text{dB}$ cutoff at $\omega_{c} = 1/\tau$.
- **Selection Criteria for KTU Exam Answers:** Range, resolution, accuracy, linearity, bandwidth, contact vs non-contact, environment (temperature, dust, vibration).
- **Phase-sensitive detection** in LVDT cancels null voltage, harmonics, and gives direction — a guaranteed 2-mark concept.
- **Always state assumptions** (e.g., $\Delta d \ll d_{0}$, small $x$ for LVDT linearity) — examiners reward rigour.
- **Always include units** in the final answer of every numerical — failure to do so costs 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-part.
- **Block-diagram flow** to memorise: *Physical Input → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Controller → Actuator → Feedback*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position and proximity sensors-I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Position and proximity sensors-I

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Position and Proximity Sensors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Position sensors** are transducers that detect the **linear or angular displacement** of a target object and convert it into a usable electrical output signal. **Proximity sensors** are non-contact devices that detect the **presence, absence, or distance** of a target without physical touch.

In mechatronic systems, these sensors form the **first stage of the feedback loop**, providing spatial awareness to controllers. The **KTU 2024 Scheme** categorizes them under the broader umbrella of *displacement and position transducers*, which are foundational to closed-loop control, robotics, and CNC systems.

### Taxonomy of Sensors (Syllabus-Aligned)

| Class | Principle | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Contact-type Position** | Resistive, electromechanical | Potentiometers (linear/rotary) |
| **Inductive Position** | Mutual/self-inductance variation | LVDT, RVDT, Synchros, Resolvers |
| **Eddy-Current Proximity** | Electromagnetic induction losses | Eddy-current probes |
| **Capacitive Proximity** | Capacitance change | Capacitive plates, concentric sensors |
| **Inductive Proximity** | Reluctance change | Ferromagnetic-core inductive switches |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Feet and Inches" of a Machine:** Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to find a wall in a dark room. You could **touch** the wall (contact sensor), **hear** the air compression change as you approach (ultrasonic), or **feel heat radiating** from it (infrared). Position and proximity sensors are the **"eyes and fingers"** of a mechatronic system — they tell the controller *where* a part is and *how close* it is to another surface.

### Key Physical Constants and Standards

- **Vacuum Permittivity (ε₀):** $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Primary LVDT Excitation Frequency:** Typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz**
- **Standard Linear Range for LVDT:** $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 500$ mm
- **Resolution of Potentiometer:** Limited by winding pitch (typically **0.01 mm to 0.1 mm**)
- **Eddy-Current Probe Frequency:** **50 kHz to 10 MHz**
- **Inductive Sensor Sensing Range:** Typically **1 mm to 50 mm**

---

## 1.3 Why These Sensors Matter in Mechatronics

Position and proximity sensors bridge the gap between the **mechanical world** (moving parts) and the **electrical/control world** (microcontrollers, PLCs). Without them:

- Servo systems cannot close the position loop.
- Robots cannot localize end-effectors.
- Automated assembly lines cannot verify part placement.
- Safety interlocks (e.g., door-closed detection) cannot function.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 focuses on sensors whose output is **analog (continuous)** — Part-I covers the foundational passive/active transducers (potentiometer, LVDT, eddy-current, capacitive, inductive). Part-II typically covers *digital* position sensing (encoders) and *tactile/specialty* sensors.

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Role

A position sensor typically sits in the **sensing layer** of a mechatronic system:

```
Mechanical Displacement → [Sensor] → Analog/Digital Signal → [Signal Conditioning] → [Controller] → [Actuator]
```

Every sub-topic in this module must be understood in terms of:
1. **What physical variable changes** with displacement?
2. **What electrical parameter** is converted (R, L, C, M, V)?
3. **What is the output equation** relating the two?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Potentiometric Position Sensors

### 2.1.1 Construction and Principle

A **potentiometer** is a resistive element with a movable wiper. It operates as a **voltage divider** when a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied across its two end terminals.

- **Linear Potentiometer:** Wiper moves along a straight resistive track.
- **Rotary Potentiometer:** Wiper rotates on a circular track (typically 0°–360°).

### 2.1.2 Operating Logic

1. Apply reference voltage $V_{ref}$ across terminals A (top) and B (bottom).
2. Wiper C slides along the resistive element of total length $L$ (or total resistance $R_{total}$).
3. The output voltage is **proportional to the wiper's fractional displacement**.

### 2.1.3 Output Equation

For a uniform (linear) resistance distribution:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{total}}$$

Where:
- $x$ = wiper displacement from the reference end (m)
- $L$ = total track length (m)
- $R_{x}$ = resistance between reference end and wiper (Ω)
- $R_{total}$ = total resistance of the track (Ω)

### 2.1.4 Why This Works — Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> The resistive track is wound with uniform spacing of resistance wire. Each "turn" of wire has a tiny resistance $\Delta R$. As the wiper crosses each turn, $R_x$ increases by $\Delta R$. By Ohm's Law, voltage divides in the same ratio as resistance.

### 2.1.5 Real-World Utility

- Joystick position feedback in RC controllers
- Throttle position sensors in older vehicles
- Linear actuators with integrated position feedback
- Servo-motor shaft position in CNC machines

### 2.1.6 Limitations

- **Contact wear** — mechanical degradation over millions of cycles
- **Limited resolution** — quantized to one wire-winding pitch
- **Loading effect** — output impedance affects linearity
- **Sliding noise** — contact resistance variation

---

## 2.2 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

### 2.2.1 Construction

An LVDT consists of:
- **One primary winding** ($N_p$ turns) — center of the bobbin
- **Two identical secondary windings** ($N_{s1}$, $N_{s2}$) — symmetrically placed on either side of the primary
- A **movable ferromagnetic core** (armature) attached to the target

### 2.2.2 Operating Logic

1. Primary is excited by an AC source $V_p$ (typically 1–10 kHz).
2. The core couples flux into the two secondaries.
3. Secondaries are connected in **series-opposition** (anti-phase).
4. Net output $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$.

### 2.2.3 Core Coupling Principle

- When the core is at the **null (center) position:** $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$ → $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the core moves **toward secondary 1:** $V_{s1}$ increases, $V_{s2}$ decreases.
- When the core moves **toward secondary 2:** $V_{s1}$ decreases, $V_{s2}$ increases.

The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ indicates displacement magnitude; the **phase** (with reference to $V_p$) indicates displacement direction.

### 2.2.4 Output Equation (Ideal Linear Range)

$$V_{out} = k_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

Where $k_{LVDT}$ is the **sensitivity** (V/mm), and $x$ is the displacement from null.

### 2.2.5 Why LVDT Is Foundational

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LVDT is the **gold standard** for non-contact linear position measurement in industrial automation. KTU examiners frequently test:
> 1. The **null-balance concept** (core at center → zero output).
> 2. **Phase detection** for direction sensing.
> 3. **Series-opposition connection** of secondaries.
> 4. The role of **ferromagnetic core** (flux concentration).

### 2.2.6 Engineering Utility

- Aircraft flight-control surface feedback
- Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position
- Pressure transducer internal sensing (via Bourdon tube + LVDT)
- High-precision machine tool positioning

---

## 2.3 Eddy-Current Proximity Sensors

### 2.3.1 Principle

A high-frequency AC current in a **sensor coil** generates an alternating magnetic field. When a **conductive target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target's surface (Faraday's Law). These eddy currents create an **opposing magnetic field** (Lenz's Law), reducing the coil's inductance and increasing its effective resistance.

### 2.3.2 Operating Logic

1. Sensor coil excited at high frequency (50 kHz – 10 MHz).
2. Eddy currents in target dissipate energy → reduce coil's $Q$-factor.
3. Coil impedance $Z$ changes as a function of target distance $d$.
4. The change in impedance is detected by an oscillator or bridge circuit.

### 2.3.3 Impedance Relation

$$Z = R_{eff} + j\omega L_{eff}$$

Where $R_{eff}$ and $L_{eff}$ both depend on target distance $d$, target conductivity $\sigma$, and target permeability $\mu$.

### 2.3.4 Key Design Dependencies

- **Conductive target required** (aluminum, copper, steel).
- **Non-ferrous metals** → eddy-current losses dominate.
- **Ferromagnetic targets** → both permeability AND eddy-current effects.
- **Surface finish** affects measurement consistency.
- **Frequency** must be high enough to confine field near target surface (**skin effect**).

### 2.3.5 Why This Works

> [!NOTE]
> The induced eddy currents flow in a thin layer near the target surface (**skin depth** $\delta$). As the target gets **closer**, more flux links the target, the induced currents grow, and the reflected impedance back to the sensor coil grows. The coil's oscillation amplitude drops — this drop is calibrated to distance.

### 2.3.6 Engineering Utility

- **Non-contact** displacement/vibration measurement on rotating shafts
- Crack detection in aerospace structures
- Coin validation in vending machines
- Position feedback in turbo-machinery

---

## 2.4 Capacitive Proximity Sensors

### 2.4.1 Principle

A capacitive sensor measures changes in **electrical capacitance** between two electrodes when a target approaches. The target acts as a **third electrode** or alters the **dielectric** between them.

### 2.4.2 Two Main Modes

| Mode | Operating Principle | Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Variable-distance** | Plate separation $d$ changes | $C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$ |
| **Variable-area** | Overlap area $A$ changes | $C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$ |
| **Variable-dielectric** | $\epsilon_r$ changes (target enters field) | $C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r(x) A}{d}$ |

### 2.4.3 Operating Logic

1. Sensor electrode pair forms a capacitor with an AC excitation.
2. As target approaches, the fringing field interacts with the target.
3. Capacitance changes (increases typically).
4. The change is converted to voltage via an oscillator-detector circuit.

### 2.4.4 Concentric Cylindrical Capacitor (used in many industrial sensors)

$$C = \frac{2\pi \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$$

Where $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius, $L$ = overlap length.

### 2.4.5 Engineering Utility

- Liquid-level sensing (through glass walls)
- Material identification (different $\epsilon_r$)
- Touch-screen technology
- Humidity sensors (hygroscopic dielectric)
- Detecting **both metallic and non-metallic** targets (unique advantage over inductive sensors)

---

## 2.5 Inductive Proximity Sensors

### 2.5.1 Principle

An inductive sensor generates a high-frequency electromagnetic field using an **open-core coil**. When a **ferromagnetic target** enters this field, it changes the **reluctance** of the magnetic circuit, modifying the coil's inductance $L$ and thus its impedance $Z$.

### 2.5.2 Inductance Equation

$$L = \frac{N^2}{R_{m}} = \frac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$$

Where $R_m$ = magnetic reluctance, $N$ = number of turns, $\mu$ = permeability, $A$ = cross-section, $l$ = flux path length.

### 2.5.3 Operating Logic

1. LC oscillator generates high-frequency field (~100 kHz – 1 MHz).
2. Oscillation amplitude is maintained only if no target is in the field.
3. When a **ferrous target** enters, eddy-current and hysteresis losses in the target damp the oscillation.
4. The amplitude drop triggers a switching output (ON/OFF).

### 2.5.4 Key Dependency — Target Material

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inductive sensors **only detect ferromagnetic materials** (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt). They are **blind** to aluminum, copper, and plastics — a critical KTU exam point.

### 2.5.5 Engineering Utility

- End-of-travel limit switches on linear actuators
- Object counting on conveyor belts
- Machine tool position verification
- Automotive gear-position sensing
- Pneumatic cylinder piston-position feedback (via embedded magnet)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Quick-Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE.** The exam frequently asks students to "derive the output equation" for any of the sensors below.

| # | Sensor | Key Output Equation | Units | Major Assumptions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Potentiometer (linear) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | V | Uniform resistance track |
| 2 | Potentiometer (rotary) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | V | Uniform winding |
| 3 | LVDT (ideal) | $V_{out} = k_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | V | Within linear range, core not saturated |
| 4 | LVDT (sensitivity form) | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{x}{a}$ | V | $a$ = half-length of primary |
| 5 | Eddy-current coil | $Z = R_{eff}(d) + j\omega L_{eff}(d)$ | Ω | Linear calibration region |
| 6 | Capacitive (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Fringing neglected |
| 7 | Capacitive (cylindrical) | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F | Long-cylinder approximation |
| 8 | Inductive coil | $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$ | H | Closed magnetic path |
| 9 | Mutual inductance | $M = \dfrac{N_1 N_2 \mu A}{l}$ | H | Ideal coupling |
| 10 | Sensitivity (general) | $S = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dx}$ | V/mm or V/° | Slope of transfer curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** All absolute-value notations in equations are written as `\vert` (not `|`) to prevent markdown table corruption. For instance, voltage sensitivity is written as $S = \vert dV_{out}/dx \vert$ in the answer book, but stored here without pipes.

---

## 2.7 Cross-Sensor Comparison (Examiner's Favorite)

| Feature | Potentiometer | LVDT | Eddy-Current | Capacitive | Inductive |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Contact** | Yes | No | No | No | No |
| **Range** | mm–m | $\mu$m–500 mm | 0.1–10 mm | 1–30 mm | 1–50 mm |
| **Resolution** | $\sim$10 $\mu$m | Infinite | $\sim$0.1 $\mu$m | $\sim$1 $\mu$m | Switching only |
| **Target material** | Any | Ferromagnetic | Conductive | Any | Ferromagnetic |
| **Harsh environment** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium-high | High | Medium | Low |
| **Output** | DC analog | AC (needs demod) | AC | AC | Switching |
| **KTU Weightage** | Low | **Very High** | Medium | Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Potentiometer Output Equation

### Setup
- Total resistance $R_{total}$ spread uniformly over length $L$.
- Wiper at displacement $x$ from the lower end.
- Reference voltage $V_{ref}$ applied across full track.

### Step 1 — Resistance Per Unit Length

$$\rho_{lin} = \frac{R_{total}}{L} \quad \text{(Ω/m)}$$

### Step 2 — Resistance Below Wiper

$$R_{x} = \rho_{lin} \cdot x = \frac{R_{total} \cdot x}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Voltage Divider Rule

The output $V_{out}$ is the voltage across the lower portion (resistance $R_x$):

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{total}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{total} \cdot x / L}{R_{total}}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each step cancels one geometric-to-electrical mapping. The total resistance $R_{total}$ cancels out in the final form, showing that the output depends only on the **geometric fraction** $x/L$ — a beautiful and intuitive result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT Output Equation (Full Analysis)

### Setup
- Primary $N_p$ turns, two identical secondaries $N_{s1}$ and $N_{s2}$.
- AC excitation $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ on primary.
- Core displacement $x$ from null position.
- Range of linear operation: $\vert x \vert \leq a$ (where $a$ is half the primary length).

### Step 1 — Flux Generated by Primary

For a sinusoidal primary excitation:

$$\phi_p = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2 — Induced EMF in Each Secondary (Ideal Transformer Equation)

By Faraday's Law applied to an ideal transformer:

$$V_{s} = N_{s} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

For a sinusoidal flux $\phi = \phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$:

$$V_{s} = N_{s} \cdot \omega \phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 3 — Voltage Induced in Each Secondary

The flux linking each secondary depends on the core's position. Define:
- $M_1(x)$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary 1
- $M_2(x)$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary 2

The amplitude of induced voltages:

$$V_{s1,rms} = j\omega M_1(x) \cdot I_p$$

$$V_{s2,rms} = j\omega M_2(x) \cdot I_p$$

### Step 4 — Mutual Inductance as a Function of Displacement

Within the linear region, the mutual inductances vary linearly with core position:

$$M_1(x) = M_0 - kx$$

$$M_2(x) = M_0 + kx$$

Where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at null position, and $k$ is the coupling coefficient per unit displacement.

### Step 5 — Net Output Voltage (Series-Opposition)

The two secondaries are connected in series-opposition, so the net output is the **difference**:

$$V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = j\omega I_p \cdot [M_1(x) - M_2(x)]$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Mutual Inductances

$$V_{out} = j\omega I_p \cdot [(M_0 - kx) - (M_0 + kx)]$$

$$V_{out} = j\omega I_p \cdot [-2kx]$$

### Step 7 — Final Output Equation

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -2 j\omega I_p k \cdot x}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The result shows:
- **Magnitude** $\vert V_{out} \vert$ is **proportional to displacement** $x$ — perfect for linear measurement.
- The **sign** (or phase) indicates **direction** of displacement.
- At null ($x = 0$), $V_{out} = 0$ — a key feature of the differential design.
- The factor **$2$** is why LVDT is called *differential* — it doubles the sensitivity compared to a single-secondary design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitive Sensor with Variable Distance

### Setup
- Two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by distance $d$.
- Dielectric of permittivity $\epsilon = \epsilon_0 \epsilon_r$ between them.

### Step 1 — Define Electric Field and Voltage

For uniform field between plates:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r}$$

Where $\sigma = Q/A$ is the surface charge density.

### Step 2 — Voltage Between Plates

$$V = E \cdot d = \frac{Q d}{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}$$

### Step 3 — Capacitance Definition

By definition, $C = Q/V$:

$$C = \frac{Q}{Q d / (\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A)} = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$$

### Step 4 — Final Equation

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity to Distance

If $d$ is the variable and $A$, $\epsilon_r$ are constant:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

The **negative sign** indicates capacitance increases as distance decreases.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

> **Problem:** A linear potentiometer has total resistance $10$ kΩ distributed over a length of $50$ mm. A reference voltage of $5$ V is applied. If the wiper is at $12$ mm from the reference end, calculate:
> (a) Output voltage
> (b) Output resistance (resistance between wiper and ground)
> (c) Sensitivity

### Solution

#### (a) Output Voltage

Using the derived equation:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 5 \cdot \frac{12}{50}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 1.2 \text{ V}}$$

#### (b) Output Resistance (Resistance Between Wiper and Reference End)

Resistance per unit length:

$$\rho_{lin} = \frac{R_{total}}{L} = \frac{10000}{50} = 200 \text{ Ω/mm}$$

Resistance at wiper position:

$$R_x = \rho_{lin} \cdot x = 200 \cdot 12 = 2400 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R_x = 2.4 \text{ kΩ}}$$

#### (c) Sensitivity

Sensitivity is the slope of the transfer characteristic:

$$S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = \frac{V_{ref}}{L} = \frac{5}{50}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.1 \text{ V/mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means **every 1 mm of wiper movement produces 0.1 V output change** — useful for calibrating the readout electronics.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — LVDT Style Problem

> **Problem:** An LVDT has a sensitivity of $0.5$ V/mm. The primary is excited with a $5$ V, $2$ kHz AC source. If the core is displaced by $8$ mm toward secondary 1, find:
> (a) Output voltage magnitude
> (b) Phase of output relative to primary

### Solution

#### (a) Magnitude

$$V_{out} = S \cdot x = 0.5 \cdot 8 = 4 \text{ V (rms)}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 4 \text{ V rms}}$$

#### (b) Phase

By convention:
- Displacement toward **secondary 1** → output in **phase** with primary.
- Displacement toward **secondary 2** → output **180° out of phase** with primary.

$$\boxed{\text{Phase} = 0° \text{ (in phase with primary)}}$$

This phase information is what allows a **phase-sensitive demodulator** to determine direction.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Eddy-Current Skin Depth

### Setup
Eddy currents are not uniform through the target — they concentrate near the surface.

### Step 1 — Define Skin Depth

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}}$$

Where:
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $\mu$ = permeability of target (H/m)
- $\sigma$ = conductivity of target (S/m)
- $\rho = 1/\sigma$ = resistivity (Ω·m)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)

### Step 2 — Interpretation

$\delta$ is the depth at which eddy-current density falls to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value.

**Conversion Logic:** Higher frequency → smaller skin depth → eddy currents confined to thin surface layer. This is why eddy-current sensors are excellent for **surface inspection** of conductive materials.

### Step 3 — Numerical Example

For aluminum ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m) at $f = 100$ kHz:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \cdot 10^5 \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 3.5 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{1.38 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.27 \text{ mm}}$$

This means eddy currents flow in the topmost 0.27 mm — perfect for surface flaw detection.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Sensor | Output Electrical Variable | Governing Physical Law | Linear Range | Hysteresis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Potentiometer | $V_{out}$ (DC) | Ohm's Law | Full track | Moderate |
| LVDT | $V_{out}$ (AC) | Faraday's Law | $\pm a$ | Very low |
| Eddy-Current | $Z$ (impedance) | Faraday + Lenz | 0.5$\delta$ – 3$\delta$ | Low |
| Capacitive | $C$ (capacitance) | Gauss's Law | Within calibrated span | Very low |
| Inductive | $L$ (inductance) | Ampere's Law | Switching threshold only | High |
