These three equations can solve for up to **three unknown reactions** in a planar problem.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Second Law | $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | $\vec{F}$ in **N**, $m$ in **kg**, $\vec{a}$ in **m/s²** |
| 2 | Weight | $W = mg$ | $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 3 | Linear Momentum | $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ | $\vec{p}$ in **kg·m/s** |
| 4 | Impulse–Momentum | $\vec{J} = \int \vec{F}\,dt = \Delta \vec{p}$ | $\vec{J}$ in **N·s** |
| 5 | Force Components | $F_x = F\cos\theta,\ F_y = F\sin\theta$ | $\theta$ from $x$-axis |
| 6 | Resultant Magnitude | $R = \sqrt{R_x^{\,2} + R_y^{\,2}}$ | 2-D plane |
| 7 | Resultant Direction | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$ | measured CCW from $+x$ |
| 8 | Moment of Force | $M_O = F \cdot d$ | $d$ = perpendicular moment arm |
| 9 | Couple Moment | $M = F \cdot d$ | $d$ = separation of forces |
| 10 | Equilibrium | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0,\ \sum M_O = 0$ | static, coplanar |
| 11 | Friction (static) | $f_s \le \mu_s N$ | impending motion: $f_s = \mu_s N$ |
| 12 | Friction (kinetic) | $f_k = \mu_k N$ | $\mu_k < \mu_s$ typically |
| 13 | Centripetal Force | $F_c = \dfrac{mv^2}{r} = m\omega^2 r$ | directed toward centre |
| 14 | Hooke's Law (spring) | $F = kx$ | $k$ in **N/m**, $x$ in **m** |

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Mechatronic Engineering

- **Force Sensors / Load Cells:** Convert mechanical force into measurable electrical signals using strain gauges (operating on Hooke's law).
- **Robotic Manipulators:** Force-torque sensors at end-effectors for compliant motion control.
- **Actuator Selection:** Determining required thrust/torque of DC motors, hydraulic cylinders, and pneumatic pistons.
- **Haptic Devices:** Controlled feedback forces applied to the user through mechanical interfaces.
- **Vibration Isolation:** Spring–damper force analysis to protect sensitive mechatronic assemblies.
- **CNC Machines:** Cutting force estimation for tool wear prediction and feed-rate optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Second Law from Momentum

**Starting Point:** The **linear momentum** of a particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ is defined as:

$$\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the time derivative to both sides to find the rate of change of momentum:

$$\frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\,(m\vec{v})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the product rule of differentiation:

$$\frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\,\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} + \vec{v}\,\frac{dm}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** For a system with **constant mass** (which is the standard assumption in classical mechatronics — no fuel burning, no mass loss):

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute this back into the equation:

$$\frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\,\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$ is, by definition, the acceleration $\vec{a}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$$

**Step 6:** By definition of Newton's Second Law, the **net external force** equals this rate of change of momentum:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$$

**Conclusion:** The familiar relation $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ is the constant-mass simplification of the more general momentum-based definition. This is the form universally used in KTU board problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Resultant of Two Coplanar Concurrent Forces (Parallelogram Law)

Consider two forces $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ acting at a common point $O$, with an angle $\alpha$ between them.

**Step 1:** Resolve each force into rectangular $x$ and $y$ components.

For $\vec{F}_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ from the $x$-axis:
$$F_{1x} = F_1 \cos\theta_1, \quad F_{1y} = F_1 \sin\theta_1$$

For $\vec{F}_2$ at angle $\theta_2$ from the $x$-axis:
$$F_{2x} = F_2 \cos\theta_2, \quad F_{2y} = F_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Step 2:** Sum the components to get the resultant components.

$$R_x = F_{1x} + F_{2x} = F_1\cos\theta_1 + F_2\cos\theta_2$$

$$R_y = F_{1y} + F_{2y} = F_1\sin\theta_1 + F_2\sin\theta_2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the magnitude using the Pythagorean theorem.

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^{\,2} + R_y^{\,2}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the direction (angle from $x$-axis).

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$$

**Special Case — Parallelogram Law:** When $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ are placed tail-to-tail and a parallelogram is completed, the diagonal represents the resultant. Its magnitude can be written compactly using the law of cosines:

$$R = \sqrt{F_1^{\,2} + F_2^{\,2} + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\phi}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ when placed tail-to-tail.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A robotic arm end-effector is subjected to three coplanar concurrent forces:
- $\vec{F}_1 = 100\,\text{N}$ along the $+x$ direction.
- $\vec{F}_2 = 150\,\text{N}$ at $60°$ from the $+x$ axis (counter-clockwise).
- $\vec{F}_3 = 80\,\text{N}$ at $135°$ from the $+x$ axis (counter-clockwise).

Find the **magnitude** and **direction** of the resultant force.

**Step 1:** Compute the $x$-components.

$$F_{1x} = 100\cos(0°) = 100.00\,\text{N}$$

$$F_{2x} = 150\cos(60°) = 150 \times 0.5000 = 75.00\,\text{N}$$

$$F_{3x} = 80\cos(135°) = 80 \times (-0.7071) = -56.57\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the $y$-components.

$$F_{1y} = 100\sin(0°) = 0.00\,\text{N}$$

$$F_{2y} = 150\sin(60°) = 150 \times 0.8660 = 129.90\,\text{N}$$

$$F_{3y} = 80\sin(135°) = 80 \times 0.7071 = 56.57\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the components.

$$R_x = 100.00 + 75.00 + (-56.57) = 118.43\,\text{N}$$

$$R_y = 0.00 + 129.90 + 56.57 = 186.47\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude of the resultant.

$$R = \sqrt{(118.43)^2 + (186.47)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{14025.7 + 34771.1} = \sqrt{48796.8}$$

$$R \approx 220.90\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5:** Direction of the resultant.

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{186.47}{118.43}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5744)$$

$$\alpha \approx 57.58°\ \text{(CCW from +x axis)}$$

**Answer:** $\vec{R} \approx 220.9\,\text{N}$ at $57.58°$ above the $+x$-axis.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Equilibrium of a Lever (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A uniform horizontal beam of length $L = 4\,\text{m}$ and weight $W = 200\,\text{N}$ is supported at its ends by two vertical reactions $R_A$ (at $A$, $x=0$) and $R_B$ (at $B$, $x=L$). A point load of $P = 300\,\text{N}$ is applied at $x = 1.5\,\text{m}$ from $A$. Find $R_A$ and $R_B$.

**Step 1:** Draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD).

The beam has: upward reactions $R_A$ and $R_B$; downward weight $W = 200\,\text{N}$ acting at the centre ($x = 2\,\text{m}$); downward applied load $P = 300\,\text{N}$ at $x = 1.5\,\text{m}$.

**Step 2:** Apply vertical force equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_A + R_B - W - P = 0$$

$$R_A + R_B = 200 + 300 = 500\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3:** Apply moment equilibrium about point $A$ (CCW positive).

$$\sum M_A = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_B(L) - W\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) - P(1.5) = 0$$

$$R_B(4) - 200(2) - 300(1.5) = 0$$

$$4R_B - 400 - 450 = 0$$

$$4R_B = 850 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_B = 212.5\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4:** Back-substitute to find $R_A$.

$$R_A = 500 - R_B = 500 - 212.5 = 287.5\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5:** Verify by taking moments about $B$.

$$\sum M_B = -R_A(4) + W(2) + P(2.5) = -287.5(4) + 200(2) + 300(2.5)$$

$$= -1150 + 400 + 750 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $R_A = 287.5\,\text{N}$ (upward) and $R_B = 212.5\,\text{N}$ (upward).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Force Analysis Architecture in Mechatronic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EXTERNAL INPUT"] --> B["ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM"]
    B -- "Applied Force F_a" --> C["MECHANICAL LINKAGE"]
    C -- "Transmitted Force F_t" --> D["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
    D -- "Electrical Signal" --> E["CONTROLLER UNIT"]
    E -- "Feedback Command" --> B
    C -- "Reaction Forces" --> F["FIXED SUPPORT / FRAME"]
    G["DISTURBANCE FORCES"] --> C
    H["GRAVITATIONAL FORCE"] --> C
    I["FRICTION FORCE"] --> C
```

### 4.2 Free Body Diagram Construction — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Identify the body to isolate"] --> S2["STEP 2: Draw isolated body with shape and dimensions"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Identify ALL external forces acting on the body"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Mark forces at correct points and directions"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Label magnitudes with symbols"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Choose reference axes and moment centre"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Apply equilibrium equations"]
    S7 --> S8["SOLUTION: Unknown reactions and internal forces"]
```

### 4.3 Force Resolution Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["GIVEN FORCE VECTOR F"] --> R1["ANGLE THETA FROM X AXIS"]
    R1 --> C1["COMPONENT Fx = F cos theta"]
    R1 --> C2["COMPONENT Fy = F sin theta"]
    C1 --> M["MAGNITUDE F = sqrt Fx sq + Fy sq"]
    C2 --> M
    M --> D["DIRECTION THETA = tan inv Fy over Fx"]
    C1 --> RES["RESULTANT BY SUMMING COMPONENTS"]
    C2 --> RES
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Force in a Closed-Loop Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["REFERENCE FORCE COMMAND"] --> CMP["COMPARATOR"]
    CMP -- "ERROR SIGNAL" --> CTRL["CONTROLLER GAIN K"]
    CTRL -- "VOLTAGE COMMAND" --> AMP["POWER AMPLIFIER"]
    AMP -- "DRIVE SIGNAL" --> MOT["DC MOTOR ACTUATOR"]
    MOT -- "MECHANICAL FORCE F" --> LOAD["MECHANICAL LOAD"]
    LOAD -- "POSITION OUTPUT" --> FS["FORCE SENSOR LOAD CELL"]
    FS -- "SENSOR VOLTAGE Vs" --> CMP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above block diagrams illustrate the **role of force as an engineering variable** in a mechatronic chain. From the actuator that **produces** force, through the linkage that **transmits** it, to the load cell that **measures** it — every stage is governed by the principles outlined in this module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define force. State Newton's three laws of motion.**

**Model Answer:**

Force is a vector quantity that tends to cause a change in the state of motion or shape of a body upon which it acts. Its SI unit is the **Newton (N)**.

**Newton's Three Laws:**
1. **First Law (Inertia):** A body remains at rest or in uniform straight-line motion unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.
2. **Second Law (Momentum):** The net external force equals the rate of change of linear momentum: $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt = m\vec{a}$ (for constant mass).
3. **Third Law (Action–Reaction):** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Action and reaction always act on **different bodies**.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between mass and weight. State the units of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Mass | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measure of the amount of matter in a body | Force exerted on a body due to gravity |
| Nature | Scalar quantity | Vector quantity |
| SI Unit | **Kilogram (kg)** | **Newton (N)** |
| Variation | Independent of location | Varies with location (depends on $g$) |
| Measurement | Beam balance | Spring balance |
| Formula | Measured directly | $W = mg$ |

For example, a body of mass 10 kg weighs $W = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1\,\text{N}$ on Earth, but only about $16.3\,\text{N}$ on the Moon (where $g \approx 1.63\,\text{m/s}^2$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain the parallelogram law of forces. Two forces of magnitude **80 N** and **120 N** act at a point with an angle of **60°** between them. Determine the magnitude and direction of the resultant using the parallelogram law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a *couple*. A motor delivers a torque of **25 N·m** to a shaft through a pulley of radius **10 cm**. Find the tangential force exerted on the belt wrapped around the pulley. State the principle used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Statement of Parallelogram Law:**
> If two forces acting simultaneously at a point are represented in magnitude and direction by the adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then their resultant is represented in magnitude and direction by the diagonal of the parallelogram passing through that point.

**Given:** $F_1 = 80\,\text{N}$, $F_2 = 120\,\text{N}$, $\phi = 60°$ (angle between forces).

**Step 1:** Apply the parallelogram law formula (law of cosines).

$$R = \sqrt{F_1^{\,2} + F_2^{\,2} + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\phi}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(80)^2 + (120)^2 + 2(80)(120)\cos 60°}$$

$$R = \sqrt{6400 + 14400 + 19200 \times 0.5}$$

$$R = \sqrt{6400 + 14400 + 9600}$$

$$R = \sqrt{30400} \approx 174.36\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2:** Find the angle $\alpha$ that $R$ makes with $F_1$ (using the parallelogram sine relation):

$$\frac{\sin\alpha}{F_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{R}$$

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{F_2 \sin\phi}{R} = \frac{120 \times \sin 60°}{174.36} = \frac{120 \times 0.8660}{174.36} = \frac{103.92}{174.36}$$

$$\sin\alpha \approx 0.5960 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha \approx 36.59°$$

**Answer:** $R \approx 174.36\,\text{N}$, making an angle $\alpha \approx 36.59°$ with the 80 N force.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Definition of Couple:**
A **couple** consists of two equal, opposite, and parallel forces whose lines of action are separated by a finite perpendicular distance. A couple produces **pure rotation** (no translation) and its moment is independent of the reference point chosen.

**Given:** Torque $M = 25\,\text{N·m}$, pulley radius $r = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.10\,\text{m}$.

**Principle Used:** Moment of a couple = one force × perpendicular distance between the two forces (i.e., the diameter or radius depending on geometry).

For a belt wrapped around a pulley, the **tangential force** $F$ acts at the rim. The torque generated by this tangential force is:

$$M = F \times r$$

**Step 1:** Solve for the tangential force:

$$F = \frac{M}{r} = \frac{25\,\text{N·m}}{0.10\,\text{m}}$$

$$F = 250\,\text{N}$$

**Answer:** The tangential (belt) force is **250 N**. The principle used is the moment of a couple, which equals the tangential force times the radius of the pulley.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the *moment of a force* about a point. A uniform horizontal beam of length **6 m** and weight **300 N** is pivoted at one end. A load of **500 N** is suspended from the other end. Find the reaction at the pivot and the magnitude of the force that must be applied vertically upward at a point **2 m from the pivot** to keep the beam in equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State **Varignon's Theorem**. Two forces of magnitudes **100 N** and **150 N** act at a point and their resultant is **200 N**. Find the angle between the two forces. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** The **moment of a force** about a point is the product of the magnitude of the force and the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force. It measures the rotational tendency of the force.

$$M_O = F \cdot d$$

**Given:** Length $L = 6\,\text{m}$, weight $W = 300\,\text{N}$ at centre ($x = 3\,\text{m}$), end load $P = 500\,\text{N}$ at $x = 6\,\text{m}$, pivot reaction $R_P$ (vertical) at $x = 0$, applied upward force $F_a$ at $x = 2\,\text{m}$.

**Part 1 — Reaction at pivot (no applied force yet, beam is not in equilibrium):**

Vertical equilibrium alone is insufficient without $F_a$. So we proceed to **Part 2**, then back-substitute.

**Part 2 — Equilibrium with applied force $F_a$ at $x = 2\,\text{m}$:**

Take moments about the pivot $P$ (CCW positive, taking downward loads as creating CW moment — taken negative):

$$\sum M_P = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_a(2) - W(3) - P(6) = 0$$

$$2F_a - 300(3) - 500(6) = 0$$

$$2F_a - 900 - 3000 = 0$$

$$2F_a = 3900 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_a = 1950\,\text{N}$$

**Part 3 — Find the pivot reaction** from vertical force equilibrium.

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_P + F_a - W - P = 0$$

$$R_P = W + P - F_a = 300 + 500 - 1950$$

$$R_P = -1150\,\text{N}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the pivot reaction actually acts **downward** (the applied upward force is so large it tends to lift the pivot end). The magnitude is **1150 N directed downward**.

**Answer:** $R_P = 1150\,\text{N}$ (downward), $F_a = 1950\,\text{N}$ (upward).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Statement of Varignon's Theorem:**
> The moment of the resultant of a system of coplanar concurrent forces about any point in the plane is equal to the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces about the same point.

**Given:** $F_1 = 100\,\text{N}$, $F_2 = 150\,\text{N}$, resultant $R = 200\,\text{N}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the parallelogram law (cosine form):

$$R^2 = F_1^{\,2} + F_2^{\,2} + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\phi$$

$$(200)^2 = (100)^2 + (150)^2 + 2(100)(150)\cos\phi$$

$$40000 = 10000 + 22500 + 30000\cos\phi$$

$$40000 = 32500 + 30000\cos\phi$$

$$30000\cos\phi = 7500$$

$$\cos\phi = \frac{7500}{30000} = 0.25$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the angle:

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.25) \approx 75.52°$$

**Answer:** The angle between the two forces is approximately **75.52°**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing mass with weight** — Weight is a force ($W = mg$); mass is the quantity of matter (in kg). Marks are routinely deducted for stating "weight = 1 kg".
> 2. **Sign errors in equilibrium** — Always define a clear sign convention (e.g., CCW positive for moments, upward positive for forces) **before** writing the equations.
> 3. **Wrong moment arm** — Using the slant distance instead of the **perpendicular** distance from the pivot to the line of action.
> 4. **Direction of friction** — Friction always **opposes the relative motion (or tendency of motion)**. Drawing it in the wrong direction is a frequent error.
> 5. **Mixing up action and reaction** — Remember Newton's Third Law pair acts on **two different bodies**; they never appear in the same free body diagram.
> 6. **Forgetting units in final answer** — Always report force in **N**, moment in **N·m**, and angles in **degrees** (unless otherwise specified).
> 7. **Rounding too early** — Keep at least 4 significant digits during intermediate steps; round only at the final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Force** is a **vector** (magnitude + direction + point of application); SI unit is the **Newton (N)**.
- **Newton's Second Law:** $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ is the workhorse equation for force problems.
- **Weight** is the gravitational force on a body: $W = mg$, with $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.
- **Resolve** forces into $x$- and $y$-components using $F\cos\theta$ and $F\sin\theta$ before summing.
- **Resultant** is the vector sum: $R = \sqrt{R_x^{\,2} + R_y^{\,2}}$, direction $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(R_y/R_x)$.
- **Moment of force** = Force × perpendicular moment arm; vector form: $\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$.
- **Varignon's Theorem** simplifies moment calculations by allowing component-wise summing.
- **Couple** = two equal, opposite, parallel forces; produces pure rotation; moment $M = F \cdot d$ (independent of reference point).
- **Equilibrium** requires $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$ (coplanar case).
- **Friction** opposes relative motion: $f_s \le \mu_s N$ (static), $f_k = \mu_k N$ (kinetic).
- **Centripetal force** for circular motion: $F_c = mv^2/r = m\omega^2 r$, directed toward the centre.
- **Spring force** (Hooke's Law): $F = kx$, where $k$ is the spring stiffness in N/m.
- **Units to memorize:** $1\,\text{kgf} = 9.81\,\text{N}$; $1\,\text{lbf} \approx 4.448\,\text{N}$; $1\,\text{dyn} = 10^{-5}\,\text{N}$.
- **Free Body Diagram** is the most critical first step — always draw and label it before writing any equation.
- **Sign convention** must be declared explicitly at the beginning of every equilibrium solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Pressure** is defined as the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on any real or imaginary surface in contact with it. Mathematically, it is expressed as the limit of the ratio of the normal force to the area as the area approaches a point.

The governing mathematical expression is:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F_n}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF_n}{dA}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Fluid Pressure (measured in **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**)
- $dF_n$ = Infinitesimal normal force component
- $dA$ = Infinitesimal area element

By convention, **SI Unit of Pressure = 1 N/m² = 1 Pa (Pascal)**, named in honor of the French mathematician and physicist **Blaise Pascal**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Stacked Books" Concept**
> Imagine a vertical stack of books on a table. The bottom book experiences the most "pressure" because it bears the weight of all the books above it. Similarly, in a static fluid column, the pressure at any depth is determined by the weight of the fluid column above that point. The deeper you go in a swimming pool, the more you feel the "push" of water against your eardrums — this is fluid pressure in action.

Consider a hydraulic car jack used in workshops. When you push down on a small piston, the pressure generated is transmitted through the incompressible hydraulic oil to lift a heavy car on a much larger piston. This is the practical embodiment of fluid pressure in **mechatronic actuation systems**.

## 1.3 Scalar Nature of Pressure

A critical concept for KTU exams:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fluid pressure is a **scalar quantity**, not a vector. Although force is a vector, pressure at a point in a static fluid acts **equally in all directions** (Pascal's Principle). This isotropy is what makes fluid pressure uniquely powerful in engineering applications.

## 1.4 Standard Units and Conversions (Must Memorize)

| System | Unit | Symbol | Equivalent in Pa |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SI** | Pascal | Pa | 1 N/m² |
| **SI (kPa)** | Kilopascal | kPa | 10³ Pa |
| **SI (MPa)** | Megapascal | MPa | 10⁶ Pa |
| **CGS** | Bar | bar | 10⁵ Pa |
| **Imperial** | PSI | lbf/in² | 6894.76 Pa |
| **Meteorological** | Atmosphere | atm | 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa |
| **Liquid Column** | mm of Hg | mmHg | 133.322 Pa |
| **Liquid Column** | m of H₂O | mH₂O | 9806.65 Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Memory Hook:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} \approx 1 \text{ bar}$ (commonly rounded in industry).

## 1.5 Classification of Pressure (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum (zero reference)**. Always positive.
- **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured with respect to **local atmospheric pressure**. Can be positive (compression) or negative (vacuum).
- **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Magnitude of pressure **below** atmospheric; expressed as a positive number.

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For all KTU numerical problems, **always clarify the reference** before solving. Most industrial sensors (Bourdon tubes, strain gauges) measure **gauge pressure**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Fluid Pressure

1. **Pressure acts normal to any surface** in contact with the fluid. There is no shear component in a fluid at rest.
2. **Pressure at a point is the same in all directions** (Pascal's Law — proven experimentally in 1653).
3. **Pressure varies only with depth** in a static, incompressible fluid under gravity.
4. **Pressure is a scalar**, even though force is a vector.
5. **Pressure always acts inward (compressive)** on a fluid element — fluids cannot sustain tensile stress at rest.

## 2.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (The Master Equation)

For a static incompressible fluid under uniform gravity, the pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface is:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure at depth $h$
- $P_0$ = Pressure at the free surface (usually atmospheric)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = Vertical depth from free surface (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "+" sign?** Atmospheric pressure $P_0$ always exists and **adds to** the hydrostatic contribution $\rho g h$. If the surface is open to atmosphere, $P_0 = P_{atm}$. If sealed with gas, $P_0$ is the gas pressure.

## 2.3 Pressure Head and Pressure Energy

The quantity $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ is called the **pressure head** and is expressed in metres of fluid. This is critical for **Bernoulli's equation** and **flow measurement** in mechatronic systems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Basic pressure definition | Force-area problems |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Tanks, reservoirs |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute-gauge relation | Sensor calibration |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head | Flow measurement |
| 5 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Force from pressure | Hydraulic cylinders |
| 6 | $W = P \cdot \Delta V$ | Work done by pressure | Actuator energy |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \rho g \Delta h$ | Differential manometer | U-tube manometers |
| 8 | $P_1 = P_2$ (same elevation) | Pascal's Law | Hydraulic press |
| 9 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation | Flow conservation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Hydraulic lever | Force multiplication |

## 2.5 Pascal's Law (Cornerstone of Hydraulics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** *Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of the containing vessel.*

**Engineering Utility in Mechatronics:**

- **Hydraulic Presses:** Used in plastic injection molding, metal forming, and forging.
- **Hydraulic Brakes:** Car and aircraft braking systems.
- **Hydraulic Lifts:** Vehicle service stations and aircraft jacks.
- **Hydraulic Accumulators:** Energy storage in mechatronic systems for peak demand.
- **Pneumatic Positioners:** Process control valves in automation.

### Force Multiplication in Hydraulic Systems

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \implies F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

If $A_2 = 10 \cdot A_1$, then a small input force of 100 N produces an output force of **1000 N**. The trade-off: displacement reduces by the same ratio (conservation of energy).

## 2.6 Manometry — Measuring Fluid Pressure

### 2.6.1 Simple U-Tube Manometer

For a differential manometer connecting two points A and B:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where $\rho_m$ is the manometric fluid density (typically mercury or a colored oil) and $\rho_f$ is the process fluid density.

### 2.6.2 Inclined Manometer

For improved sensitivity at low pressures:

$$\Delta P = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$$

Where $L$ is the inclined length of the meniscus movement and $\theta$ is the inclination angle from horizontal.

## 2.7 Real-World Applications in Mechatronics

| Application | Pressure Range | Sensor/Actuator Used |
|---|---|---|
| Pneumatic grippers | 4–8 bar | Solenoid valves, FRL unit |
| Hydraulic press | 100–700 bar | Load cell, relief valve |
| Aircraft hydraulics | 200–350 bar | Servo valve, LVDT |
| Robotic arm hydraulic | 70–210 bar | Proportional valve |
| Water supply system | 2–6 bar | Pressure transducer |
| Process control | 0–10 bar | Diaphragm sensor |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Variation with Depth

### Statement to Prove
In a static, incompressible fluid under gravity, pressure increases linearly with depth as $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Consider a fluid element.**

Take a small vertical column of fluid with cross-sectional area $A$ and height $dh$ at depth $h$ below the free surface.

$$\text{Volume of element} = A \cdot dh$$

**Step 2 — Determine the forces acting on the element.**

Three vertical forces act on the element:
- Pressure force on top surface: $P \cdot A$ (acting downward)
- Pressure force on bottom surface: $(P + dP) \cdot A$ (acting upward)
- Weight of the element: $W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh \cdot g$ (acting downward)

**Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition.**

For a static fluid, the net vertical force must equal zero (Newton's First Law):

$$(P + dP) \cdot A - P \cdot A - \rho \cdot A \cdot dh \cdot g = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the equation.**

The $P \cdot A$ terms cancel out:

$$dP \cdot A = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh \cdot g$$

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dh$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with appropriate limits.**

Integrate from the free surface (where pressure = $P_0$ and $h = 0$) to an arbitrary depth $h$ (where pressure = $P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals.**

Assuming $\rho$ and $g$ are constants for an incompressible fluid under uniform gravity:

$$P - P_0 = \rho g h$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This equation is a direct consequence of **Newton's Second Law** applied to a static fluid element. The weight of the fluid column above any point must be supported by an equal increase in pressure from below.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force on a Submerged Vertical Plane Surface

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for the total hydrostatic force on a vertical rectangular gate submerged in a liquid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**

Consider a vertical rectangular surface of width $b$ and height $H$, submerged with its top edge at depth $h_1$ and bottom edge at depth $h_2$ from the free surface.

**Step 2 — Pressure at an elemental strip.**

Consider a horizontal strip of thickness $dh$ at depth $h$. The pressure at this depth is:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

**Step 3 — Force on the elemental strip.**

The area of the strip is $dA = b \cdot dh$, so the elemental force is:

$$dF = P \cdot dA = (P_0 + \rho g h) \cdot b \cdot dh$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to find total force.**

$$F = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} (P_0 + \rho g h) \cdot b \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$F = b \cdot P_0 \cdot (h_2 - h_1) + b \cdot \rho g \cdot \frac{(h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize that $(h_2^2 - h_1^2) = (h_2 - h_1)(h_2 + h_1)$.**

Let $H = h_2 - h_1$ (height of gate) and $\bar{h} = (h_1 + h_2)/2$ (centroid depth):

$$F = P_0 \cdot b \cdot H + \rho g \bar{h} \cdot b \cdot H$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form.**

The total area of the gate is $A = b \cdot H$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{F = (P_0 + \rho g \bar{h}) \cdot A = P_c \cdot A}$$

Where $P_c$ is the **pressure at the centroid** of the submerged surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is a high-yield derivation. The result $F = P_c \cdot A$ is a **general result** valid for **any shape** of submerged plane surface, not just rectangular. The centroid is the key to solving complex problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Center of Pressure for a Vertical Rectangular Surface

### Concept
The resultant hydrostatic force does **not** act at the centroid for a vertical surface — it acts lower, at the **center of pressure** $h_p$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Moment about the free surface.**

Taking moments of the elemental force about the free surface (at $h = 0$):

$$dM = h \cdot dF = h \cdot (P_0 + \rho g h) \cdot b \, dh$$

**Step 2 — Total moment.**

$$M = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot (P_0 + \rho g h) \cdot b \, dh$$

$$M = b \cdot P_0 \cdot \frac{(h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{2} + b \cdot \rho g \cdot \frac{(h_2^3 - h_1^3)}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Use the result $h_p = M/F$.**

For pure hydrostatic pressure ($P_0 = 0$, i.e., neglecting atmospheric), and using $h_1 = 0$ for simplicity (gate from free surface to depth $H$):

$$F = \rho g \cdot b \cdot \frac{H^2}{2}$$

$$M = \rho g \cdot b \cdot \frac{H^3}{3}$$

$$h_p = \frac{M}{F} = \frac{\rho g \cdot b \cdot H^3 / 3}{\rho g \cdot b \cdot H^2 / 2}$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{h_p = \frac{2H}{3}}$$

For a gate extending from the free surface to depth $H$, the resultant force acts at $\frac{2H}{3}$ below the free surface, i.e., at $\frac{H}{3}$ from the bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Formula** (for any submerged plane surface with centroid at $\bar{h}$ and second moment of area $I_c$ about the centroidal axis):
> $$h_p = \bar{h} + \frac{I_c}{A \cdot \bar{h}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: U-Tube Manometer Equation

### Problem
Two points A and B in a pipe are connected by a U-tube manometer containing mercury (density $\rho_{Hg}$). The fluid in the pipe has density $\rho_f$. Derive the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Start at point A and trace pressure changes down and up the manometer.**

$$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (z_A - z_1) - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_B) = P_B$$

**Step 2 — Note that the net vertical distance from A to the lower mercury level minus the upper mercury level yields a simplified form.**

After algebraic simplification:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the manometric fluid is **much denser** than the process fluid (e.g., mercury vs. water, $\rho_{Hg} \gg \rho_f$), we often approximate:
> $$P_A - P_B \approx \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Solving Pressure Problems in Mechatronics

| Problem Type | Key Equation | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| Depth pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Forgetting atmospheric term |
| Force on submerged plate | $F = P_c \cdot A$ | Using edge depth, not centroid |
| Center of pressure | $h_p = \bar{h} + I_c / (A \bar{h})$ | Confusing $\bar{h}$ with $h_p$ |
| Manometer | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Forgetting manometric fluid density |
| Hydraulic lever | $F_2 = F_1 (A_2 / A_1)$ | Ignoring friction losses |
| Buoyancy (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$ | Confusing density (object vs fluid) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hydrostatic Pressure Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm] --> B[Free Surface of Fluid]
    C[Fluid Column of Height h] --> B
    B --> D[Fluid Element at Depth h]
    E[Density rho] --> D
    F[Gravity g] --> D
    D --> G[Hydrostatic Pressure P = P_atm + rho g h]
    G --> H[Pressure Transducer]
    H --> I[Signal Conditioning]
    I --> J[Microcontroller ADC]
    J --> K[Display / Actuator Control]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Force Multiplication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Side"]
        A1[Small Piston Area A1] --> B1[Input Force F1]
    end
    subgraph Transmission["Hydraulic Fluid (Incompressible Oil)"]
        C1[Pressure P transmitted equally] 
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Side"]
        D1[Large Piston Area A2] --> E1[Output Force F2 = F1 x A2/A1]
    end
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: U-Tube Manometer Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pipe Point A: Pressure P_A] --> M1[Down leg - Process Fluid rho_f]
    M1 --> MX[Mercury Interface Left: Height h_L]
    MX --> M2[Mercury Column: Height Difference h]
    M2 --> MY[Mercury Interface Right: Height h_R]
    MY --> M3[Up leg - Process Fluid rho_f]
    M3 --> P2[Pipe Point B: Pressure P_B]
    EQ[P_A - P_B = rho_Hg x g x h - rho_f x g x h_delta]
    P1 --> EQ
    P2 --> EQ
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Pressure Measurement & Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint Pressure] --> SUM((Comparator))
    FB[Feedback from Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PID Controller / Microcontroller]
    CTRL --> DRV[PWM Driver / Valve Actuator]
    DRV --> VLV[Proportional Control Valve]
    VLV --> ACT[Hydraulic / Pneumatic Actuator]
    ACT --> PROC[Process / Load]
    PROC --> SENSE[Pressure Transducer]
    SENSE --> FB
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Submerged Surface Force & Center of Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FS[Free Surface P_atm] --> A1[Submerged Vertical Plate]
    A1 --> STR[Horizontal Strip at Depth h, Width b]
    STR --> EF[Elemental Force dF = rho g h b dh]
    EF --> INT[Integration over Depth h1 to h2]
    INT --> TF[Total Force F = P_centroid x Area]
    INT --> CP[Center of Pressure h_p = h_bar + I_c / A h_bar]
    TF --> RES[Resultant Force Acting at h_p]
    CP --> RES
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question Bank]**
**Define fluid pressure. State and explain Pascal's Law. Mention two engineering applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fluid pressure is the normal force per unit area exerted by a fluid on a surface. It is mathematically expressed as $P = dF/dA$, with SI unit of Pascal (N/m²).

**Pascal's Law (1.5 marks):** Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions throughout the fluid and to the walls of the container. This implies that the pressure at any two points at the same elevation in a connected fluid is equal: $P_1 = P_2$ if $z_1 = z_2$.

**Applications (0.5 mark):** 
1. Hydraulic press / lift in automobile workshops.
2. Hydraulic braking system in vehicles and aircraft.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Question Bank]**
**Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure. A tire pressure gauge reads 30 psi. If atmospheric pressure is 14.7 psi, find the absolute pressure in the tire.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Difference (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| Value at atmosphere | 1 atm (positive) | 0 |
| Sign | Always positive | Can be +ve or −ve |
| Sensor example | Sealed reference transducer | Bourdon gauge |

**Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 30 + 14.7 = 44.7 \text{ psi}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Set A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 1 Important Question]**

**(a)** State and derive the hydrostatic pressure equation. A closed tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.85 to a depth of 4 m. If the air pressure above the oil surface is 120 kPa, determine the pressure at the bottom of the tank. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube mercury manometer is connected to two pipes A and B carrying water. The mercury level difference is 200 mm, with the higher mercury level on side B. If pipe A is at elevation 1.5 m and pipe B is at 1.0 m below the manometer centerline, find the pressure difference $(P_A - P_B)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B1(a)

**Step 1 — State Hydrostatic Equation (2 marks):**

The pressure at a depth $h$ below a free surface in a static incompressible fluid is:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key result comes from equilibrium of a fluid element: $dP = \rho g \, dh$, integrated to yield $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- Specific gravity of oil = 0.85, so $\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $P_0 = P_{air} = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
- $h = 4 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

$$P = 120 \times 10^3 + 850 \times 9.81 \times 4$$

$$P = 120{,}000 + 33{,}354 = 153{,}354 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 153.35 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for B1(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the manometer equation (1 mark):**

For a U-tube manometer with mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and water ($\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \Delta z$$

**Step 2 — Identify given values (1 mark):**

- Mercury height difference: $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Elevation difference: Pipe A is 1.5 m above the centerline, Pipe B is 1.0 m below the centerline, so $\Delta z = z_A - z_B = 1.5 - (-1.0) = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- Mercury is **higher** on side B → pressure at A is **greater** than B at the same elevation

**Step 3 — Compute pressure difference (5 marks):**

$$P_A - P_B = (13{,}600)(9.81)(0.2) - (1000)(9.81)(2.5)$$

$$P_A - P_B = 26{,}683.2 - 24{,}525 = 2{,}158.2 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 2.16 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** 
> 1. Students often **forget to convert mm to m** (lose 0.5 mark).
> 2. Students frequently **drop the water column term** $\rho_f g \Delta z$ (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Sign convention confusion:** When mercury is higher on side B, students sometimes wrongly conclude $P_B > P_A$ without using the proper equation. Always use the systematic trace method: start at A, add $\rho g \Delta z$ when going down, subtract when going up, then equate to $P_B$.

---

### Question B1 — Set B (Internal Choice) (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Module 1 Important Question]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the total hydrostatic force acting on a vertical submerged plane surface. Find the location of the center of pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 50 mm and a large piston of diameter 300 mm. An input force of 200 N is applied on the small piston. Calculate: (i) the pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid, (ii) the output force on the large piston, and (iii) the distance moved by the large piston if the small piston moves 100 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B1-Set B(a)

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry (1 mark):**

Consider a vertical plane surface submerged in a static liquid with its top edge at depth $h_1$ and bottom edge at depth $h_2$ below the free surface.

**Step 2 — Elemental force on a strip (2 marks):**

At depth $h$, pressure is $P = \rho g h$. For a horizontal strip of width $b$ and thickness $dh$:

$$dF = P \cdot dA = \rho g h \cdot b \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Total force by integration (2 marks):**

$$F = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \rho g h b \, dh = \rho g b \left[\frac{h^2}{2}\right]_{h_1}^{h_2} = \rho g b \cdot \frac{(h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{2}$$

Using centroid depth $\bar{h} = (h_1 + h_2)/2$ and area $A = b(h_2 - h_1)$:

$$\boxed{F = \rho g \bar{h} A = P_c \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Center of Pressure (2 marks):**

Taking moments about the free surface:

$$M = \int h \cdot dF = \rho g b \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h^2 \, dh = \rho g b \cdot \frac{(h_2^3 - h_1^3)}{3}$$

Since $h_p = M / F$:

$$h_p = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{h_2^3 - h_1^3}{h_2^2 - h_1^2}$$

For a general plane surface:

$$\boxed{h_p = \bar{h} + \frac{I_c}{A \bar{h}}}$$

where $I_c$ is the second moment of area about the centroidal axis.

---

#### Model Solution for B1-Set B(b)

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $d_2 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$, $s_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Areas (1 mark):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pressure developed (2 marks):**

By Pascal's Law, pressure at the small piston equals pressure at the large piston:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 101{,}859 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 101.86 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Output force (2 marks):**

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 101{,}859 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 = 7200 \text{ N} = 7.2 \text{ kN}}$$

Alternative check: $F_2 = F_1 \times (A_2/A_1) = 200 \times 36 = 7200$ N ✓ (since $A_2/A_1 = (d_2/d_1)^2 = 36$)

**Step 4 — Distance moved by large piston (2 marks):**

By conservation of volume (incompressible fluid): $A_1 s_1 = A_2 s_2$

$$s_2 = \frac{A_1 s_1}{A_2} = \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1}{7.069 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$\boxed{s_2 = 2.78 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.78 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hydraulic Press Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Diameter must be in **metres** before squaring; mm² gives wrong answer by factor of 10⁶. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Pascal's Law** and writing $F_2 = F_1$ (incorrect; you get 1 mark at most for setup).
> 3. **Forgetting volume conservation** for the displacement part. Lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not verifying with the ratio shortcut** $A_2/A_1 = (d_2/d_1)^2 = 36$ — useful for double-checking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fluid Pressure**

- ✅ **Pressure = Normal Force / Area**, scalar quantity, SI unit is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — the **master equation** for static fluid pressure.
- ✅ **Pressure at a point is isotropic** — same in all directions (Pascal's Law).
- ✅ **Absolute pressure = Gauge pressure + Atmospheric pressure** — memorize this relation.
- ✅ **For a U-tube manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — never forget the manometric fluid density.
- ✅ **Force on a submerged plane surface:** $F = P_c \cdot A$ where $P_c$ is pressure at the **centroid**.
- ✅ **Center of pressure** is **always below the centroid** for vertical surfaces ($h_p > \bar{h}$).
- ✅ **Hydraulic press force gain:** $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1)$; displacement reduces by same ratio.
- ✅ **Conversion constants:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} \approx 1 \text{ bar}$; $1 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$; $1 \text{ mmHg} = 133.32 \text{ Pa}$.
- ✅ **Always convert mm to m** and use **SI units** throughout to avoid KTU valuation penalties.
- ✅ **Archimedes' Principle (bonus):** Buoyant force $F_B = \rho_f g V_{displaced}$ — relevant to submerged objects in mechatronic fluid systems.
- ✅ **Real-world importance:** Hydraulic/pneumatic systems, brake systems, robotic actuators, and process control valves are the **core mechatronic applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook for the Master Equation:** **"Pee-Zero plus Rho-Gee-Haitch"** — sing it, sketch it, and solve it. This single equation unlocks 60%+ of KTU Module 1 numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid flow sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquid flow sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Liquid Flow Sensor** (or Liquid Flow Transducer) is a mechatronic measurement device that converts the kinematic state of a moving liquid — specifically its volumetric or mass flow rate — into a standardized electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital pulse) that can be processed by a microcontroller, PLC, or data acquisition system.

In mechatronic terminology, a liquid flow sensor is the **primary sensing element** in a closed-loop fluid control architecture. It bridges the **physical domain** (fluid mechanics) with the **information domain** (electronics and computing), which is the very essence of mechatronic integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Liquid flow sensors are treated under the broader category of *Process Variable Sensors* within Module 1 of OEEET833. Students are expected to understand the **transduction principle**, **output interfacing**, and **signal conditioning** aspects relevant to embedded control.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tap-Water Intuition

Imagine the **water meter installed on the pipe entering your house**. When you open the tap, water flows through the meter. Inside, a small turbine (or magnetic rotor) spins faster when more water passes. Each rotation triggers a tiny magnet passing a Hall-effect sensor, generating a pulse. The meter counts pulses and displays the volume.

**That mechanical counter is essentially a liquid flow sensor in its most familiar form.**

Translating this analogy to engineering:

| Familiar Element | Engineering Equivalent |
|------------------|------------------------|
| Tap opening wider | Increasing volumetric flow rate $Q$ |
| Turbine spinning faster | Higher rotational frequency $f$ |
| Hall pulse per rotation | Electrical pulse output |
| Display reading | Microcontroller count register |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *Every liquid flow sensor ultimately translates "how much liquid per second" into "how many electrical events per second"* — this is the mechatronic essence of the topic.

## 1.3 Classification of Liquid Flow Sensors (Overview)

Liquid flow sensors fall under two umbrella classifications based on the **measurement quantity**:

1. **Volumetric Flow Sensors** — measure volume per unit time ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)
2. **Mass Flow Sensors** — measure mass per unit time ($\text{kg}/\text{s}$)

And three **transduction principles**:

1. **Differential-Pressure based** — Orifice, Venturi, Pitot tube
2. **Velocity based** — Turbine, Electromagnetic, Ultrasonic, Vortex
3. **Direct-displacement based** — Coriolis, Thermal mass, Positive displacement

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Quantities:**
> - Density of water at **4 °C**: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Standard volumetric flow unit (SI): $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
> - Standard mass flow unit (SI): $\text{kg/s}$
> - Reynolds Number transition (Laminar → Turbulent): $\text{Re} \approx 2300$ (pipes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Differential Pressure (Obstruction-Type) Flow Sensors

**Core Idea:** When a liquid is forced through a constriction (orifice/venturi), its velocity increases and pressure drops. Measuring this pressure drop $\Delta P$ gives the flow rate.

**Why it works (the physics):** Conservation of mass (continuity) + Conservation of energy (Bernoulli). Faster fluid = lower static pressure.

**How it is implemented:**

- A primary element (orifice plate / venturi / nozzle) creates a known pressure drop.
- A differential pressure transducer (typically a strain-gauge bridge or capacitive sensor) measures $\Delta P$.
- A signal conditioner converts the analog pressure into a standard output (**4–20 mA** or **0–10 V**).

### 2.1.2 Turbine Flow Sensor

**Core Idea:** Liquid flow drives a multi-bladed rotor. The rotor's angular velocity $\omega$ is proportional to flow velocity $v$.

- A permanent magnet on the rotor passes a **pickup coil** (or Hall sensor).
- Output is a **frequency $f$** proportional to flow: $f = k \cdot Q$.
- Suitable for clean, non-abrasive liquids (water, light oils).

### 2.1.3 Electromagnetic Flow Sensor

**Core Idea:** Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A conductive liquid flowing through a transverse magnetic field induces an EMF across electrodes.

- $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$
- Output voltage $E$ is **directly proportional** to flow velocity $v$.
- Only works with **electrically conductive** liquids (water, slurries, blood).

### 2.1.4 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

**Core Idea:** Sound pulses travel at different effective speeds *with* and *against* the flow. The **time difference** $\Delta t$ correlates with velocity.

- Uses **piezoelectric transducers** as both transmitter and receiver.
- Non-invasive (clamp-on type) — ideal for hygienic and corrosive applications.

### 2.1.5 Coriolis Mass Flow Sensor

**Core Idea:** Liquid flowing through a vibrating U-tube experiences the **Coriolis force**, which twists the tube proportionally to mass flow rate.

- Direct mass measurement — unaffected by density, temperature, viscosity.
- Used in high-accuracy process industries (oil & gas, pharmaceuticals).

### 2.1.6 Vortex-Shedding Flow Sensor

**Core Idea:** A bluff body placed in the flow generates alternating vortices (Kármán vortex street). Vortex frequency $f_v$ is proportional to flow velocity.

- Output is a **pulse frequency** — linear, stable, and accurate.
- Strouhal number governs the relationship.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Saver — Memorize These Equations:**

| # | Sensor / Principle | Key Equation | Variable Meaning |
|---|--------------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Orifice / Venturi (General)** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$ | $Q$: volumetric flow, $C_d$: discharge coefficient, $A_2$: throat area, $\beta = D_2/D_1$ |
| 2 | **Venturi Meter** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho}{2}(v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | from Bernoulli equation |
| 3 | **Orifice Plate** | $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1-(A_o/A_p)^2}}$ | $h$: manometric head |
| 4 | **Pitot Tube** | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | $h$: stagnation-head difference |
| 5 | **Turbine Flowmeter** | $f = K \cdot Q$ | $K$: K-factor (pulses per unit volume) |
| 6 | **Electromagnetic** | $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$ | Faraday-induced EMF |
| 7 | **Ultrasonic (Transit-Time)** | $v = \dfrac{L^2}{2 d} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_1 t_2}$ | $d$: path length along flow, $t_1$, $t_2$: upstream/downstream times |
| 8 | **Vortex Shedding** | $f_v = \text{St} \cdot \dfrac{v}{D}$ | $\text{St}$: Strouhal number ($\approx 0.2$), $D$: bluff body width |
| 9 | **Coriolis Mass Flow** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_t}{2 L^2 \omega} \cdot \Delta \tau$ | $C_t$: tube stiffness, $\Delta \tau$: time lag of tubes |
| 10 | **Reynolds Number** | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | flow regime indicator |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial & Mechatronic Relevance:**
> - **Process Automation:** Closed-loop control of chemical dosing, fuel injection, and coolant circulation in CNC machines.
> - **Biomedical Devices:** Insulin pumps, dialysis machines, blood flow measurement.
> - **Automotive Mechatronics:** Fuel rail pressure sensing, EGR flow monitoring, AdBlue dosing.
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Precision irrigation based on real-time flow feedback.
> - **Building Automation:** HVAC chilled water balancing, leak detection systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discharge Through an Orifice Plate (with full algebraic expansion)

Consider a pipe of cross-sectional area $A_1$ carrying an incompressible liquid of density $\rho$. A thin concentric orifice plate restricts the flow to a contracted area $A_2$ at the **vena contracta**.

**Step 1: Apply the Continuity Equation**
The mass flow entering must equal mass flow leaving.

$$
\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2
$$

Cancelling $\rho$:

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2
$$

$$
v_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} v_2
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (vena contracta)**

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $v_1$ from continuity**

$$
v_1^2 = \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 v_2^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]
$$

**Step 4: Define $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ and solve for $v_2$**

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by area to get ideal theoretical discharge**

$$
Q_{\text{ideal}} = A_2 \cdot v_2 = A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}
$$

**Step 6: Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for viscous and contraction losses**

$$
Q = C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $C_d$ is empirically determined — typically **0.61 to 0.65** for sharp-edged orifice plates and **0.95 to 0.99** for venturi meters. The closer $C_d$ is to 1, the closer the actual flow is to the ideal (lossless) theoretical value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Venturi Meter Velocity from Bernoulli

**Given:** A venturi with inlet diameter $D_1$ and throat diameter $D_2$. A differential manometer reads $h$ meters of the manometric fluid (density $\rho_m$) above the process fluid (density $\rho$).

**Step 1: Bernoulli equation (same elevation)**

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2: Continuity**

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \;\Rightarrow\; v_1 = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 v_2
$$

**Step 3: Pressure difference from manometer**

$$
P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho) g h
$$

**Step 4: Combine**

$$
\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \dfrac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}
$$

Substituting $v_1$:

$$
\dfrac{(\rho_m - \rho) g h}{\rho} = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4\right]
$$

**Step 5: Solve for throat velocity $v_2$**

$$
v_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(\rho_m - \rho) g h}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 6: Final volumetric flow rate (with discharge coefficient $C_d$)**

$$
Q = C_d \, A_2 \, v_2 = \dfrac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(\rho_m - \rho) g h}{\rho}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity from Pitot Tube (Stagnation Principle)

**Step 1:** A Pitot tube measures the difference between **stagnation pressure** $P_0$ and **static pressure** $P_s$.

$$
P_0 - P_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2
$$

**Step 2:** In a manometric reading, $P_0 - P_s = \rho g h$

$$
\rho g h = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2
$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $\rho$ and solve for $v$

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the simplest flow-velocity equation in the syllabus — high-weightage in KTU Part A questions.**

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criteria Table — Common KTU Examination Question:**

| Property | Orifice Plate | Venturi | Turbine | Electromagnetic | Ultrasonic | Coriolis |
|----------|---------------|---------|---------|-----------------|------------|----------|
| Measures | Volumetric | Volumetric | Volumetric | Volumetric | Volumetric | **Mass** |
| Output type | $\Delta P$ → analog | $\Delta P$ → analog | Frequency pulse | Analog voltage | Digital time | Frequency/analog |
| Accuracy | ±2–4 % | ±1 % | ±0.5 % | ±0.5 % | ±1–2 % | ±0.1 % |
| Pressure loss | High | **Low** | Medium | None | None | None |
| Conductive liquid required? | No | No | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High | **Very High** |
| Suitable for dirty liquids | Limited | Good | Poor | Good | Good | Good |
| Best Application | General industry | High-flow pipelines | Clean fuels, water | Slurries, chemicals | Hygienic, corrosive | High-precision batching |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow Chart of Liquid Flow Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Liquid Flow Sensors"]:::root --> B["Volumetric Flow Sensors"]:::branch
    A --> C["Mass Flow Sensors"]:::branch

    B --> D["Differential Pressure Type"]:::leaf
    B --> E["Velocity Type"]:::leaf
    B --> F["Positive Displacement Type"]:::leaf
    B --> G["Variable Area Type"]:::leaf

    D --> D1["Orifice Plate"]:::detail
    D --> D2["Venturi Meter"]:::detail
    D --> D3["Flow Nozzle"]:::detail
    D --> D4["Pitot Tube"]:::detail

    E --> E1["Turbine Flowmeter"]:::detail
    E --> E2["Electromagnetic Flowmeter"]:::detail
    E --> E3["Ultrasonic Flowmeter"]:::detail
    E --> E4["Vortex Shedding Flowmeter"]:::detail

    F --> F1["Oval Gear Meter"]:::detail
    F --> F2["Lobed Impeller Meter"]:::detail

    G --> G1["Rotameter"]:::detail

    C --> H["Coriolis Mass Flowmeter"]:::detail
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

> **Explain the working principle of an electromagnetic flow meter. Mention any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer (Expected for full 3 marks):**

An electromagnetic flow meter works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When an electrically conductive liquid flows through a transverse magnetic field $B$, an EMF $E$ is induced across two electrodes placed perpendicular to both the flow direction and the field:

$$
E = B \cdot L \cdot v
$$

where $L$ is the distance between the electrodes (effective length of conductor) and $v$ is the average flow velocity. The induced EMF is proportional to the flow velocity and hence to the volumetric flow rate.

**Two Limitations:**
1. The liquid must be **electrically conductive** — it cannot measure flow of hydrocarbons, oils, or pure distilled water.
2. It is **sensitive to entrained air bubbles** and external electromagnetic interference; therefore proper earthing and bubble-free flow are mandatory.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

> **What is the significance of the discharge coefficient $C_d$ in orifice-type flow meters?**

**Model Answer:**

The discharge coefficient $C_d$ is a **dimensionless empirical correction factor** that accounts for the deviation between the **ideal theoretical flow** (derived from Bernoulli and continuity assuming frictionless, non-viscous flow) and the **actual measured flow** through an obstruction-type primary element.

- For a **sharp-edged orifice plate**: $C_d \approx 0.61$ to $0.65$.
- For a **venturi meter**: $C_d \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$ (much closer to ideal).

**Significance:**
- It accounts for **viscous losses**, **vena contracta effect** (where the jet continues to contract downstream of the orifice), and **turbulence**.
- Without $C_d$, the calculated flow would be overestimated by 30–40% for orifice plates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)(b)]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a turbine flow meter. Derive the relationship between output frequency and volumetric flow rate.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A turbine flow meter consists of:
1. A **multi-bladed rotor** (typically ferromagnetic) mounted coaxially on a central shaft.
2. A **magnetic pickup coil** (or Hall-effect sensor) mounted on the outside of the flow body.
3. **End bearings** to support the rotor.
4. A **flow straightener** upstream to remove swirl.

**Working:**
- The flowing liquid strikes the blades of the rotor and imparts a tangential force.
- The rotor attains an angular velocity $\omega$ proportional to the flow velocity.
- Each blade tip carries a magnet (or is itself ferromagnetic) and passes the pickup coil, inducing a voltage pulse.
- The pulse frequency $f$ is measured by the microcontroller.

**Derivation of $f$ – $Q$ relationship:**

Let $N$ = number of blades, $v$ = axial fluid velocity, $v_t$ = tangential velocity of blade tip.

The volumetric flow rate is:

$$
Q = A \cdot v
$$

The tangential velocity of the blade tip is related to fluid velocity through the **blade angle $\theta$**:

$$
v_t = \dfrac{v}{\tan \theta}
$$

The rotor rotates at frequency $f$ (revolutions per second), so:

$$
v_t = 2 \pi R f
$$

Equating:

$$
2 \pi R f = \dfrac{v}{\tan \theta}
$$

Solving for $v$:

$$
v = 2 \pi R f \tan \theta
$$

Substituting into $Q = A v$:

$$
Q = 2 \pi R A \tan \theta \cdot f
$$

Defining the **K-factor** $K = 2 \pi R A \tan \theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{f}{K}\,} \qquad \text{or equivalently} \qquad f = K Q
$$

> This linear relationship is the key reason turbine flow meters are widely used in digital instrumentation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **An orifice plate of diameter 25 mm is fitted in a pipe of 75 mm diameter. The pressure drop across the plate is 8 kPa with water flow ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). If $C_d = 0.62$, calculate the volumetric flow rate.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Pipe diameter $D_1 = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Orifice diameter $D_2 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $\Delta P = 8 \text{ kPa} = 8000 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_d = 0.62$

**Step 1: Calculate areas**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate diameter ratio $\beta$**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.075} = \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\beta^4 = \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^4 = \dfrac{1}{81} = 0.01235
$$

**Step 3: Apply the orifice flow equation**

$$
Q = C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

$$
Q = (0.62)(4.909 \times 10^{-4}) \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8000}{1000 \times (1 - 0.01235)}}
$$

$$
Q = (0.62)(4.909 \times 10^{-4}) \sqrt{\dfrac{16000}{987.65}}
$$

$$
Q = (3.043 \times 10^{-4}) \sqrt{16.2}
$$

$$
Q = (3.043 \times 10^{-4}) \times (4.025)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 1.225 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.225 \text{ L/s}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> **Describe the principle of ultrasonic flow measurement using the transit-time (time-of-flight) method. State its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
Two piezoelectric transducers are mounted diagonally on the pipe, one upstream and one downstream of the flow. Each transducer alternately transmits and receives ultrasonic pulses.

- **Time of flight downstream** (with flow): $t_1 = \dfrac{L}{c - v \cos\theta}$
- **Time of flight upstream** (against flow): $t_2 = \dfrac{L}{c + v \cos\theta}$

where $L$ = path length along acoustic axis, $c$ = speed of sound in the liquid, $v$ = flow velocity, $\theta$ = angle of acoustic path with pipe axis.

**Derivation of velocity:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{1}{t_2} = \dfrac{(c+v\cos\theta) - (c - v\cos\theta)}{L} = \dfrac{2 v \cos\theta}{L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v = \dfrac{L}{2 \cos\theta} \left(\dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{1}{t_2}\right)
$$

Equivalently, using $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ (time difference):

$$
\boxed{\,v = \dfrac{L^2}{2 d \cdot t_1 \cdot t_2} \cdot \Delta t\,}
$$

where $d$ is the axial projection of the path.

**Advantages:**
1. **Non-invasive** — clamp-on sensors require no pipe cutting; ideal for sterile/hygienic applications.
2. **No pressure drop** — no obstruction in the flow path.
3. Suitable for **corrosive, abrasive, and dirty** liquids.
4. No moving parts — **high reliability** and long life.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Requires **clean, bubble-free** liquid; air bubbles cause signal scattering.
2. **High initial cost** and requires careful calibration.
3. Accuracy is lower than Coriolis (±1–2 % vs ±0.1 %).
4. Performance depends on **flow profile** (Reynolds number).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> **A venturi meter has inlet diameter 200 mm and throat diameter 100 mm. A differential U-tube mercury manometer (with water above mercury) reads a deflection of 250 mm. Calculate the volumetric flow rate of water. Take $C_d = 0.98$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.200 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- $h = 0.250 \text{ m}$ (mercury deflection)
- $C_d = 0.98$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Areas**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Diameter ratio**

$$
\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; (D_2/D_1)^4 = 0.0625
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683
$$

**Step 3: Pressure difference from manometer**

$$
\Delta P = (\rho_{\text{Hg}} - \rho_{\text{water}}) \, g \, h
$$

$$
\Delta P = (13600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.250)
$$

$$
\Delta P = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.250 = 30901.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4: Apply venturi flow equation**

$$
Q = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{\text{water}}}}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{(0.98)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})}{0.9683} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 30901.5}{1000}}
$$

$$
Q = (7.949 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \sqrt{61.803}
$$

$$
Q = (7.949 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (7.861)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 0.0625 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 62.5 \text{ L/s}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - Forgetting to **square the diameter ratio** (it is $(D_2/D_1)^4$, not $(D_2/D_1)^2$).
> - Using $\rho_{\text{Hg}}$ instead of $(\rho_{\text{Hg}} - \rho_{\text{water}})$ in the manometer equation — this is a **favourite KTU trap**.
> - Confusing whether $h$ is **mercury deflection** or **equivalent head of water**.
> - Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$ when the question specifies SI units — always follow the value given in the problem.
> - **Forgetting the discharge coefficient** $C_d$ — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this in 7-mark problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Liquid Flow Sensors:**

- ✅ **Two fundamental categories:** Volumetric flow ($Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) and Mass flow ($\dot{m}$ in $\text{kg/s}$).
- ✅ **Bernoulli + Continuity = foundation** of all obstruction-type flow meters.
- ✅ **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** is the single most important correction factor — know its typical range for orifice vs. venturi.
- ✅ **Turbine meter output is a frequency** $f = K Q$ — clean linear relationship, ideal for digital interfacing.
- ✅ **Electromagnetic meter requires conductive liquid** — based on Faraday's Law $E = B L v$.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic transit-time uses time difference $\Delta t$** — fully non-invasive, suitable for sterile applications.
- ✅ **Coriolis gives true mass flow** — highest accuracy, highest cost, used in batching applications.
- ✅ **Vortex shedding:** $f_v = \text{St} \cdot v/D$, with $\text{St} \approx 0.2$ for a wide range of Re.
- ✅ **Pitot tube:** simplest equation $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ — high-yield for 3-mark questions.
- ✅ Always include **units and Reynolds number checks** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Remember the **beta ratio** $\beta = D_2/D_1$ and the term $(1-\beta^4)$ in orifice/venturi derivations.
- ✅ **Mechatronic integration point:** signal conditioning (amplification, filtering, ADC) bridges the sensor output to the microcontroller — this is the **"mechatronic" essence** of the topic.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BCT EU VR":**
> **B**ernoulli-based (Orifice, Venturi, Pitot) | **C**oriolis (mass) | **T**urbine (frequency) |
> **E**lectromagnetic (Faraday) | **U**ltrasonic (time-of-flight) | **V**ortex (Strouhal) | **R**otameter (variable area).
