# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydraulic Actuation System — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulic Actuation System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A power transmission system that uses a pressurized incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil with a kinematic viscosity of **32 cSt to 68 cSt at 40 °C**) as the working medium to generate, control, and transmit mechanical power through the principle enunciated by **Pascal's Law (1663)**.

**Hydraulic Energy Conversion Chain:**

$$ \text{Electrical / Mechanical Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pump}}\; \text{Hydraulic Pressure Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Valve}}\; \text{Controlled Flow} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Actuator}}\; \text{Mechanical Work} $$

**Working Pressure Classification (KTU Industrial Standard):**

| Class | Pressure Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Low Pressure | 0 – 70 bar | Mobile hydraulics, vehicle brakes |
| Medium Pressure | 70 – 200 bar | Machine tools, injection moulding |
| High Pressure | 200 – 700 bar | Aerospace, heavy forging presses |

---

## 1.2 Pneumatic Actuation System — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic Actuation System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A power transmission system employing **compressible atmospheric air** (compressed to typical working pressures of **4 bar to 10 bar** in industrial practice) as the energy carrier to produce linear or rotary mechanical motion via cylinders or motors.

**Standard ISO 5599 Reference Pressure:** **6 bar (≈ 0.6 MPa)**

**Pneumatic Energy Conversion Chain:**

$$ \text{Electrical Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Compressor}}\; \text{Pneumatic Pressure Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{FRL + Valves}}\; \text{Regulated Air} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Cylinder}}\; \text{Mechanical Work} $$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Balloon vs. Air Balloon" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Understanding for KTU Students**

**Hydraulic System = A Squeezed Water-Filled Balloon (Incompressible)**

Imagine a completely water-filled balloon with a small nozzle. When you squeeze it, **every bit of pressure you apply instantly transfers to the nozzle** — and the water shoots out with tremendous, controlled force. Water barely compresses, so the response is **fast, stiff, and powerful**. This is exactly how a hydraulic cylinder works: a pump squeezes oil through closed lines into a piston, and the piston pushes with predictable, high force.

**Pneumatic System = A Squeezed Air-Filled Balloon (Compressible)**

Now imagine a balloon filled with air. When you squeeze it, the air **compresses first** before producing motion. There is a noticeable delay (a "springiness") before force reaches the outlet. The output is **softer, springier, and less precise**, but air can travel long distances through pipes cheaply. This is the pneumatic cylinder: a compressor squeezes air, and the springy, compressible air pushes the piston.

**The Single Most Important Difference:**

$$ \underbrace{\text{Hydraulic}}_{\text{Bulk Modulus } K \;\approx\; 1.7\;\text{GPa (effectively incompressible)}} \quad\text{vs.}\quad \underbrace{\text{Pneumatic}}_{\text{Bulk Modulus } K \;\approx\; 0.14\;\text{MPa (highly compressible)}} $$

This **≈ 12 000 ×** difference in compressibility explains **why hydraulics are precise and strong, while pneumatics are cheap, clean, and intrinsically safe.**

---

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**

- **Pascal's Law (1653):** Foundation of all fluid power systems.
- **Joseph Bramah (1795):** Patented the first hydraulic press in London.
- **William Armstrong (1848):** Built the first industrial hydraulic accumulator at a shipyard in Newcastle.
- **Modern adoption:** Hydraulic presses today reach up to **80 000 tonnes** (forging aircraft wing spars).
- **Pneumatic origin:** The word *pneumatic* derives from the Greek *pneuma* meaning "breath / wind."

---

## 1.5 System Boundary Block — Where Fluid Power Fits in Mechatronics

In a mechatronic system, the fluid power stage sits between the **controller (electronics)** and the **mechanical load (output)**. The full mechatronic feedback loop is:

$$ \text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Driver / Servo Amplifier} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Hydraulic / Pneumatic Actuator}}_{\text{This Module}} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Mechanical Load} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sensor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Controller} $$

This makes fluid power the **"muscle"** of any mechatronic product — from robotic arms in automotive assembly lines to aircraft flight-control surfaces.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Physics — Pascal's Law

**Statement:** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted **equally and undiminished** to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$ P \;=\; \frac{F}{A} \quad \bigl[\text{Pa} \;=\; \text{N/m}^{2}\bigr] $$

**Why it matters in mechatronics:** This is the principle that lets a tiny input force at a small piston generate a huge output force at a large piston — the **force-multiplying effect** of a hydraulic press.

---

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Hydraulic / Pneumatic Equations

### A. Cylinder Force Equation (Force Generation)

For a double-acting cylinder with piston bore area $A$ subjected to inlet pressure $P$ (and back-pressure $P_b$ on the rod side):

$$ F_{\text{out}} \;=\; P \cdot A_{\text{bore}} \;-\; P_b \cdot A_{\text{rod\,side}} $$

For a single-acting (spring-returned) cylinder:

$$ F_{\text{out}} \;=\; P \cdot A_{\text{bore}} \;-\; F_{\text{spring}} $$

### B. Cylinder Velocity Equation (Speed Generation)

Flow rate $Q$ entering the cylinder fills a volume $V \;=\; A \cdot x$ in time $t$:

$$ v_{\text{piston}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \quad \bigl[\text{m/s}\bigr] $$

### C. Mechanical Power Delivered by the Actuator

$$ P_{\text{mech}} \;=\; F \cdot v \;=\; P \cdot Q \quad [\text{Watts}] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important "trinity"** in KTU fluid-power questions:
> **Pressure × Flow = Mechanical Power.**

---

## 2.3 Pump Flow and Torque Equations

For a positive-displacement pump with displacement $V_d$ [m³/rev] running at $N$ [rev/s]:

$$ Q_{\text{pump}} \;=\; V_d \cdot N \quad \bigl[\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}\bigr] $$

$$ T_{\text{pump}} \;=\; \frac{V_d \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi} \quad [\text{N·m}] $$

$$ P_{\text{pump,ideal}} \;=\; 2 \pi N T \;=\; P \cdot Q \quad [\text{W}] $$

The actual (input) power required is the ideal power divided by the overall efficiency $\eta_{\text{ov}}$:

$$ P_{\text{in}} \;=\; \frac{P \cdot Q}{\eta_{\text{ov}}} $$

---

## 2.4 Flow Through an Orifice / Valve

**Incompressible (Hydraulic) — Bernoulli + Discharge Coefficient:**

$$ Q \;=\; C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}} $$

**Compressible (Pneumatic, sub-sonic, $P_b/P_u > 0.528$):**

$$ Q \;=\; C_d \cdot A_v \cdot P_u \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{R T} \cdot \frac{\bigl(P_b/P_u\bigr)^{1.4286} \cdot \bigl[1 - (P_b/P_u)^{0.4286}\bigr]}{0.4286}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For **KTU board exams**, only the simplified hydraulic orifice equation is required. Always state $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.65$ for sharp-edged orifices and $C_d \approx 0.97$ for well-rounded nozzles.

---

## 2.5 Reynolds Number — Identifying the Flow Regime

$$ Re \;=\; \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{v \, D}{\nu} $$

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range |
|---|---|
| Laminar | $Re < 2300$ |
| Transitional | $2300 < Re < 4000$ |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ |

**Practical insight:** Most hydraulic circuits operate in the **turbulent regime** ($Re > 4000$) because of high pipe velocities (typically 2 – 6 m/s).

---

## 2.6 Frictional Pressure Drop in Pipes (Darcy–Weisbach)

$$ \Delta P_f \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2} $$

For laminar flow $f = 64 / Re$; for turbulent flow, $f$ is read from the Moody chart or computed from the Colebrook equation.

---

## 2.7 Air-Consumption Formula for a Pneumatic Cylinder

A single extension of a cylinder with bore $D$ and stroke $L$ draws (measured at atmospheric pressure) a free-air volume of:

$$ V_{\text{free\,air}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot L \cdot \frac{P_{\text{work}} + P_{\text{atm}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} \quad \bigl[\text{m}^{3}\bigr] $$

For $n$ cycles per minute, the compressor must supply:

$$ Q_{\text{compressor}} \;=\; V_{\text{free\,air}} \cdot n \quad \bigl[\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{min}\bigr] $$

---

## 2.8 KTU 2024 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical KTU Value |
|:--:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure | $P = F/A$ | Pa = N/m² | Hydraulic: 100–200 bar; Pneumatic: 6 bar |
| 2 | Cylinder Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | Hydraulic: 50–500 kN |
| 3 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = Q/A$ | m/s | Hydraulic: 0.1–0.5 m/s; Pneumatic: 1–3 m/s |
| 4 | Mechanical Power | $P = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q$ | W | — |
| 5 | Pump Flow Rate | $Q = V_d \cdot N$ | m³/s | — |
| 6 | Pump Torque | $T = V_d \cdot \Delta P / 2\pi$ | N·m | — |
| 7 | Orifice Flow (hyd.) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ | m³/s | $C_d \approx 0.62$ |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = vD/\nu$ | — | Laminar $< 2300 <$ Turbulent |
| 9 | Friction Loss | $\Delta P_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | — |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -\Delta P \,/\, (\Delta V/V)$ | Pa | Oil: 1.7 GPa; Air: 0.14 MPa |
| 11 | Compressor Work (Isothermal) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | J | — |
| 12 | Compressor Work (Adiabatic, $\gamma=1.4$) | $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \bigl[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\bigr]$ | J | — |
| 13 | Air Consumption | $V_{\text{free}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} L \cdot \frac{P_{\text{work}}+1}{1} \cdot n$ | m³/min | — |
| 14 | Cylinder Stroke Time | $t = A \cdot L / Q$ | s | — |
| 15 | Hydraulic Press Force Amp. | $F_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} \cdot A_2 / A_1$ | N | — |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters — KTU Application Vignette**

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft landing gear, flaps, and flight-control surfaces use **hydraulic actuators at 207 bar (3000 psi)**. The Airbus A380 has four independent hydraulic systems.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS) use hydraulic actuation for precise, fast-response braking.
- **Manufacturing:** CNC machine-tool slides use **hydraulic servo systems** for sub-micron positioning.
- **Packaging Industry:** Pick-and-place pneumatic cylinders operate at **6 bar** for high-speed (60+ cycles/min) assembly.
- **Food Industry:** Pneumatic systems dominate because **no oil contamination** is acceptable.
- **Robotics:** Modern humanoid robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics Atlas) use hydraulic actuators for their exceptional power-to-weight ratio.
- **Mining & Construction:** Excavators use hydraulic systems with operating pressures up to **350 bar**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force-Multiplying Hydraulic Press (Pascal's Law)

> **KTU Reference:** Derivation 1 is a high-probability 7-mark question in ESE Module-2.

**Setup:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of area $A_1$ and a large piston of area $A_2$, connected by a closed oil-filled chamber. A force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston; find the output force $F_2$ on the large piston.

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law.**
The pressure generated at piston 1 is:

$$ P_1 \;=\; \frac{F_1}{A_1} $$

**Step 2 — Equate pressures (Pascal's Law).**
Because the fluid is incompressible and the chamber is closed, this same pressure appears at piston 2:

$$ P_2 \;=\; P_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{F_2}{A_2} \;=\; \frac{F_1}{A_1} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the output force.**

$$ \boxed{\,F_2 \;=\; F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\,} $$

**Step 4 — Volume conservation (sanity check).**
The volume displaced by piston 1 equals the volume gained by piston 2:

$$ A_1 \cdot x_1 \;=\; A_2 \cdot x_2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_2 \;=\; x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} $$

This shows that while force is multiplied by the area ratio, **stroke is divided by the same ratio** — energy is conserved ($F_1 x_1 = F_2 x_2$ in the ideal case).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Force, Velocity & Power (from First Principles)

**Setup:** A double-acting cylinder of bore $D$ and rod diameter $d$ is supplied with oil at gauge pressure $P$ and flow rate $Q$. Derive the expressions for output force, piston velocity, and mechanical power.

**Step 1 — Compute piston areas.**

$$ A_{\text{bore}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \qquad A_{\text{rod\,side}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \bigl(D^{2} - d^{2}\bigr)}{4} $$

**Step 2 — Net force (extension direction).**
The pressure acts on the bore; the back-pressure acts on the rod side:

$$ F_{\text{ext}} \;=\; P \cdot A_{\text{bore}} \;-\; P_b \cdot A_{\text{rod\,side}} $$

If the return line is connected to tank ($P_b \approx 0$):

$$ F_{\text{ext}} \;\approx\; P \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} $$

**Step 3 — Piston velocity (extension).**
Oil flow $Q$ fills the bore-side volume $A_{\text{bore}} \cdot x$ in time $t$:

$$ v_{\text{ext}} \;=\; \frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A_{\text{bore}}} \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}} $$

**Step 4 — Mechanical power output.**

$$ P_{\text{mech}} \;=\; F \cdot v \;=\; \left(P \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}\right) \;=\; P \cdot Q \quad[\text{W}] $$

This is the elegant result: **ideal mechanical power of any fluid-power actuator equals pressure times flow rate.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pump Sizing (Displacement and Torque)

**Given:** Required flow $Q$ at system pressure $P$, pump speed $N$ in rpm.
**Find:** Pump displacement $V_d$ and required drive torque $T$.

**Step 1 — Pump displacement from flow rate.**
Convert $N$ to rev/s: $N_{\text{Hz}} = N/60$.

$$ V_d \;=\; \frac{Q}{N_{\text{Hz}}} \;=\; \frac{60 \, Q}{N} \quad \bigl[\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{rev}\bigr] $$

**Step 2 — Theoretical torque (force × radius, integrated).**
For a piston pump with $z$ pistons of area $a$ on a cam radius $R$:

$$ T_{\text{theory}} \;=\; \frac{V_d \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi} \quad [\text{N·m}] $$

**Step 3 — Drive power (accounting for efficiency).**

$$ P_{\text{drive}} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi \, N_{\text{Hz}} \, T}{\eta_m} \;=\; \frac{P \cdot Q}{\eta_{\text{ov}}} $$

where $\eta_m$ is the mechanical efficiency of the pump.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compressor Work (Adiabatic, for Pneumatic Systems)

**Given:** Atmospheric intake of volume $V_1$ at $P_1$ is compressed to $P_2$.
**Find:** Minimum work required per cycle (adiabatic, $\gamma = 1.4$).

**Step 1 — Adiabatic relation between states.**

$$ P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \;=\; P_2 V_2^{\gamma} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_2 \;=\; V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/\gamma} $$

**Step 2 — Work done on the gas (from $P\,dV$ integral).**

$$ W \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V^{-\gamma} \, dV $$

$$ W \;=\; P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[\frac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \;=\; \frac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left(V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V_2$ and simplify.**

$$ W \;=\; \frac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left[V_1^{1-\gamma}\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}\right] $$

$$ W \;=\; \frac{P_1 V_1}{1-\gamma} \left[\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} - 1\right] $$

**Step 4 — Use $(1-\gamma)/\gamma = -1/(\gamma-1)$.**

$$ W \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] \quad [\text{Joules}] $$

**Step 5 — Numerical form for air ($\gamma = 1.4$).**

$$ W \;=\; 3.5 \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2857} - 1\right] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hint:** The isothermal case ($\gamma = 1$) gives the *theoretical minimum* work $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$. The adiabatic case (above) gives the actual work delivered by a real compressor and is the one examiners prefer.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style, 14-mark pattern)

> **Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm and rod diameter $d = 45$ mm. It is supplied with oil at $P = 100$ bar during extension. The load is $F_L = 30$ kN. The pump flow is $Q = 40$ L/min. Calculate:
> (a) the extension force available and whether it is sufficient to lift the load,
> (b) the extension time for a stroke of $L = 500$ mm,
> (c) the mechanical power delivered during extension.

**Given data (SI units):**

$$ D = 0.080\;\text{m}, \quad d = 0.045\;\text{m}, \quad P = 100 \times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa}, $$

$$ Q = 40 \;\text{L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}, \quad L = 0.500\;\text{m} $$

**Part (a) — Available extension force:**

$$ A_{\text{bore}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2} $$

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = P \cdot A_{\text{bore}} = (100 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = 50\,265\;\text{N} \approx 50.27\;\text{kN} $$

**Comparison with load:**

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = 50.27\;\text{kN} \;>\; F_L = 30\;\text{kN} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Load CAN be lifted.} $$

**Part (b) — Extension time:**

$$ t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{A_{\text{bore}} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.500)}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{2.513 \times 10^{-3}}{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.77\;\text{s} $$

**Part (c) — Mechanical power delivered:**

$$ P_{\text{mech}} = P \cdot Q = (100 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (6.667 \times 10^{-4}) $$

$$ P_{\text{mech}} = 6\,667\;\text{W} \approx 6.67\;\text{kW} $$

**Final Answer Summary:**

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = 50.27\;\text{kN},\quad t_{\text{ext}} = 3.77\;\text{s},\quad P_{\text{mech}} = 6.67\;\text{kW} $$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Component Roles

| Component | Function | Symbolic Model | Energy Form Handled |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pump | Converts mechanical → hydraulic | $Q = V_d N$ | Mechanical → Pressure |
| Reservoir | Stores fluid, dissipates heat | Volume buffer | — |
| Pressure Relief Valve | Limits max pressure | Opens at $P_{\text{set}}$ | Pressure → Heat |
| Direction-Control Valve | Routes flow path | 4/2, 4/3, 5/2, 5/3 | — |
| Flow-Control Valve | Sets flow rate (speed) | Orifice: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ | Pressure → Flow |
| Check Valve | One-way flow | Diode analogue | — |
| Cylinder | Hydraulic → linear motion | $F = P A$, $v = Q/A$ | Pressure → Mechanical |
| Motor | Hydraulic → rotary motion | $T = V_d \Delta P / 2\pi$ | Pressure → Mechanical |
| Accumulator | Stores pressure energy | $E = \Delta P \cdot V$ | Stores pressure |
| Filter | Removes contaminants | — | — |
| FRL (Pneumatic) | Filter-Regulator-Lubricator | Pressure regulation | — |
| Servo Valve | Proportional flow control | $Q \propto i$ (input current) | Electrical → Hydraulic |

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC[Energy Converters]
        P[A Pump]
        M[B Motor]
        C[C Cylinder]
        RM[D Air Motor]
    end
    subgraph CTL[Control Elements]
        PRV2[E Pressure Relief]
        DCV3[F Direction Valve]
        FCV2[G Flow Control]
        CHK[H Check Valve]
        SV[I Servo Valve]
    end
    subgraph AUX[Auxiliary Elements]
        ACC[J Accumulator]
        RES[K Reservoir]
        FLT[L Filter]
        HX[M Heat Exchanger]
        FRL2[N FRL Unit]
    end
    P --> CTL
    CTL --> ENC
    ENC --> AUX
    AUX --> P
```

## 4.5 Signal-Flow Schematic of an Electro-Hydraulic Servo Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Command Voltage] --> AMP[Servo Amplifier]
    AMP --> SV2[Electro Hydraulic Servo Valve]
    SV2 --> CYL3[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL3 --> LD3[Load Position]
    LD3 --> LVDT[LVDT Position Sensor]
    LVDT --> FB[Feedback Signal]
    FB --> SUM[Sum Junction]
    SUM --> AMP
    CMD --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block diagram above is the canonical **electro-hydraulic servo loop** tested in KTU Module-2 questions. The "muscle" of the system is the cylinder, while the "brain" is the controller summing command and feedback. The "spinal cord" is the servo valve — it converts a small electrical current into a precisely modulated hydraulic flow.

## 4.6 Comparative Schematic Symbols (ISO 1219 Reference)

| Symbol (textual description) | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Two parallel lines (pump) | Hydraulic pump | Air compressor |
| Triangle pointing to circle (motor) | Hydraulic motor | Air motor |
| Rectangle with rod (cylinder) | Hydraulic cylinder | Pneumatic cylinder |
| Square with arrows (DCV) | 4/3 or 5/3 DCV | 5/2 DCV (spring return) |
| Spring (zig-zag) | Spring return | Spring return |
| Filter (diamond with line) | Hydraulic filter | FRL filter element |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — (3 marks)

**Q: State Pascal's Law and explain its significance in hydraulic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pascal's Law (1653):** "Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid and acts perpendicular to the walls of the container."

**Significance:**
1. It forms the theoretical basis of **all hydraulic power-transmission systems**.
2. It enables a small input force $F_1$ on a small piston to produce a much larger output force $F_2$ on a larger piston, where the force ratio equals the area ratio: $F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1$.
3. It is the reason hydraulic systems can lift extremely heavy loads (e.g., hydraulic jacks, cranes, presses) using modest input effort.
4. It also implies that pressure can be transmitted instantaneously over long pipe runs, enabling **remote actuation**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — (3 marks)

**Q: Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuation systems on the basis of (i) working medium, (ii) compressibility, and (iii) typical operating pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Working medium | Mineral oil (incompressible) | Atmospheric air (compressible) |
| (ii) Compressibility | Negligible — bulk modulus ≈ 1.7 GPa | High — bulk modulus ≈ 0.14 MPa |
| (iii) Operating pressure | 50 – 700 bar (typically 100–200 bar) | 4 – 10 bar (typically 6 bar) |

**Conclusion:** Hydraulics deliver high force and precise positioning; pneumatics deliver clean, cheap, low-force motion at high speed.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Fluid Power Derivations**
> - Do **not** confuse bore area $A_{\text{bore}}$ with annular (rod-side) area $A_{\text{annulus}}$ — for **extension** use $A_{\text{bore}}$, for **retraction** use $A_{\text{annulus}} = A_{\text{bore}} - A_{\text{rod}}$.
> - Always convert **bar to Pa** ($1\;\text{bar} = 10^{5}\;\text{Pa}$) and **L/min to m³/s** (divide by $60\,000$) — **one in three students loses a mark here.**
> - In the $P \cdot Q$ power formula, $P$ is **gauge** pressure, not absolute.
> - When asked "find the time," show $t = A L / Q$ with units clearly substituted — partial marks depend on the unit step.

---

### Question B1 (Choice A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — (14 marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **hydraulic power transmission system**. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100\;\text{mm}$, rod diameter $d = 60\;\text{mm}$, and stroke $L = 600\;\text{mm}$. It is supplied with oil at a pressure of $120\;\text{bar}$ during extension and $80\;\text{bar}$ during retraction. The pump flow is $50\;\text{L/min}$. Determine: **(i)** the extension force, **(ii)** the retraction force, **(iii)** the extension time, and **(iv)** the power developed during extension. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B1 (a)

A **hydraulic power transmission system** converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy, transmits it through a fluid medium, and reconverts it into mechanical work at the actuator.

**Block Diagram (functional):**

$$ \text{Prime Mover} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hydraulic Pump} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Control Valves} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hydraulic Actuator (Cylinder / Motor)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Load} $$

**Main Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prime Mover** (electric motor / IC engine) | Provides mechanical input power to the pump. |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Pump** | Converts mechanical energy into hydraulic pressure energy. |
| 3 | **Reservoir** | Holds the fluid, allows heat dissipation, and separates air. |
| 4 | **Filters** | Remove contaminants to protect valves and actuators. |
| 5 | **Pressure Relief Valve** | Limits maximum system pressure for safety. |
| 6 | **Direction-Control Valve (DCV)** | Routes fluid to extend / retract / hold the cylinder. |
| 7 | **Flow-Control Valve** | Regulates flow rate, hence piston speed. |
| 8 | **Check Valve** | Allows one-way flow; acts like a hydraulic diode. |
| 9 | **Hydraulic Cylinder / Motor** | Converts hydraulic energy back into mechanical work. |
| 10 | **Pressure Gauge / Sensor** | Monitors system pressure for feedback / diagnostics. |
| 11 | **Pipes & Hoses** | Transmit pressurized fluid between components. |

**Working Principle:** When the prime mover drives the pump, oil is drawn from the reservoir and pressurised. The DCV directs pressurised oil to the cap end (or rod end) of the cylinder, producing linear motion. The flow-control valve sets the speed; the relief valve caps the maximum pressure. The return oil returns to the tank.

---

#### Model Solution to B1 (b)

**Given:**

$$ D = 0.100\;\text{m}, \quad d = 0.060\;\text{m}, \quad L = 0.600\;\text{m} $$

$$ P_{\text{ext}} = 120\;\text{bar} = 120 \times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa}, \quad P_{\text{ret}} = 80\;\text{bar} = 80 \times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa} $$

$$ Q = 50\;\text{L/min} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s} $$

**Compute the two piston areas:**

$$ A_{\text{bore}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2} $$

$$ A_{\text{annulus}} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0100 - 0.0036)}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2} $$

**(i) Extension force:**
The pressure acts on the bore; the rod side is at tank pressure ($\approx 0$).

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = P_{\text{ext}} \cdot A_{\text{bore}} = (120 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ F_{\text{ext}} = 94\,248\;\text{N} \approx \boxed{94.25\;\text{kN}} $$

**(ii) Retraction force:**
The pressure acts on the annular area.

$$ F_{\text{ret}} = P_{\text{ret}} \cdot A_{\text{annulus}} = (80 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ F_{\text{ret}} = 40\,212\;\text{N} \approx \boxed{40.21\;\text{kN}} $$

**(iii) Extension time:**

$$ t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{A_{\text{bore}} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.600)}{8.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.65\;\text{s} $$

$$ t_{\text{ext}} \approx \boxed{5.65\;\text{s}} $$

**(iv) Power developed during extension:**

$$ P_{\text{mech}} = P_{\text{ext}} \cdot Q = (120 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (8.333 \times 10^{-4}) $$

$$ P_{\text{mech}} = 10\,000\;\text{W} = \boxed{10\;\text{kW}} $$

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — (14 marks)

**(a)** Explain the working of a **pneumatic control system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of the **FRL unit** and the **5/2 DCV** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has a bore of $75\;\text{mm}$ and a stroke of $300\;\text{mm}$. The supply pressure is $6\;\text{bar}$ (gauge) and atmospheric pressure is $1\;\text{bar}$. If the cylinder operates at $30$ cycles per minute, calculate: **(i)** the free-air consumption per minute, **(ii)** the thrust force during extension, and **(iii)** the time for one extension stroke if the supply flow rate is $Q = 1000\;\text{L/min}$ (free air). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B2 (a)

A **pneumatic control system** uses compressed air as the working medium. The standard flow path is:

$$ \text{Atmosphere} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Compressor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Receiver Tank} \;\rightarrow\; \text{FRL Unit} \;\rightarrow\; \text{DCV} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Cylinder} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exhaust} $$

**FRL Unit (Filter–Regulator–Lubricator):**

1. **Filter:** Removes dust, moisture, and oil aerosols from the compressed air to protect downstream components.
2. **Regulator:** Maintains a constant downstream pressure (typically **6 bar**), independent of upstream fluctuations, ensuring repeatable cylinder force.
3. **Lubricator:** Atomises a fine oil mist into the air stream to lubricate moving parts of valves and cylinders (though many modern systems are oil-free).

**5/2 DCV (Five-Port, Two-Position Direction-Control Valve):**

- Has **5 ports** — 1 pressure inlet (P), 2 outlet ports (A & B) to drive the cylinder in either direction, and 2 exhaust ports (R & S).
- Has **2 positions** — the spring-offset position sends air to one side of the cylinder; the solenoid-actuated position reverses flow.
- When the solenoid is energised, port P is connected to A and port B to S — cylinder extends; when de-energised (spring offset), P is connected to B and A to R — cylinder retracts. This makes the 5/2 DCV the standard "double-acting cylinder" valve in industry.

**Working:** Compressed air is cleaned, regulated and lubricated by the FRL. The 5/2 DCV routes the air to the cap or rod end of the cylinder, producing reciprocating linear motion. Exhaust air is vented to atmosphere through silencers.

---

#### Model Solution to B2 (b)

**Given:**

$$ D = 0.075\;\text{m}, \quad L = 0.300\;\text{m}, \quad P_{\text{work}} = 6\;\text{bar (gauge)}, \quad P_{\text{atm}} = 1\;\text{bar} $$

$$ n = 30\;\text{cycles/min}, \quad Q = 1000\;\text{L/min (free air)} $$

**(i) Free-air consumption per minute:**

The bore volume per stroke is:

$$ V_{\text{bore}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi (0.075)^{2}}{4} \cdot 0.300 = 1.325 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3} $$

Free-air equivalent per stroke (multiplying by absolute-pressure ratio):

$$ V_{\text{free,stroke}} = V_{\text{bore}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{work}} + P_{\text{atm}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} = 1.325 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{6 + 1}{1} $$

$$ V_{\text{free,stroke}} = 9.276 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3} \approx 9.28\;\text{L} $$

For 30 cycles/min (one extension per cycle):

$$ \boxed{\,V_{\text{free,total}} = 9.276 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 30 = 0.278\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{min} \approx 278\;\text{L/min}\,} $$

**(ii) Thrust force during extension:**

$$ A_{\text{bore}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^{2}}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2} $$

$$ F_{\text{thrust}} = P_{\text{work}} \cdot A_{\text{bore}} = (6 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (4.418 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ \boxed{\,F_{\text{thrust}} = 2651\;\text{N} \approx 2.65\;\text{kN}\,} $$

**(iii) Extension time:**

$$ t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{V_{\text{bore}}}{Q_{\text{free, actual}}} $$

The actual flow delivered at working pressure is $Q_{\text{work}} = Q \cdot P_{\text{atm}}/(P_{\text{work}}+P_{\text{atm}})$:

$$ Q_{\text{work}} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \cdot \frac{1}{7} = 2.381 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s} $$

$$ t_{\text{ext}} = \frac{1.325 \times 10^{-3}}{2.381 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.557\;\text{s} $$

$$ \boxed{\,t_{\text{ext}} \approx 0.56\;\text{s}\,} $$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module-2 Revision Bullets**

- ✅ **Pascal's Law (1653):** Pressure in an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions — the foundation of hydraulics.
- ✅ **Cylinder Force:** $F = P \cdot A$ — use bore area for extension, annular area for retraction.
- ✅ **Cylinder Velocity:** $v = Q / A$.
- ✅ **Power Equation:** $P = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q$ — the most reused equation in this module.
- ✅ **Pump:** $Q = V_d N$; torque $T = V_d \Delta P / (2\pi)$.
- ✅ **Compressor work (adiabatic):** $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(P_2/P_1\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$.
- ✅ **Air consumption:** Free air = cylinder volume × $(P_{\text{work}}+P_{\text{atm}})/P_{\text{atm}}$.
- ✅ **Bulk modulus:** Oil ≈ 1.7 GPa (nearly incompressible) vs air ≈ 0.14 MPa (very compressible).
- ✅ **5/2 DCV:** Standard valve for a double-acting pneumatic cylinder.
- ✅ **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** — must be fitted on every pneumatic line.
- ✅ **Standard industrial pressures:** Hydraulic ≈ 100 – 200 bar; Pneumatic ≈ 6 bar.
- ✅ **Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic:** Hydraulics = high force, precise, oil-based; Pneumatics = clean, low force, cheap, air-based.
- ✅ **Common SI conversions:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; 1 L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s; 1 kgf/cm² ≈ 0.98 bar.
- ✅ **Always convert L/min to m³/s** in KTU numerical problems; the most common mark-losing mistake.
- ✅ **Direction of force:** In a double-acting cylinder, **extension force > retraction force** (same pressure, but bore area > annular area).
- ✅ **Speed of retraction > speed of extension** (for the same flow rate, the smaller annular area is filled faster).
- ✅ **Energy equation:** Force × displacement on piston 1 = Force × displacement on piston 2 in an ideal hydraulic press (energy is conserved; only force is amplified, at the cost of stroke).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical actuation system-I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical actuation system-I

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔌 1.1 What is an Electrical Actuation System?

An **Electrical Actuation System** is a mechatronic subsystem that converts **electrical energy** into **controlled mechanical energy** (rotary or linear motion) in order to perform a physical task such as moving a robotic arm, positioning a CNC tool, opening a valve, or driving a conveyor belt. In the KTU 2024 framework, the electrical actuator is treated as the *muscle* of the mechatronic system, while the controller (microcontroller/PLC) acts as the *brain* and the sensors act as the *nervous system*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEEET833 – Module 2):**
> *Electrical Actuation System – I* covers the construction, working principle, mathematical modelling, and selection criteria of **Direct Current (DC) Motors** and **Stepper Motors**, including their driver electronics, torque–speed characteristics, and closed-loop / open-loop control strategies.

---

## 🍽️ 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter

Imagine a restaurant waiter (the **electric actuator**) carrying plates of food (the **mechanical load**):

| Waiter Analogy | Electrical Actuator Equivalent | Function |
|----------------|-------------------------------|----------|
| Waiter's muscles | Armature windings & magnetic field | Generates force/torque |
| Brain saying "go" | Microcontroller / PWM signal | Provides command input |
| Weight of plate | Load torque ($T_L$) | Mechanical resistance |
| Speed of walking | Rotational speed $\omega$ (rad/s) | Output motion |
| Tiredness | Heat ($I^2R$ losses) | Power dissipation |

When the **voltage** is increased, the waiter walks **faster** (higher $\omega$). When **plates are heavier**, the waiter walks **slower** but pushes **harder** (lower $\omega$, higher $T$). This trade-off is the famous **torque–speed characteristic** that we will derive in Section 3.

---

## 🗂️ 1.3 Classification of Electrical Actuators (Focus of Module 2)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 concentrates on the **two most widely used** electrical actuators in industrial mechatronics:

### 1️⃣ **DC Motors (Brushed)**
- **DC Servo Motors** – high-performance, separately excited, used in robotics.
- **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Motors** – compact, low-cost, fixed field flux.
- **Shunt / Series / Compound DC Motors** – classical industrial drives.

**Key sub-topics:**
- Construction (Stator, Rotor/ Armature, Commutator, Brushes)
- Working principle (Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor)
- Back-EMF and its role in self-regulation
- Armature reaction and commutation
- Torque–speed characteristic equation
- Speed control methods (armature voltage, field flux, Ward–Leonard)
- Starting methods (3-point starter, 4-point starter)
- Power flow, efficiency, and braking (dynamic, regenerative, plugging)

### 2️⃣ **Stepper Motors**
- **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor**
- **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor**
- **Hybrid (HB) Stepper Motor** – industry standard

**Key sub-topics:**
- Construction (stator poles, rotor teeth, windings)
- Step angle and resolution
- Full-step, half-step, microstepping excitation
- Holding torque vs. detent torque vs. pull-out torque
- Open-loop positioning (no encoder needed)
- Driver ICs (L293D, DRV8825, A4988)
- Single-coil vs. dual-coil excitation
- Open-loop vs. closed-loop stepper (with encoder)

---

## 🎯 1.4 Why Electrical Actuators Dominate Mechatronics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three killer advantages** that make electrical actuators the default choice:
> 1. **Controllability** – speed and position are easily varied by voltage/current.
> 2. **Cleanliness** – no exhaust, no oil leaks (unlike hydraulic/pneumatic).
> 3. **Precision** – when coupled with encoders/resolvers, sub-degree accuracy is achievable.
>
> The trade-off is **lower power density** compared to hydraulics, which is why heavy industry still uses hydraulics for excavators and aircraft control surfaces.

---

## 📐 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $k_e$ | Back-EMF constant | **V·s/rad** (SI) |
| $k_t$ | Torque constant | **N·m/A** (SI) |
| $k_e = k_t$ | Identity in SI units | **Numerically equal** |
| $J$ | Rotor moment of inertia | **kg·m²** |
| $B$ | Viscous friction coefficient | **N·m·s/rad** |
| $R_a$ | Armature resistance | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** |
| $L_a$ | Armature inductance | **Henries (H)** |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| $T$ | Torque | **N·m** |
| $\theta$ | Angular position | **radians (rad)** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ⚙️ Brushed DC Motor — Operational Breakdown

### Step 1: Magnetic Field Generation
A **stator field** (either from a permanent magnet in PMDC, or from a field winding in wound-field DC motor) creates a **stationary magnetic flux** $\phi$ in the air gap. The flux lines travel from **North → South** across the air gap.

### Step 2: Lorentz Force on Armature Conductors
The **armature** (rotor) carries current $I_a$ through conductors placed in slots. Each conductor of effective length $l$ experiences the **Lorentz force**:

$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot l$$

This force is **tangential** to the rotor surface, producing a **torque** about the shaft axis. The collective torque of all $Z$ armature conductors is the developed electromagnetic torque $T_e$.

### Step 3: Commutation — The "Mechanical Inverter"
The **commutator** (a split-ring of copper segments) reverses the current direction in each armature coil at the precise moment the coil crosses the magnetic neutral axis. This reversal ensures that the **torque is always unidirectional**, producing continuous rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why brushes?** Carbon brushes ride on the commutator, providing sliding electrical contact. They wear out — this is the biggest maintenance drawback of brushed DC motors.

### Step 4: Back-EMF — The Self-Regulating Phenomenon
As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it **cuts the same flux** that produced the torque. By Faraday's law, this induces an EMF that **opposes** the applied voltage (Lenz's law). This is the **back-EMF**, $E_b$:

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

The net voltage available to push current through the armature resistance is now $V - E_b$. The motor automatically **speeds up until** $E_b \approx V$, at which point current $I_a$ becomes small and the motor runs at **no-load speed**.

### Step 5: Torque–Speed Trade-off
When a mechanical load is applied, the motor **slows down slightly**, $E_b$ drops, $I_a$ rises, and the electromagnetic torque $T_e$ rises to balance the load. This is the natural **self-regulating feedback** that gives DC motors their famous linear torque–speed characteristic.

---

## 2.2 🎯 Stepper Motor — Operational Breakdown

### Step 1: Discrete Angular Movement
A stepper motor rotates in **discrete angular increments** called **steps**. Each electrical pulse applied to the stator windings causes the rotor to advance by one **step angle**:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$$

Where $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth and $m$ = number of stator phases (typically 2 for HB motors).

### Step 2: Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper
- **Soft-iron toothed rotor** (no permanent magnet).
- Rotor aligns with the **path of minimum magnetic reluctance** (max flux linkage).
- Each energised stator phase "pulls" the nearest rotor teeth into alignment.
- Step angles: typically **5°, 7.5°, 15°** in discrete form.
- **No detent torque** when unenergised.

### Step 3: Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper
- Rotor is a **permanent magnet** (axial or radial).
- Stator phases pull/push the magnet poles.
- Smaller step angles possible (**3.75°, 1.8°**).
- **Strong detent torque** even when unenergised.
- Limited resolution compared to HB.

### Step 4: Hybrid (HB) Stepper — Industrial Favourite
- Combines **toothed rotor** + **permanent magnet rotor cap**.
- Step angles: typically **0.9°, 1.8°** (200 / 400 steps per revolution).
- Highest torque-to-inertia ratio.
- Used in **3D printers, CNC routers, camera gimbals, syringe pumps**.

### Step 5: Excitation Modes
| Mode | Description | Torque | Power |
|------|-------------|--------|-------|
| **Wave (1-phase ON)** | One phase energised at a time | Low | Low |
| **Full-step (2-phase ON)** | Two phases always ON | High | Medium |
| **Half-step** | Alternating 1-phase / 2-phase ON | Medium | Medium |
| **Microstepping** | Sine-cosine current profile in each phase | Smooth | Tunable |

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 2 (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are mathematically verifiable and tested in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$ | Back-EMF (machines) — flux, speed, conductors | **V** |
| 2 | $E_b = k_e \, \phi \, \omega$ | Back-EMF in SI form | **V** |
| 3 | $T_e = k_t \, \phi \, I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | **N·m** |
| 4 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt}$ | Armature voltage equation (KVL) | **V** |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e \phi}$ | Speed equation (DC motor) | **rpm** |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output power}}{\text{Input power}} = \dfrac{T \omega}{V I_a}$ | Efficiency of DC motor | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Stepper motor step angle | **degrees** |
| 8 | $f_{pulse} = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} \cdot N_r \cdot m$ | Pulse frequency for desired stepper speed | **Hz** |
| 9 | $T_{hold} = k_t \cdot I_{phase}$ | Holding torque (energised, locked rotor) | **N·m** |
| 10 | $T_{pullout} = \dfrac{T_{hold}}{1 + \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{max}}}$ | Pull-out torque envelope (approx.) | **N·m** |
| 11 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | **W** |
| 12 | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Angular velocity definition | **rad/s** |
| 13 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time constant of armature | **s** |
| 14 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{k_t k_e}$ | Mechanical time constant | **s** |
| 15 | $\dfrac{V}{k_e \phi} = N_0$ | No-load speed | **rpm** |
| 16 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t \phi V}{R_a}$ | Stall torque (at $\omega = 0$) | **N·m** |

> **Note on identity:** In the SI system, $k_e$ (in V·s/rad) and $k_t$ (in N·m/A) are **numerically equal**. In CGS / mixed units, a conversion factor (e.g., 9.55 for rad/s → rpm) is needed.

---

## 2.4 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Actuator | Why? |
|-------------|-------------------|------|
| Robotic arm joints (medium payload) | DC servo motor + encoder | Precise position, high torque |
| CNC Z-axis lead screw | Stepper or servo | Open-loop stepper is cheaper |
| 3D printer X/Y axes | NEMA 17 Hybrid stepper | Open-loop, cheap, accurate |
| Conveyor belt (constant speed) | Shunt DC motor | Linear torque–speed curve |
| Electric vehicle traction | BLDC / PMSM (out of scope) | High efficiency, high power density |
| Camera gimbal stabilisation | Hybrid stepper (microstep) | Vibration-free, smooth |
| Syringe pump (medical) | Stepper + lead screw | Discrete, repeatable volumes |

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Back-EMF Equation of a DC Motor

### 🎯 Goal:
Derive the relationship $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$, the classical EMF equation used in every KTU exam.

### Given Information:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature
- $N$ = Speed of armature (rpm)

---

### Step 1: Flux cut by ONE conductor in ONE revolution
As the armature rotates **one full revolution**, each conductor sweeps through the magnetic field and cuts flux from **all $P$ poles** (north + south). The total flux cut per revolution is:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per rev} = P \cdot \phi \quad [\text{Webers}]$$

### Step 2: Number of revolutions per second
The armature rotates at $N$ rpm, so in **seconds**:

$$\text{Revolutions per second} = \frac{N}{60}$$

### Step 3: Flux cut per conductor per SECOND
Multiply revolutions/second by flux per revolution:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = P \phi \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{Wb/s}]$$

### Step 4: Apply Faraday's Law
By Faraday's law, the EMF induced in **one conductor** equals the rate of change of flux:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{P \phi N}{60} \quad [\text{V}]$$

### Step 5: Average EMF per parallel path
The $Z$ conductors are divided among $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in **series** in one path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

> **Justification for series conductors per path:** In a **lap winding**, $A = P$, so conductors per path $= Z/P$. In a **wave winding**, $A = 2$ regardless of $P$, so conductors per path $= Z/2$. In general, series conductors per path $= Z/A$.

So the average EMF generated across each parallel path (which is the armature terminal EMF $E_b$) is:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot e_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{P \phi N}{60}$$

### Step 6: Final Equation

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P \phi N Z}{60 A} \quad [\text{Volts}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation of a DC Motor

### 🎯 Goal:
Show that electromagnetic torque $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$.

### Given:
- Power developed in the armature = mechanical power = $E_b \cdot I_a$
- Mechanical power also = $T_e \cdot \omega$

### Step 1: Equate Electrical and Mechanical Power
Assuming **100% conversion** of electrical to mechanical power (no rotational losses yet):

$$E_b \cdot I_a = T_e \cdot \omega$$

### Step 2: Substitute $\omega$ in rad/s
The armature speed in rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $E_b$ from EMF equation

$$\frac{P \phi N Z}{60 A} \cdot I_a = T_e \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

### Step 4: Cancel $N/60$ on both sides

$$\frac{P \phi Z \cdot I_a}{A} = T_e \cdot 2\pi$$

### Step 5: Solve for $T_e$

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \phi Z I_a}{A}$$

### Step 6: Group constants and define $k_t$

$$T_e = \underbrace{\left(\frac{P Z}{2\pi A}\right)}_{k_t \, \text{(machine constant)}} \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$$

$$\boxed{T_e = k_t \, \phi \, I_a \quad [\text{N·m}]}$$

### 🔑 Key Insight:
- Torque is **directly proportional** to flux $\phi$ and armature current $I_a$.
- This is why **field weakening** (reducing $\phi$) reduces torque but allows higher speed — the basis of the constant-power region in DC motor drives.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque–Speed Characteristic Equation

### 🎯 Goal:
Derive the famous **linear** torque–speed line used to size DC motors.

### Step 1: Armature KVL (Steady State)
At steady state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$, so the inductance term vanishes:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### Step 2: Substitute $E_b = k_e \phi \omega$

$$V = k_e \phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

### Step 3: Solve for $I_a$

$$I_a = \frac{V - k_e \phi \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$

$$T_e = k_t \phi \cdot \frac{V - k_e \phi \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange into $\omega$ as function of $T_e$

$$T_e = \frac{k_t \phi V}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e \phi^2}{R_a} \omega$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V}{k_e \phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_t k_e \phi^2} \cdot T_e}$$

### Step 6: Identify key points on the line

- **No-load speed** (when $T_e = 0$): $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{k_e \phi}$
- **Stall torque** (when $\omega = 0$): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t \phi V}{R_a}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The slope $\dfrac{d\omega}{dT_e} = -\dfrac{R_a}{k_t k_e \phi^2}$ is **negative** — torque and speed are inversely related. This is the heart of every DC motor selection problem.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stepper Motor Step Angle (Hybrid Type)

### 🎯 Goal:
Derive $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$ for a hybrid stepper.

### Step 1: Understand Geometry
- The hybrid rotor has $N_r$ teeth on both ends, offset by **half a tooth pitch** to create a small axial flux.
- The stator has $m$ phases (usually 2), each phase having 2 poles = total 4 stator poles in a 2-phase motor.
- Each stator pole has teeth matching the rotor teeth.

### Step 2: Tooth Pitch
The mechanical tooth pitch on the rotor is:

$$P_t = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

### Step 3: Electrical Resolution
For a 2-phase motor ($m = 2$), each electrical step advances the rotor by **one tooth pitch divided by 2** (because of the half-tooth offset):

$$\theta_s = \frac{P_t}{2} = \frac{360°}{2 N_r} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

### Step 4: For a 50-tooth rotor, 2-phase motor:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

For a 200-tooth rotor (less common; typical HB has 50 teeth → 1.8° step):

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = 1.8°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Q:** A **220 V**, **4-pole**, **wave-wound** ($A = 2$) DC shunt motor has **500 armature conductors**, **flux per pole $\phi = 30$ mWb**, and **armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \Omega$**. The motor draws an armature current of **50 A** at rated load. Find:
(a) Back-EMF
(b) Developed torque
(c) Speed at rated load
(d) No-load speed (assuming constant $\phi$)

### Solution:

#### (a) Back-EMF $E_b$
Apply KVL:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (50)(0.3) = 220 - 15 = 205 \text{ V}$$

#### (b) Developed Torque $T_e$
Use $E_b = k_e \phi \omega$ → solve for $k_e \phi$ after finding $\omega$.

Speed calculation first:

Using EMF equation:

$$E_b = \frac{P \phi N Z}{60 A} \Rightarrow N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \phi Z}$$

$$N = \frac{205 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times (30 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500} = \frac{24600}{60} = 410 \text{ rpm}$$

Now angular speed:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 410}{60} \approx 42.94 \text{ rad/s}$$

Find $k_t \phi$ via power balance:

$$T_e = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{205 \times 50}{42.94} \approx 238.7 \text{ N·m}$$

#### (c) Speed at rated load
Already computed: **$N = 410$ rpm**

#### (d) No-load speed
At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V = 220$ V.

$$N_0 = \frac{220 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.03 \times 500} = \frac{26400}{60} = 440 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **speed regulation** $= \dfrac{N_0 - N_{full}}{N_{full}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{440-410}{410} \times 100\% \approx 7.3\%$ — a good value for a DC shunt motor.

## 4.5 Torque–Speed Curve — Conceptual (Text Rendering)

Since Mermaid cannot plot Cartesian graphs, here is a **high-fidelity ASCII rendering** of the DC motor torque–speed line, which appears in every KTU 14-mark question:

```
    Speed (ω) rad/s
    ↑
    |*  ← No-load speed ω₀ = V/(kφ)
    |  *
    |    *    
    |      *  
    |        *
    |          *
    |            *
    |              *
    |                *
    |                  *
    |                    *
    |                      *
    |                        *
    |__________________________*_____→ Torque Tₑ
    0                          T_stall
```

The line is **straight** and has a **negative slope**, intersecting the $\omega$-axis at $\omega_0$ and the $T$-axis at $T_{stall}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **shunt DC motor**, this line is **rigidly linear** because $\phi$ is constant. In a **series DC motor**, the line is a **hyperbola** (constant power), with infinite no-load speed — a critical KTU point.
