# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor. State any two advantages."**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):
A PMDC motor consists of a **stator** with high-energy permanent magnets (e.g., NdFeB or ferrite) that create a constant field flux $\phi$, and a **rotor/armature** wound with conductors connected to a **commutator** rotating between carbon brushes.

**Working principle:** When DC voltage $V$ is applied across the brushes, current $I_a$ flows through the armature conductors. These conductors experience a **Lorentz force** $F = BIl$ in the magnetic field, producing a **torque** $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$ that rotates the armature. The commutator reverses the current every half-turn to ensure **unidirectional rotation**.

**Two advantages:**
1. No field winding → simpler construction, no field copper losses → higher efficiency.
2. Smaller size and lighter weight for a given rating → ideal for portable mechatronic systems like robot joints and small drones.

*(Diagram: cross-section showing magnets on stator, slotted rotor with commutator — 1 mark, Working 1 mark, Advantages 1 mark.)*

---

### ❓ Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor and Hybrid (HB) stepper motor. State one application of each."**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | VR Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Rotor | Soft iron, toothed, no magnet | Toothed rotor with embedded permanent magnet |
| Detent torque | Zero (no detent) | Significant (permanent magnet) |
| Step angle | Larger (5°–15°) | Smaller (0.9°–1.8°) |
| Holding torque | Moderate | High (best torque-to-size ratio) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate |

- **VR Stepper application:** Inexpensive positioning tasks like document feeders, valve actuators.
- **Hybrid Stepper application:** 3D printers, CNC machines, robotic arm joints requiring fine resolution.

*(Tabular differentiation 2 marks, Applications 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE convention: For Module 2, students choose **ONE of TWO** 14-mark questions. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ❓ Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"Derive the EMF and torque equations of a DC motor. A 250 V, 6-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 1200 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.15 Ω and the field winding resistance is 250 Ω. The flux per pole is 25 mWb. The motor draws an armature current of 80 A. Calculate:
(a) The back-EMF, developed torque, and speed at this load.
(b) The no-load speed, the stall torque, and the starting current if started directly across the line."**

---

#### ✅ Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Back-EMF at rated load (1 mark)**
Apply KVL to armature:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (80)(0.15) = 250 - 12 = 238 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Speed at rated load (3 marks)**
Use EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$. For wave winding, $A = 2$.

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \phi Z} = \frac{238 \times 60 \times 2}{6 \times 0.025 \times 1200} = \frac{28560}{180} = 158.67 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3: Developed Torque (3 marks)**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 158.67}{60} \approx 16.62 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_e = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega} = \frac{238 \times 80}{16.62} \approx 1145.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Alternatively**, use $k_t = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A} = \dfrac{6 \times 1200}{2\pi \times 2} \approx 573.0$ N·m/(Wb·A)

$$T_e = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a = 573.0 \times 0.025 \times 80 = 1146 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final answers (a):** $E_b = 238$ V, $N = 158.7$ rpm, $T_e \approx 1146$ N·m ✅

---

#### ✅ Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: No-load speed (2 marks)**
At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$ (neglecting field current), so $E_b \approx V = 250$ V:

$$N_0 = \frac{250 \times 60 \times 2}{6 \times 0.025 \times 1200} = \frac{30000}{180} = 166.67 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Stall torque (2 marks)**
At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$, and $I_{a,stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a} = \dfrac{250}{0.15} = 1666.7$ A

$$T_{stall} = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_{a,stall} = 573.0 \times 0.025 \times 1666.7 \approx 23868 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Starting current (3 marks)**
A DC shunt motor started **directly online (DOL)** draws the **stall current** at standstill:

$$I_{start} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{250}{0.15} \approx 1667 \text{ A}$$

This is **~21 times** the rated current (80 A) — a destructive surge that explains why **3-point / 4-point starters** with current-limiting resistors are mandatory. The starter resistance reduces the initial voltage to ~$I_a R_a$ levels and is gradually cut out as $E_b$ builds up.

**Final answers (b):** $N_0 = 166.7$ rpm, $T_{stall} \approx 23868$ N·m, $I_{start} \approx 1667$ A ✅

---

### ❓ Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a stepper motor drive system. Discuss the four excitation modes: wave, full-step, half-step, and microstepping.**
**(b) A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and is driven by a 2-phase stator. Calculate the step angle, the number of steps per revolution, and the pulse frequency required to achieve a shaft speed of 600 rpm in full-step mode."**

---

#### ✅ Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working of stepper motor drive (block diagram in words — 3 marks):**

A microcontroller (e.g., Arduino) generates **step pulses** and a **direction signal**. The pulses are sent to a **translator IC** (e.g., DRV8825 or L297) that converts the step/dir logic into a proper commutation sequence (e.g., A+, A-, B+, B-). These low-power signals drive **power MOSFETs** in an H-bridge configuration that switch **high-current** to the two stepper phase windings. The phase currents, controlled via **PWM chopping**, generate a rotating magnetic field in the stator. The rotor (with permanent magnet + teeth) follows this field, advancing by one step per pulse.

**Four excitation modes (4 marks):**

| Mode | Phases ON | Step Multiplier | Torque | Smoothness |
|------|-----------|-----------------|--------|------------|
| **Wave (1-phase ON)** | One | 1× | Lowest | Jerky |
| **Full-step (2-phase ON)** | Two | 1× | Highest | Acceptable |
| **Half-step** | Alternating 1/2 | 0.5× | Medium | Better |
| **Microstepping** | Sinusoidal | 1/(N) | Tunable | Excellent (256 microsteps/step typical) |

- **Wave** – minimum power, only 1 winding energised at a time.
- **Full-step** – both windings always on, full holding torque, full step.
- **Half-step** – alternates 1 and 2 windings ON, doubles resolution.
- **Microstepping** – each phase driven with sinusoidal current, rotor stops at intermediate positions between full steps, eliminates resonance, gives ultra-smooth motion.

---

#### ✅ Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $N_r = 50$ teeth, $m = 2$ phases, $N = 600$ rpm target, full-step mode.

**Step 1: Step Angle (2 marks)**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 2} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

**Step 2: Steps per revolution (2 marks)**

$$\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 3: Pulse frequency for 600 rpm (3 marks)**
600 rpm = 10 revolutions per second.

$$\text{Pulses/second} = (\text{Steps/rev}) \times (\text{rev/s}) = 100 \times 10 = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{pulse} = 1 \text{ kHz}}$$

If microstepping (say 8×) is used, the required pulse frequency = $8 \times 1000 = 8$ kHz.

---

## ⚠️ 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on Module 2 questions:**

1. **Confusing $A$ (parallel paths) for a wave vs. lap winding:** Wave → $A = 2$ (always). Lap → $A = P$. Mixing these up gives wrong $E_b$ and wrong $N$ — losing **3–4 marks** in one shot.
2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** before computing $T_e$ from power balance.
3. **Not stating the SI unit identity** $k_e = k_t$ — KTU examiners allocate **½ to 1 mark** for correct unit identity.
4. **In stepper derivations**, missing the half-tooth pitch offset in hybrid motors.
5. **DOL starting calculation** — students forget that $E_b = 0$ at standstill, leading to $I_a = V/R_a$ which is the **stall current**, NOT the running current.
6. **No neat diagrams** in 14-mark questions → up to **2 marks deducted** under "presentation".

---

## 🧠 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Electrical Actuation System-I: Final Cheat Bullets**

### DC Motor Essentials
- ⚡ **EMF equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$  →  KTU's most-tested equation.
- ⚙️ **Torque equation:** $T_e = k_t \phi I_a$ → linear with current.
- 🔁 **Torque-speed line:** $\omega = \omega_0 - \dfrac{R_a}{(k\phi)^2} T_e$ → straight, negative slope.
- 🚀 **No-load speed:** $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{k_e \phi}$ → depends on V and $\phi$.
- 🛑 **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t \phi V}{R_a}$ → destructive if held.
- 🎚️ **Speed control methods:** Armature voltage control (constant torque region), Field flux control (constant power region), Ward-Leonard (high-performance).
- 🛡️ **Starters:** 3-point (no-field current zero protection), 4-point (any field current works).
- 🔋 **Power stages:** Input electrical ($V I_a$) → Core losses + Armature copper loss → Mechanical developed power ($E_b I_a$) → Brush loss + Friction & Windage → Output shaft power ($T_{sh} \omega$).

### Stepper Motor Essentials
- 🦷 **Step angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$
- 🔢 **Steps/rev = 200** for 1.8° (NEMA 17), **48** for 7.5°, **24** for 15°.
- 🎚️ **Excitation modes:** Wave (low torque) → Full-step (high torque) → Half-step (high res) → Microstep (smoothest).
- 🔒 **Holding torque** ≈ applied when energised, locked rotor.
- 🔓 **Detent torque** = residual torque from permanent magnet (HB only) when de-energised.
- ⏱️ **Pull-out torque** = max torque before losing synchronisation at a given speed.
- 💥 **Open-loop risk:** If load torque exceeds pull-out torque, the rotor **stalls and skips steps** — position is lost.
- 🔌 **Drivers:** L293D (low current), DRV8825 (2.5 A peak, microstepping), TMC2208 (silent microstepping).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- 🤝 **Back-EMF** is the **self-regulating** mechanism that gives DC motors their linear torque-speed curve.
- 🧠 **$k_e = k_t$** in SI — use this identity to simplify derivations.
- ⚖️ **Stepper = open-loop precision**, **Servo = closed-loop precision**.
- 💡 **Energy efficiency** of DC shunt motor at rated load is typically **85–90%**; of stepper in microstepping ≈ **30–60%** (due to constant holding current).

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Hints
- **Module 2 carries 15 marks** in the End Semester Exam (ESE) — usually **1 Part-A (3 marks) + 1 Part-B (12 marks)** split.
- Part-B always has an **internal choice** — practice both DC motor and Stepper motor long questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical actuation system-II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical actuation system-II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Stepper Motor — The Digital-To-Mechanical Converter

### Formal Definition
A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a discrete train of input electrical pulses (digital commands) into a precisely defined angular (or linear) mechanical increment called a **step**. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular displacement known as the **step angle (β)**, making it a true *open-loop* positioning device with no need for feedback sensors in most applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Stepper motors are the *heart of mechatronic positioning*. They bridge the digital microcontroller world (pulses) and the physical world (rotation/linear motion) — making them indispensable in CNC machines, 3D printers, robotic joints, and disk drives.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Escalator Stair
Imagine an **escalator**: each step brings you a fixed, predetermined distance forward — whether you want it or not. You don't need to "measure" how far you've gone because *every step is identical*. A stepper motor behaves the same way:
- **Each pulse = one "step"** (a fixed angular jump, e.g., 1.8°).
- **No feedback required** (open-loop positioning).
- **Total position = Number of pulses × Step angle**.

The catch? If the load is too heavy, the motor may *miss steps* silently — like trying to climb an escalator that's moving too fast. This is the critical limitation of stepper systems.

---

## 1.2 Servo Motor — The Closed-Loop Position Tracker

### Formal Definition
A **servo motor** is a rotary or linear actuator designed for **high-precision closed-loop position, velocity, or torque control**. Unlike a stepper motor, it operates with a continuous feedback loop (typically using an encoder, resolver, or potentiometer) that constantly compares the *commanded* position with the *actual* position and corrects the error in real time.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control in Your Car
Think of **cruise control**:
- You set the *desired speed* (setpoint).
- The system *measures* the actual speed (feedback).
- If the car slows down on a hill, the system *automatically* increases throttle until speed matches the setpoint.
- A **servo motor** does exactly this — but for **position** rather than speed, and *thousands of times per second*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU-Favorite Exam Point):**
> | Feature | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
> |---|---|---|
> | Control Mode | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
> | Feedback | ❌ Not required | ✅ Encoder/Resolver mandatory |
> | Holding Torque at Standstill | ✅ High (without current) | ⚠️ Requires continuous current |
> | Missed Steps? | ✅ Possible (silent failure) | ❌ Self-correcting |
> | Best For | Low-cost, moderate-speed positioning | High-speed, high-precision tracking |

---

## 1.3 Solenoid — The Electromagnetic Linear Pusher

### Formal Definition
A **solenoid** is a static electromagnetic actuator that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical force/motion via a magnetic field generated by a current-carrying coil, which attracts a movable ferromagnetic plunger.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Doorbell Hammer
When you press a doorbell button, a coil gets energized, pulls a metal hammer (plunger) forward, the hammer strikes the bell, and a spring pushes it back when you release. A **solenoid** works on exactly this principle — *electrical pulse → linear push/pull*.

---

## 1.4 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor — The Maintenance-Free Speed Demon

### Formal Definition
A **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by a DC supply (via an inverter) where the rotor is a permanent magnet and the stator carries the windings. Electronic commutation (via Hall sensors or sensorless algorithms) replaces mechanical brushes and commutators.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Compass in a Magnetic Field
Hold a compass near a bar magnet — the needle (rotor) aligns with the magnetic field. Now, *electronically flip* the field continuously → the compass spins! A BLDC motor does this with extreme precision using electronic switching instead of mechanical brushes.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions Recap (KTU Quick-Glance):**
> - **Step Angle (β):** The minimum angular displacement per pulse.
> - **Holding Torque:** Maximum torque the motor can hold while energized and stationary.
> - **Pull-out Torque:** Maximum load torque at which the motor can run without losing synchronism.
> - **Field-Controlled Servo:** Control signal applied to the field winding.
> - **Armature-Controlled Servo:** Control signal applied to the armature winding (preferred, linear).
> - **Solenoid Force:** $F \propto i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dx}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stepper Motor — Types and Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor

**Construction:** Soft-iron toothed rotor + concentrated stator windings. Rotor has *no* permanent magnet.

**Operating Principle:** The rotor always aligns itself to the path of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum permeance). When a phase is energized, the nearest rotor teeth align with the energized stator teeth.

**Step Angle Formula:**

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r \cdot \text{Ph}}$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = step angle (degrees)
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth per pole pair
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth
- $\text{Ph}$ = number of phases

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does VR work?** The rotor teeth physically snap to the position where the air-gap reluctance is lowest — like a ball rolling into the nearest valley. Each phase-switch moves the rotor to the next "valley."

---

### 2.1.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor

**Construction:** Rotor is a permanent magnet (axially or radially magnetized). Stator windings create magnetic poles that attract/repel the rotor poles.

**Step Angle:** Typically larger than VR (e.g., **45°**, 90°) because rotor pole count is low.

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{2 \cdot N_p}$$

Where $N_p$ = number of rotor pole pairs.

---

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Stepper Motor (KTU Most Asked)

**Construction:** Combines features of VR and PM — a *toothed* rotor made of axially magnetized permanent magnet pieces sandwiched between soft-iron caps. Best of both worlds: high resolution + high torque.

**Step Angle:** Very small (typically **1.8°**, 0.9°, 0.72°).

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

Where $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth (typically 50, 100, or 200).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU Numerical:** For a hybrid stepper with **200 rotor teeth**, step angle = $360°/200 = \mathbf{1.8°}$ → giving **200 steps/revolution**. This is the industry standard.

---

### 2.1.4 Stepper Motor Drive Modes (Sequencing)

| Drive Mode | Phases ON at a Time | Torque | Power Consumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave (1-phase ON)** | 1 | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Full-Step (2-phase ON)** | 2 | Highest (~40% more) | Higher |
| **Half-Step** | 1 or 2 | Intermediate | Intermediate |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Half-stepping doubles the resolution (β/2) by alternating between wave and full-step excitation patterns.

---

### 2.1.5 Static and Dynamic Characteristics

- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** Maximum torque the motor can hold without rotation when energized.
- **Detent Torque:** Residual torque when *no* phase is energized (zero in VR, present in PM and hybrid).
- **Pull-in Curve:** Maximum stepping rate at which the motor can *start/stop* without losing steps (limited by inertia).
- **Pull-out Curve:** Maximum torque the motor can drive at *a given slewing speed* without losing synchronism (system slew region).

> [!NOTE]
> **Slewing:** Operating continuously at high speed. **Pull-in (start-stop):** Operating below acceleration limits. Always: **Pull-out torque > Pull-in torque**.

---

## 2.2 Servo Motor — Field-Controlled vs. Armature-Controlled

### 2.2.1 Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor (KTU Favorite)

The control voltage $V_c$ is applied to the **armature** while the field is held constant (separately excited).

**Governing Equations:**

$$V_c = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

$$T_m = K_t I_a \quad \text{(motor torque)}$$

$$E_b = K_b \omega_m \quad \text{(back EMF)}$$

**Transfer Function (Position Output):**

For position control (servo = positional), the output is angular displacement $\theta_m(s)$:

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ L_a J_m s^2 + (R_a J_m + B_m L_a) s + (R_a B_m + K_b K_t) \right]}$$

**Approximation (neglecting armature inductance $L_a$ for typical KTU problems):**

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ R_a J_m s + (R_a B_m + K_b K_t) \right]}$$

Defining the **motor gain** $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t}$ and **motor time constant** $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_b K_t}$:

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

---

### 2.2.2 Field-Controlled DC Servo Motor

Control voltage applied to the **field winding** (armature current held constant).

$$T_m = K_f \cdot I_f \cdot I_a = K_f' \cdot V_c$$

When $I_a$ is held constant via a high-current source, torque becomes *directly proportional* to field voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Field-controlled servos are **NON-LINEAR** because of the square-law magnetization curve ($T \propto I_f^2$ at low currents due to saturation). **Armature-controlled servos are preferred** for linear, predictable response.

---

## 2.3 Solenoids — Force Equation

The electromagnetic force on a solenoid plunger is derived from energy conservation. The energy stored in the magnetic field of an inductor $L$ carrying current $i$ is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L i^2$$

The force is the negative gradient of co-energy with respect to displacement $x$:

$$F = \frac{\partial W}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i=\text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Force is proportional to **$i^2$** (not $i$) — meaning the solenoid **cannot reverse direction** by changing current polarity. The force is always attractive (toward minimum reluctance).

---

## 2.4 Brushless DC Motor (BLDC) — Key Equations

**Back EMF Constant ($K_e$)** and **Torque Constant ($K_t$)**: In SI units, $K_e = K_t$.

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

$$T = K_t \cdot I$$

**Speed Equation (for a 3-phase BLDC):**

$$\omega = \frac{V_s - 2 I_a R_a}{K_e}$$

Where $V_s$ is the DC bus voltage and the factor 2 represents the two phases conducting at any instant.

---

## 2.5 Linear Motors & Voice Coil Actuators

A **linear motor** "unrolls" a rotary motor into a straight line. The **voice coil actuator** (used in hard disk read/write heads) is a special case — it produces force proportional to current via Lorentz force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot N$$

Where $B$ = magnetic flux density, $L$ = coil length, $N$ = number of turns.

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Topic | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VR Stepper Step Angle | $\beta = 360°/(N_s \cdot N_r \cdot \text{Ph})$ | Step angle based on teeth |
| 2 | Hybrid Stepper Step Angle | $\beta = 360°/N_r$ | Rotor teeth determine resolution |
| 3 | PM Stepper Step Angle | $\beta = 180°/N_p$ | Based on rotor pole pairs |
| 4 | Steps per Revolution | $n = 360°/\beta$ | Total pulses for one full rotation |
| 5 | Shaft Position | $\theta = n \cdot \beta$ | Where $n$ = number of pulses |
| 6 | Solenoid Force | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL/dx$ | Proportional to $i^2$ |
| 7 | Armature Servo TF | $\theta_m/V_c = K_m / [s(\tau_m s+1)]$ | Approximate position transfer function |
| 8 | Motor Torque | $T_m = K_t I_a$ | Torque proportional to current |
| 9 | Back EMF | $E_b = K_b \omega_m$ | Opposes applied voltage |
| 10 | BLDC Speed | $\omega = (V_s - 2I_a R_a)/K_e$ | Steady-state speed |
| 11 | Voice Coil Force | $F = B I L N$ | Lorentz force on current-carrying coil |
| 12 | Stepper Resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \beta/360°$ | Fraction of revolution per step |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Stepper Motors:** 3D printers (Prusa, Creality), CNC routers, camera autofocus lenses, textile machines, satellite antenna pointing.
- **Servo Motors:** Industrial robots (KUKA, FANUC), CNC machining centers, drone gimbal stabilization, telescope tracking.
- **Solenoids:** Automotive starter motors (engagement), fuel injectors, door locks, vending machines, MRI contrast injectors.
- **BLDC Motors:** Drones (DJI), electric vehicles (Tesla traction motors are 3-phase AC induction — but hub motors use BLDC), computer fans, hard disk spindles.
- **Linear Motors:** Maglev trains, semiconductor wafer steppers (photolithography), pick-and-place machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stepper Motor Step Angle for Hybrid Configuration

### Problem Statement
A **hybrid stepper motor** has a rotor with **$N_r = 50$ teeth** and is driven by a **2-phase** stator winding in full-step mode. Calculate:
(a) The step angle in degrees and radians.
(b) The number of pulses required for the shaft to rotate **3 complete revolutions**.
(c) The angular displacement if **$n = 175$ pulses** are applied.

---

### Given Data
- $N_r = 50$ (rotor teeth)
- Number of phases = 2
- Full-step excitation

---

### Step 1 — Apply the Hybrid Stepper Formula

For a hybrid stepper motor, the step angle is governed entirely by the rotor tooth count (since the rotor tooth pitch and stator tooth pitch are offset by half a tooth-pitch to create detent torque):

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Conversion logic:** One full mechanical revolution = 360°, divided equally among all rotor teeth — each tooth corresponds to one stable equilibrium position.

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{50} = 7.2° \text{ per step}$$

**Convert to radians:**

$$\beta_{\text{rad}} = 7.2° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{7.2 \pi}{180} = 0.1257 \text{ rad}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is unusually large for a hybrid motor — typical hybrids have **1.8°** ($N_r = 200$). A 7.2° motor is feasible but uncommon.

---

### Step 2 — Pulses for 3 Revolutions

Each revolution requires $N_r = 50$ pulses.

$$n_{3\text{ rev}} = 3 \times 50 = 150 \text{ pulses}$$

---

### Step 3 — Angular Displacement for $n = 175$ Pulses

$$\theta = n \times \beta = 175 \times 7.2°$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 175 \times 7.2 \\
&= 1260°
\end{aligned}$$

Since full revolutions = $\lfloor 1260° / 360° \rfloor = 3$ full revolutions + remainder:

$$\theta_{\text{remainder}} = 1260° - 3(360°) = 1260° - 1080° = 180°$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta = 3 \text{ revolutions } + 180°$ (or $4\pi$ radians total).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Armature-Controlled DC Servo Motor Transfer Function

### Problem Statement
Derive the **open-loop transfer function** $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)}$ for an armature-controlled DC servo motor, neglecting armature inductance.

---

### Given System Equations

**Armature Circuit (KVL):**

$$V_c = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

**Motor Torque Equation:**

$$T_m = K_t I_a$$

**Mechanical Load Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for rotation):**

$$J_m \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} + B_m \frac{d\theta_m}{dt} = T_m - T_L$$

**Back EMF Coupling:**

$$E_b = K_b \frac{d\theta_m}{dt}$$

---

### Step 1 — Laplace Transform of Armature Equation

Assuming zero initial conditions and neglecting $L_a$ (KTU standard assumption for tractability):

$$V_c(s) = R_a I_a(s) + K_b \omega_m(s)$$

Where $\omega_m(s) = s \cdot \theta_m(s)$.

**Conversion logic:** Inductor voltage drop $L_a(di/dt) \to L_a s I_a(s)$ in s-domain; setting $L_a = 0$ removes the inductive term.

$$V_c(s) = R_a I_a(s) + K_b s \theta_m(s) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

---

### Step 2 — Laplace Transform of Mechanical Equation (with $T_L = 0$ for simplicity)

$$J_m s^2 \theta_m(s) + B_m s \theta_m(s) = T_m(s)$$

$$T_m(s) = [J_m s^2 + B_m s] \theta_m(s) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

---

### Step 3 — Express Torque in Terms of Current

$$T_m(s) = K_t I_a(s) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

---

### Step 4 — Solve Equation (2) for $\theta_m(s)$ in Terms of $I_a(s)$

From (2) and (3):

$$K_t I_a(s) = [J_m s^2 + B_m s] \theta_m(s)$$

$$\theta_m(s) = \frac{K_t I_a(s)}{s(J_m s + B_m)} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Conversion logic:** Factor out $s$ from the denominator: $J_m s^2 + B_m s = s(J_m s + B_m)$.

---

### Step 5 — Solve Equation (1) for $I_a(s)$

$$I_a(s) = \frac{V_c(s) - K_b s \theta_m(s)}{R_a} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

---

### Step 6 — Substitute (5) into (4)

$$\theta_m(s) = \frac{K_t}{s(J_m s + B_m)} \cdot \frac{V_c(s) - K_b s \theta_m(s)}{R_a}$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiply both sides by $s(J_m s + B_m) R_a / K_t$:

$$\theta_m(s) \cdot s(J_m s + B_m) R_a = K_t [V_c(s) - K_b s \theta_m(s)]$$

Expand left side:

$$R_a s(J_m s + B_m) \theta_m(s) = K_t V_c(s) - K_t K_b s \theta_m(s)$$

Collect $\theta_m(s)$ terms on the left:

$$R_a s(J_m s + B_m) \theta_m(s) + K_t K_b s \theta_m(s) = K_t V_c(s)$$

Factor out $s \cdot \theta_m(s)$:

$$s \theta_m(s) \left[ R_a (J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b \right] = K_t V_c(s)$$

Expand the bracket:

$$s \theta_m(s) \left[ R_a J_m s + R_a B_m + K_t K_b \right] = K_t V_c(s)$$

---

### Step 7 — Final Transfer Function

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ R_a J_m s + (R_a B_m + K_t K_b) \right]}$$

Define:

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b} \quad \text{(motor gain constant)}$$

$$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b} \quad \text{(motor mechanical time constant)}$$

Then the transfer function simplifies to the **canonical KTU form**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The **$s$** in the denominator ⇒ **double integrator** behavior (1/s from velocity-to-position, another integration inherent in armature dynamics) → type-2 system → zero steady-state error for step inputs.
> - **$\tau_m$** represents how quickly the motor accelerates.
> - **$K_m$** sets the steady-state position per unit voltage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solenoid Force for Linear Solenoid

### Problem Statement
A solenoid has an inductance that varies with plunger displacement $x$ as:

$$L(x) = L_0 + kx$$

where $L_0 = 0.5$ H, $k = 0.2$ H/m, and the coil current is held constant at $I = 2$ A. Calculate the magnetic force on the plunger.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the Force Formula

$$F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute $dL/dx$

$$\frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}(L_0 + kx) = k$$

---

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$F = \frac{1}{2} (2)^2 (0.2)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 0.2 \\
&= 0.4 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Direction:** The force is directed to **increase $x$** (positive direction) because the system seeks *minimum reluctance* (maximum inductance).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stepper vs Servo vs BLDC

| Parameter | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor | BLDC Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commutation** | Electronic (open-loop) | Mechanical brushes | Electronic (closed-loop sensorless/sensored) |
| **Position Accuracy** | ±5% (no feedback) | ±0.01% (with encoder) | ±0.1% (with Hall sensors) |
| **Speed Range** | Low–moderate (0–2000 rpm typical) | Wide (0–6000 rpm) | Very high (0–100,000 rpm) |
| **Torque at Standstill** | High holding torque | Requires continuous current | Sensorless issue at zero speed |
| **Efficiency** | Low–moderate (30–60%) | Moderate (60–75%) | High (85–95%) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium–high |
| **Failure Mode** | Silent (missed steps) | Loud (overshoot/oscillation) | Sensor failure detection required |
| **Best Application** | Open-loop positioning | High-precision tracking | High-speed continuous rotation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hybrid Stepper Motor — Stator Phase Excitation Sequence (Full-Step Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["Step 1: Phase A ON, Phase B OFF"] --> step2["Step 2: Phase A ON, Phase B ON"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Phase A OFF, Phase B OFF wait Phase Reverse"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Phase A reverse, Phase B ON"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Phase A reverse, Phase B reverse"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Phase A OFF, Phase B reverse"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 8: Cycle complete, rotor moved by beta"]
    step8 --> step1
```

---

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Servo Motor Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmd["Commanded Position theta_cmd t"] --> sum["Summing Junction: e = theta_cmd minus theta_actual"]
    fb["Feedback Path: theta_actual"]
    sum --> ctrl["Controller: PID Amplifier"]
    ctrl --> amp["Power Amplifier: Gain Ka"]
    amp --> motor["DC Servo Motor: Km divided by s times tau m s plus 1"]
    motor --> out["Output: theta_actual t"]
    out --> fb
    fb --> sum
```

---

## 4.3 Solenoid Construction and Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    coil["Current Carrying Coil: L x i"] --> field["Magnetic Flux phi"]
    field --> gap["Air Gap: variable with plunger position x"]
    gap --> plunger["Ferromagnetic Plunger: experiences attractive force F"]
    plunger --> force["Output: Linear Force F = 1 over 2 times i squared times dL by dx"]
    spring["Return Spring: opposes plunger motion"] -.->|restoring force| plunger
    coil -.->|when de energized| spring
```

---

## 4.4 BLDC Motor — Electronic Commutation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwm["PWM Command Signal from MCU"] --> driver["3 Phase Inverter: 6 MOSFETs IGBTs"]
    driver --> phase1["Phase A ON"]
    driver --> phase2["Phase B ON"]
    driver --> phase3["Phase C ON"]
    phase1 --> rotor["Permanent Magnet Rotor: aligns with stator field"]
    phase2 --> rotor
    phase3 --> rotor
    rotor --> sensor["Hall Sensors: detect rotor position"]
    sensor --> decoder["Commutation Decoder: determines next phase sequence"]
    decoder --> driver
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Overall Mechatronic Actuator Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Application Requirements"] --> q1{"Position or Speed Control?"}
    q1 -->|Position| q2{"Open loop OK?"}
    q1 -->|Speed| blc["Use BLDC or DC Motor"]
    q2 -->|Yes Low cost| stp["Stepper Motor: 1.8 or 0.9 degrees"]
    q2 -->|No High precision| srv["Servo Motor with Encoder Feedback"]
    stp --> chk1{"Heavy Load?"}
    chk1 -->|Yes| hyb["Hybrid Stepper: high torque"]
    chk1 -->|No| pm["PM Stepper: sufficient"]
    srv --> chk2{"Continuous rotation?"}
    chk2 -->|Yes| acs["AC Servo Induction"]
    chk2 -->|No| dcs["DC Servo Brush type acceptable"]
    blc --> end["Selected Actuator"]
    hyb --> end
    pm --> end
    acs --> end
    dcs --> end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** and **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** based on construction, step angle, and detent torque.

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Criterion | VR Stepper Motor | PM Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotor Material** | Soft iron (no magnet) | Permanent magnet |
| **Step Angle** | Small (e.g., 15°, 7.5°) | Larger (45°, 90°) |
| **Detent Torque** | Zero (no residual magnetism) | Present (magnet holds rotor) |
| **Inertia** | Low rotor inertia | Higher (due to magnet mass) |
| **Holding Torque** | Depends on excitation only | Higher even without excitation (due to magnet) |

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q:** State the **advantages and disadvantages** of armature-controlled DC servo motors over field-controlled DC servo motors.

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Advantages:**
1. **Linear torque-speed characteristics** over a wide range — predictable control.
2. **Quick response** due to lower inductance in armature circuit.
3. **No magnetic saturation issues** because field operates at constant high flux.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Requires **higher control power** to drive the armature.
2. Commutator and brush maintenance required.
3. Heat dissipation in armature winding is concentrated.

---

## 5.2 📙 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q:** **(a)** With a neat construction diagram, explain the working of a **Hybrid Stepper Motor**. Derive the expression for its **step angle**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has **200 rotor teeth** and is driven by a 2-phase winding in **half-step mode**.
   - (i) Calculate the **step angle** in degrees and radians.
   - (ii) Find the **number of pulses** required to rotate the shaft by **720°**.
   - (iii) If the motor runs at **500 pulses per second**, calculate the **shaft speed in RPM**. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION A:

#### Part (a) — Hybrid Stepper Motor (7 Marks)

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A hybrid stepper motor consists of:
- A **two-phase stator** with salient poles carrying concentrated windings (each pole has small teeth on its face).
- A **two-section rotor** made of axially magnetized permanent magnets sandwiched between two soft-iron end-caps. Each end-cap is toothed (e.g., 50 teeth). The two end-caps are mechanically offset by **half a tooth pitch**.
- This combination of *variable reluctance* (from teeth) and *permanent magnet excitation* (from magnet) gives the hybrid motor its name.

**Working (2 Marks):**
When phase A is energized, the rotor teeth on one end align with phase A stator teeth, while the rotor teeth on the other end are offset by half a pitch (due to the axial offset). Reversing the current polarity in phase A causes the rotor to advance by **one rotor tooth pitch**, i.e., one step. Sequential switching between phases produces continuous rotation.

**Step Angle Derivation (2 Marks):**

Let $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth per end-cap. Since the two end-caps are offset by half a tooth pitch, energizing one phase causes rotation by half a tooth pitch; switching to the next phase causes another half pitch. The **full step** is one complete tooth pitch:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

For $N_r = 50$: $\beta = 360°/50 = 7.2°$.
For $N_r = 200$: $\beta = 360°/200 = 1.8°$ (industry standard).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N_r = 200$, 2-phase, half-step mode.

**(i) Step Angle Calculation (2 Marks):**

In half-step mode, the effective step is **half** of the full step:

$$\beta_{\text{full}} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

$$\beta_{\text{half-step}} = \frac{1.8°}{2} = 0.9°$$

Converting to radians:

$$\beta_{\text{rad}} = 0.9° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.01571 \text{ rad}$$

---

**(ii) Pulses for 720° (2 Marks):**

$$n = \frac{\theta_{\text{desired}}}{\beta_{\text{half-step}}} = \frac{720°}{0.9°/pulse}$$

$$n = 800 \text{ pulses}$$

---

**(iii) Shaft Speed in RPM (3 Marks):**

Pulse frequency $f = 500$ pulses/sec.

Each pulse advances the rotor by $\beta = 0.9°$.

Angular velocity in degrees per second:

$$\omega = f \times \beta = 500 \times 0.9 = 450°/\text{s}$$

Convert to RPM:

$$\omega_{\text{RPM}} = 450 \frac{°}{\text{s}} \times \frac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}} \times \frac{1 \text{ rev}}{360°}$$

$$\omega_{\text{RPM}} = 450 \times \frac{60}{360} = 450 \times \frac{1}{6} = 75 \text{ RPM}$$

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q:** **(a)** Derive the **transfer function** $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)}$ of an **armature-controlled DC servo motor**, neglecting armature inductance. Define all symbols clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **construction and working of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** with a block diagram. Compare BLDC motors with conventional brushed DC motors. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION B:

#### Part (a) — DC Servo Motor Transfer Function (7 Marks)

**Symbols (1 Mark):**
- $V_c$ = control voltage applied to armature
- $R_a, L_a$ = armature resistance and inductance
- $I_a$ = armature current
- $E_b$ = back EMF
- $K_b$ = back EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = torque constant (N·m/A)
- $T_m$ = motor developed torque
- $J_m$ = moment of inertia of rotor + load
- $B_m$ = viscous friction coefficient
- $\theta_m$ = angular displacement of motor shaft
- $T_L$ = load torque

**Governing Equations (2 Marks):**

$$V_c = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b \quad \text{(KVL)}$$

$$T_m = K_t I_a \quad \text{(torque-current)}$$

$$E_b = K_b \frac{d\theta_m}{dt} \quad \text{(back EMF)}$$

$$J_m \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} + B_m \frac{d\theta_m}{dt} = T_m - T_L \quad \text{(Newton's law for rotation)}$$

**Laplace Transform (1 Mark):** Assuming zero initial conditions and $T_L = 0$:

$$V_c(s) = R_a I_a(s) + K_b s \theta_m(s) \quad \text{...(1) (neglecting } L_a \text{)}$$

$$K_t I_a(s) = (J_m s^2 + B_m s) \theta_m(s) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Algebraic Manipulation (2 Marks):**

From (2): $\quad I_a(s) = \dfrac{s(J_m s + B_m) \theta_m(s)}{K_t}$

Substitute into (1):

$$V_c(s) = R_a \cdot \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \theta_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b s \theta_m(s)$$

$$V_c(s) = s \theta_m(s) \left[ \frac{R_a (J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

$$V_c(s) = s \theta_m(s) \cdot \frac{R_a J_m s + R_a B_m + K_t K_b}{K_t}$$

**Final Transfer Function (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s (R_a J_m s + R_a B_m + K_t K_b)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}}$$

Where $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b}$ and $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J_m}{R_a B_m + K_t K_b}$.

---

#### Part (b) — BLDC Motor (7 Marks)

**Construction (2 Marks):**
A BLDC motor has:
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnet (typically 2–8 pole pairs, rare-earth materials like NdFeB).
- **Stator:** Three-phase windings (U, V, W) embedded in laminated silicon steel slots.
- **Position Sensors:** Three Hall-effect sensors (or back-EMF sensing) to detect rotor angular position.
- **Electronic Controller:** Inverter with 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs to commutate phases based on rotor position.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
Based on rotor position feedback from Hall sensors, the controller energizes exactly **two of the three phases** at any time (e.g., U-V, V-W, W-U in sequence). The energized stator winding produces a magnetic field that *pulls* the nearest rotor pole-pair into alignment. Sequential switching advances the field, and the rotor follows synchronously. No mechanical brushes — commutation is purely electronic.

**Block Diagram Explanation (1 Mark):**
The block diagram in Section 4.4 shows: MCU → PWM driver → 3-phase inverter → stator windings → rotor (with Hall sensors feeding back position to the commutation decoder).

**Comparison with Brushed DC Motor (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Brushed DC | BLDC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brush-commutator) | Electronic (inverter) |
| Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Very Low |
| Efficiency | 60–75% | 85–95% |
| Speed Range | Limited by brush arcing | Very high (>10,000 RPM) |
| Heat Dissipation | In rotor (poor) | In stator (good) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (electronics) |
| Lifespan | Short (brushes) | Long |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the step angle in half-step mode** — Many students use full-step angle directly. KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit confusion in RPM calculation** — Always convert deg/s → rad/s → RPM by multiplying by $60/(2\pi)$ OR by $60/360$ if you kept degrees.
> 3. **Sign error in transfer function derivation** — The negative feedback from back EMF goes on the *same side* as the input voltage; rearranging gives the standard form. Don't drop the $K_b K_t$ term in the denominator.
> 4. **Stepper motor "missed steps" is not a fault** — It is a *characteristic* of open-loop operation; examiners want this distinction.
> 5. **Solenoid force is $i^2$ — NOT $i$** — Solenoids cannot reverse by current polarity. Examiners specifically test this misconception.
> 6. **No mention of Hall sensors in BLDC** — Without position sensing, commutation is impossible. Missing this costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing holding torque with detent torque** — Holding torque requires excitation; detent torque exists without it.

---

## 5.3 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stepper Motor Formula:** $\beta = 360°/N_r$ (hybrid) and $\beta = 360°/(N_s \cdot N_r \cdot \text{Ph})$ (VR).
- ✅ **Hybrid stepper** is most common in industry due to high resolution + high torque.
- ✅ **PM stepper** has detent torque; **VR stepper** does NOT.
- ✅ **Half-step mode** halves the step angle → doubles resolution.
- ✅ **Pull-in curve** = start/stop region; **Pull-out curve** = slewing region (higher torque limit).
- ✅ **Armature-controlled servos** are linear and preferred; field-controlled servos are non-linear.
- ✅ **Servo transfer function:** $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ (with $L_a$ neglected).
- ✅ **Solenoid force** is proportional to $i^2$ — always attractive, cannot reverse.
- ✅ **BLDC motors** use electronic commutation via Hall sensors + 3-phase inverter.
- ✅ **Voice coil actuators** use Lorentz force ($F = BILN$) — used in disk drives.
- ✅ **Stepper motors** are *open-loop*; **servos** are *closed-loop* — this is the #1 KTU comparison question.
- ✅ **Detent torque** ≠ **Holding torque**: detent = no current, holding = with current.
- ✅ **Number of pulses for any rotation:** $n = \theta_{\text{desired}}/\beta$.
- ✅ **Shaft speed in RPM** from pulse frequency: $\omega_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta \cdot 60}{360°}$.
- ✅ **KTU Favorite Numerical:** Always includes a hybrid stepper with 200 teeth → 1.8° step angle.
- ✅ **Armature inductance neglected** in standard KTU derivations — don't forget to state this assumption.
- ✅ **BLDC rotor** is on the outside or inside? → **Inside** (surrounded by stator windings for better cooling).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data presentation system signal conditioning: introduction to signal processing and Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data presentation system signal conditioning: introduction to signal processing and Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Presentation System
A **Data Presentation System** is the terminal stage of a mechatronic measurement chain that converts processed electrical signals into a human-perceivable or machine-readable form. It forms the final block in a typical measurement chain (Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Data Presentation System).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Data Presentation System displays, records, or transmits the output of a measurement system in a format suitable for interpretation, decision-making, or further processing (e.g., CRO, digital display, recorders, computer-based DAS).

**Common Data Presentation Devices:**
- **Analog Meters** (voltmeter, ammeter, galvanometer)
- **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)**
- **Strip Chart / X-Y Recorders**
- **LED / LCD Digital Displays**
- **Computer Monitors / HMI Screens**
- **Data Loggers**

### 1.2 Signal Conditioning
**Signal Conditioning** is the manipulation of a raw sensor signal to make it suitable for the next stage of processing (e.g., ADC, display unit). Raw signals from sensors are usually too weak, noisy, or incompatible with the destination hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU):** Signal conditioning is the process of converting a signal from one form to another (e.g., amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization) so that it can be effectively utilized by the data acquisition/display system.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of signal conditioning as a **translator at a UN conference**. A French delegate (sensor) speaks softly into a poor microphone (raw, noisy signal). The translator (signal conditioner) amplifies his voice, removes background noise, and converts it to a clear English broadcast (clean, standardized signal) that the audience (ADC/display) can easily understand.

### 1.3 Functional Operations in Signal Conditioning
1. **Amplification** – Boosts weak signals to a usable level.
2. **Attenuation** – Reduces signal amplitude to within dynamic range.
3. **Filtering** – Removes unwanted frequency components (noise).
4. **Isolation** – Electrically separates sensor from rest of system (safety).
5. **Linearization** – Corrects non-linear sensor response.
6. **Impedance Matching** – Minimizes loading effects.
7. **Cold-Junction Compensation** – For thermocouples.
8. **Excitation** – Provides power to passive sensors (strain gauge).

### 1.4 Introduction to Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)
An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a DC-coupled, high-gain, differential electronic voltage amplifier with two inputs (inverting and non-inverting) and a single output. It is the workhorse of analog signal conditioning.

**Conceptual Analogy:** An op-amp is like a **differential lever with a very long arm**. It looks at the *difference* between two input forces and produces a *much larger* mechanical output. Because the arm is so long (very high open-loop gain ≈ 10⁵ to 10⁶), tiny input differences are enormously amplified, which is why we must use **negative feedback** to control the gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Op-Amp Characteristics (Standard KTU Values):**
> - Open-loop gain (A): **∞ (infinite)**
> - Input impedance (Z_in): **∞ (infinite)**
> - Output impedance (Z_out): **0 (zero)**
> - Bandwidth: **∞ (infinite)**
> - Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR): **∞ (infinite)**
> - Offset voltage & current: **0**

### 1.5 The Op-Amp Symbol and Terminals
An op-amp has **5 basic terminals**:
1. **Inverting Input** (−) — denoted `V⁻`
2. **Non-inverting Input** (+) — denoted `V⁺`
3. **Output** — `V_out`
4. **Positive Supply** — +V_CC (e.g., +15 V)
5. **Negative Supply** — −V_EE (e.g., −15 V)

For analysis, +V_CC and −V_EE are often ignored and the op-amp is treated as a **two-port device**.

### 1.6 Virtual Short & Virtual Ground Concepts
- **Virtual Short:** With negative feedback, the differential input voltage $V_d = V^+ - V^- \approx 0$ (i.e., $V^+ = V^-$).
- **Virtual Ground:** When the non-inverting input is grounded ($V^+ = 0$), the inverting input also appears to be at 0 V (ground potential), but is *not* physically connected to ground.

> [!NOTE]
> These two concepts are the **backbone of op-amp analysis** in the KTU exam. Master these before attempting any circuit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Op-Amp Configurations — Operational Theory

#### (A) Inverting Amplifier
- Input signal applied to the **inverting (−) terminal** through resistor $R_1$.
- Non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output to inverting input.

**Why it works:** The virtual short forces the inverting input to 0 V. Input current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R_1$ flows through $R_1$, but since the input impedance is infinite, this current must flow through $R_f$. This creates an output voltage of opposite polarity to the input.

**Closed-loop gain (magnitude):**

$$
A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion**.

#### (B) Non-Inverting Amplifier
- Input signal applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.
- Feedback network ($R_1$, $R_f$) connected between output, inverting input, and ground.

**Why it works:** Virtual short forces $V^- = V_{in}$. Voltage divider across $R_1$ sets $V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$. Equating yields the gain.

**Closed-loop gain:**

$$
A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

Always ≥ 1, output is **in phase** with input.

#### (C) Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer)
- A special case of the non-inverting amplifier where $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$.
- Gain = 1, but provides **impedance transformation** — high input impedance, low output impedance.
- Used for **impedance matching / isolation** between stages.

#### (D) Summing Amplifier
- Multiple input signals applied at the inverting node through individual resistors.
- Output is the **inverted weighted sum** of the inputs.

$$
V_{out} = -\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1}V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2}V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3}V_3\right)
$$

If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$, then $V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R}(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$ — true **analog adder**.

#### (E) Difference (Differential) Amplifier
- Two inputs: $V_1$ at the inverting terminal via $R_1$, $V_2$ at the non-inverting terminal via $R_3$.
- Output is proportional to the **difference** of the two inputs.

$$
V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}\left(V_2 - V_1\right) \quad \text{when } \frac{R_f}{R_1} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

#### (F) Instrumentation Amplifier
- A high-performance version of the difference amplifier built using **three op-amps**.
- Extremely high input impedance, very high CMRR, and **adjustable gain via a single resistor** $R_g$.

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}
$$

Used universally with **strain gauges, thermocouples, and biomedical sensors**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Voltage Gain $A_{CL}$ | Input Impedance | Phase |
|---|---------------|------------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Inverting Amplifier | $-\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $R_1$ | 180° |
| 2 | Non-Inverting Amplifier | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $\infty$ | 0° |
| 3 | Voltage Follower | $1$ | $\infty$ | 0° |
| 4 | Summing Amplifier (n inputs) | $-\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \cdots\right)$ | $R_1 \mid R_2 \cdots$ | 180° |
| 5 | Difference Amplifier | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)$ | Depends | 0°/180° |
| 6 | Instrumentation Amplifier | $1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | Very High | 0° |
| 7 | Differentiator (ideal) | $-s R_f C_1$ | $X_{C_1}$ | +90° |
| 8 | Integrator (ideal) | $-\dfrac{1}{s R_1 C_f}$ | $R_1$ | −90° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: In the table, the symbol $\mid$ is used to denote parallel combination (e.g., $R_1 \mid R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$). Do **not** confuse it with the absolute value symbol which is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ in equations.

### 2.3 Active Filters Using Op-Amps

Filters selectively pass or reject frequency components. **Active filters** use op-amps to provide gain and avoid inductor use.

#### (i) First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter
- Passes low frequencies, attenuates high frequencies.
- Cutoff frequency: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$
- Passband gain: $A_p = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- Roll-off: **−20 dB/decade** beyond $f_c$

$$
H(s) = \frac{-R_f / R_1}{1 + sR_fC_f}
$$

#### (ii) First-Order Active High-Pass Filter
- Passes high frequencies, blocks DC.
- Cutoff frequency: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$
- Passband gain: $A_p = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_2}$

$$
H(s) = \frac{-sR_fC_1}{1 + sR_1C_1}
$$

#### (iii) Active Band-Pass Filter
- Cascade of HPF + LPF, OR a single stage with L-C-R network.
- Center frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_f C_1 C_f}}$ (for Sallen-Key topology)
- Quality factor $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\text{BW}}$

### 2.4 Key Op-Amp Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Importance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------|
| Open-loop gain | $A_{OL}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | Determines closed-loop accuracy |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBW | $1$ MHz to $1$ GHz | Sets usable frequency range |
| Slew Rate | $SR$ | $0.5$ to $5000$ V/μs | Limits large-signal response |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | $80$ to $120$ dB | Rejects common-mode noise |
| Input Offset Voltage | $V_{io}$ | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5$ mV | Causes DC error |
| Input Bias Current | $I_B$ | nA to pA | Affects high-impedance circuits |
| Power Supply Rejection Ratio | PSRR | $80$ to $100$ dB | Sensitivity to supply noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Slew Rate Limitation:** $SR = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}\bigg|_{max}$. For a sine wave $V_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, we need:
> 
> $$SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$$
> 
> If violated, the output becomes a **triangular wave** (distortion). This is a favorite KTU question!

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Industrial automation:** 4–20 mA current loop conditioning.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG amplification using instrumentation amplifiers.
- **Automotive:** Sensor signal conditioning in ECU (MAP, MAF, oxygen sensors).
- **Audio:** Active crossovers, equalizers, mixers.
- **Instrumentation:** Strain gauge bridges in weighing scales.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inverting Amplifier Gain
**Circuit:** Non-inverting input grounded. Input $V_{in}$ applied through $R_1$ to inverting node. Feedback $R_f$ between output and inverting node.

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short:**
Since the non-inverting input is at 0 V, the inverting input is also virtually at 0 V (virtual ground).

$$
V^- = V^+ = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply virtual open (infinite input impedance):**
No current flows into the op-amp input terminals. Therefore, by KCL at the inverting node, the current entering through $R_1$ must equal the current leaving through $R_f$.

$$
I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Express currents using Ohm's law:**

$$
I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}
$$

$$
I_{R_f} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two currents:**

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$:**

$$
A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain
**Circuit:** Input $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting (+) terminal. Resistor $R_1$ from inverting input to ground. Feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting input.

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short:**

$$
V^- = V^+ = V_{in}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage divider at the inverting node:**
The resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$ form a voltage divider from $V_{out}$ to ground.

$$
V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $V^-$:**

$$
V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the gain:**

$$
\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Summing Amplifier (3 inputs)
**Circuit:** Inputs $V_1, V_2, V_3$ at the inverting node through $R_1, R_2, R_3$. Feedback $R_f$ between output and inverting node. Non-inverting input grounded.

**Step 1 — Virtual ground at inverting input:**

$$
V^- = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (current in = current out, since $I_{in} = 0$):**

$$
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$
\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$
V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = -\left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3} V_3 \right)}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Active First-Order Low-Pass Filter
**Circuit:** Inverting amplifier with input through $R_1$ to inverting node, **and** capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ (feedback impedance $Z_f = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{sC_f}$).

**Step 1 — In the inverting amplifier, the gain equation uses impedances:**

$$
A_{CL}(s) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_1}
$$

where $Z_1 = R_1$ and $Z_f = R_f \parallel \dfrac{1}{sC_f} = \dfrac{R_f}{1 + sR_fC_f}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
A_{CL}(s) = -\frac{R_f / (1 + sR_f C_f)}{R_1} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sR_f C_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify passband gain and cutoff frequency:**

At $s \to 0$ (DC): $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_1$ → passband gain $A_p = -R_f/R_1$.

The pole occurs at $1 + sR_fC_f = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1/(R_fC_f)$.

In terms of frequency, $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{R_f C_f}$, so:

$$
\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude response:**
For $f \ll f_c$: $\vert H \vert \approx \vert A_p \vert$ (passes signal).
For $f \gg f_c$: $\vert H \vert \approx \dfrac{\vert A_p \vert}{f/f_c}$ → roll-off of **−20 dB/decade**.

### 3.5 Slew Rate Derivation (Example Problem)
**Problem:** An op-amp has $SR = 0.5$ V/μs. Find the maximum undistorted sine wave amplitude at $f = 10$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Slew rate requirement for a sine wave:**
The output $v_{out}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ has maximum rate of change:

$$
\left| \frac{dv_{out}}{dt} \right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p
$$

**Step 2 — Equate to slew rate and solve for $V_p$:**

$$
V_p = \frac{SR}{2\pi f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (note: $SR = 0.5$ V/μs $= 0.5 \times 10^{6}$ V/s):**

$$
V_p = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
V_p = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{6.2832 \times 10^4} = \frac{500000}{62832} \approx 7.96 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_p \approx 7.96 \text{ V (peak)}}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Configuration

| Application | Recommended Configuration | Justification |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Inverting a signal | Inverting Amplifier | Provides 180° phase shift with controlled gain |
| Impedance matching | Voltage Follower | Gain = 1, very high $Z_{in}$, very low $Z_{out}$ |
| Mixing audio channels | Summing Amplifier | Weighted addition of multiple sources |
| Extracting small differential signal from common-mode noise | Instrumentation Amplifier | Extremely high CMRR |
| Removing 50 Hz hum from ECG | Notch Filter (active) | Selective frequency rejection |
| Anti-aliasing before ADC | Low-Pass Active Filter | Limits bandwidth to satisfy Nyquist |
| Removing DC offset from sensor | High-Pass Active Filter | Blocks DC, passes AC |
| True RMS measurement | Precision Rectifier | Op-amp based full-wave rectifier |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Data Acquisition & Presentation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[PHYSICAL QUANTITY] --> S2[SENSOR / TRANSDUCER]
    S2 --> S3[SIGNAL CONDITIONING BLOCK]
    S3 --> S4[AMPLIFIER OP-AMP STAGE]
    S4 --> S5[FILTER ACTIVE LPF OR HPF]
    S5 --> S6[ADC ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    S6 --> S7[MICROCONTROLLER OR PC]
    S7 --> S8[DATA PRESENTATION SYSTEM]
    S8 --> S9A[DISPLAY LED LCD]
    S8 --> S9B[RECORDER CRO PRINTER]
    S8 --> S9C[HMI SCREEN]
```

### 4.2 Inverting Amplifier Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[INPUT VIN] --- R1[RESISTOR R1]
    R1 --- NODEM[INVERTING NODE V NEG]
    NODEM --- RFB[FEEDBACK RESISTOR RF]
    RFB --- VOUT[OUTPUT VOUT]
    NODEM --- OPAMP[OP-AMP SYMBOL]
    OPAMP --- VOUT
    GNDA[GROUND] --- NODEM2[NON INVERTING INPUT V POS]
    NODEM2 --- OPAMP
```

### 4.3 Non-Inverting Amplifier Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN2[INPUT VIN] --- NODEN[NON INVERTING INPUT V POS]
    NODEN --- OPAMP2[OP-AMP SYMBOL]
    OPAMP2 --- VOUT2[OUTPUT VOUT]
    NODEM3[INVERTING NODE V NEG] --- OPAMP2
    R1A[RESISTOR R1] --- NODEM3
    R1A --- GRNDA[GROUND]
    RFA[FEEDBACK RESISTOR RF] --- NODEM3
    RFA --- VOUT2
```

### 4.4 Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[INPUT V1] --> R1A1[INPUT RESISTOR R1]
    V2[INPUT V2] --> R1A2[INPUT RESISTOR R1]
    R1A1 --> OP1[OP-AMP A1 INPUT STAGE]
    R1A2 --> OP2[OP-AMP A2 INPUT STAGE]
    RG[GAIN SETTING RESISTOR RG] -.CONNECTS BETWEEN A1 AND A2.-> OP1
    RG -.CONNECTS BETWEEN A1 AND A2.-> OP2
    OP1 --> R2A1[FEEDBACK RESISTOR R2]
    OP2 --> R2A2[FEEDBACK RESISTOR R2]
    R2A1 --> OP3[OP-AMP A3 DIFFERENCE STAGE]
    R2A2 --> R3A1[RESISTOR R3]
    R3A1 --> OP3
    OP3 --> VOUT3[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VOUT]
```

### 4.5 First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (Mermaid Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN3[INPUT VIN] --> RI1[RESISTOR R1]
    RI1 --> NODEI[INVERTING NODE]
    CF[CAPACITOR CF] --> NODEI
    RF2[RESISTOR RF] --> NODEI
    NODEI --> OPA3[OP-AMP]
    OPA3 --> VOUTI[OUTPUT VOUT]
    GND2[GROUND] --> NODENI[NON INVERTING INPUT]
    NODENI --> OPA3
    RF2 -.FEEDBACK FROM VOUT TO NODE.-> VOUTI
    CF -.FEEDBACK FROM VOUT TO NODE.-> VOUTI
```

### 4.6 Signal Conditioning Functional Block — Detailed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS1[STRAIN GAUGE BRIDGE] --> EXC1[EXCITATION SUPPLY]
    SENS1 --> AMP1[INSTRUMENTATION AMP]
    EXC1 --> AMP1
    AMP1 --> FILT1[LOW PASS ANTI-ALIAS FILTER]
    FILT1 --> ISO1[ISOLATION STAGE]
    ISO1 --> LIN1[LINEARIZATION IF NEEDED]
    LIN1 --> LEV1[LEVEL SHIFTER]
    LEV1 --> ADC1[ADC INPUT]
```

### 4.7 Op-Amp Slew Rate Distortion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SINE[IDEAL SINE WAVE] --> CHECK{CAN OP-AMP RESPOND QUICKLY ENOUGH}
    CHECK -->|SR ADEQUATE| OUT1[CLEAN SINE OUTPUT]
    CHECK -->|SR TOO LOW| OUT2[DISTORTED TRIANGULAR WAVE OUTPUT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams above represent **functional block architectures** rather than literal circuit drawings (which would require a circuit-rendering tool). Each labeled block corresponds to a real op-amp stage or signal-conditioning element in the actual schematic.

### 4.8 Standard Op-Amp Pinout Diagram (e.g., IC 741)

| Pin Number | Label | Function |
|------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | OFFSET NULL | Used to nullify input offset voltage |
| 2 | INVERTING INPUT (−) | Signal input that produces 180° phase shift |
| 3 | NON-INVERTING INPUT (+) | Signal input that preserves phase |
| 4 | V− / V_EE | Negative power supply (typically −15 V) |
| 5 | OFFSET NULL | Used to nullify input offset voltage |
| 6 | OUTPUT | Amplified output signal |
| 7 | V+ / V_CC | Positive power supply (typically +15 V) |
| 8 | NC | No connection |
