# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Define a digital controller. State the Shannon-Nyquist sampling theorem and explain why it is essential in digital control systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A digital controller is a discrete-time control element implemented via a microprocessor that processes sampled error signals and generates control outputs using difference equations.

The Shannon-Nyquist sampling theorem states that a bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a rate $f_s$ such that:

$$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} $$

i.e., the sampling frequency must be at least **twice the highest frequency** present in the signal. The minimum allowed rate, $f_s = 2f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Why it is essential:** If the theorem is violated, high-frequency components "fold back" into the lower frequency range, a phenomenon called **aliasing**. This corrupts the measured error signal, leading to incorrect control actions, instability, and even physical damage to the plant (e.g., mechanical resonance excitation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark trap):** Students often write only $f_s > 2f_{max}$ and forget to mention that the **input signal must be bandlimited**. In practice, an **anti-aliasing filter (AAF)** is mandatory before the ADC. Losing this point costs 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Explain the role of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) in a digital control system. Write its transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is a signal reconstruction device placed between the DAC and the continuous plant. Since the DAC can only output discrete voltage levels, the ZOH holds each output value **constant for one full sampling period $T$**, producing a staircase-shaped analog waveform that the plant can respond to.

**Transfer function:**

$$ G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s} $$

**Key physical insight:** The ZOH introduces an **average time delay of $T/2$**, which reduces the **phase margin** of the closed-loop system. As $T$ increases, this delay grows, eventually destabilizing the loop. Hence, the sampling period must be chosen carefully — small enough for stability, large enough to avoid computational overload.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing ZOH with a first-order hold. The ZOH is **zero-order** (constant), while a first-order hold linearly interpolates between samples.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Module Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the pulse transfer function of a continuous-time plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+2)}$ preceded by a Zero-Order Hold. Assume a sampling period $T = 1$ second. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the digital control system with forward path $D(z)G(z) = \dfrac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.3)}$ and unity feedback, determine the closed-loop pulse transfer function and check stability using the Jury stability test. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup the formula**

$$ G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{G_p(s)}{s} \right\} = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{1}{s^2(s+2)} \right\} $$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion**

$$ \frac{1}{s^2(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+2} $$

Multiplying by $s^2(s+2)$:

$$ 1 = A s(s+2) + B(s+2) + C s^2 $$

- $s = 0$: $1 = 2B \implies B = 1/2$
- $s = -2$: $1 = 4C \implies C = 1/4$
- Coefficient of $s^2$: $0 = A + C \implies A = -1/4$

**Step 3 — Apply Z-transform with $T = 1$ s**

$$ \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{1}{s^2(s+2)} \right\} = -\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} + \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{z}{z - e^{-2}} $$

With $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$:

$$ = -\frac{z}{4(z-1)} + \frac{z}{2(z-1)^2} + \frac{z}{4(z - 0.1353)} $$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $(1 - z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$**

$$ G(z) = -\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2(z-1)} + \frac{(z-1)}{4(z - 0.1353)} $$

**Step 5 — Common denominator and simplify**

After bringing to a common denominator $(z-1)(z - 0.1353)$ and simplifying:

$$ \boxed{G(z) = \frac{0.2164 z + 0.1583}{(z-1)(z - 0.1353)} } $$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Closed-loop pulse transfer function**

$$ T(z) = \frac{D(z)G(z)}{1 + D(z)G(z)} = \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.3) + 0.5} $$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation**

$$ (z-1)(z-0.3) + 0.5 = 0 $$

$$ z^2 - 1.3z + 0.3 + 0.5 = 0 $$

$$ z^2 - 1.3z + 0.8 = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Jury stability test for 2nd-order polynomial $a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2 = 0$ with $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = -1.3$, $a_2 = 0.8$**

**Condition 1:** $P(1) > 0$

$$ P(1) = 1 - 1.3 + 0.8 = 0.5 > 0 \quad \checkmark $$

**Condition 2:** $P(-1) > 0$

$$ P(-1) = 1 + 1.3 + 0.8 = 3.1 > 0 \quad \checkmark $$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$

$$ \vert 0.8 \vert < 1 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

All three conditions are satisfied → **the closed-loop digital control system is STABLE.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning (Q-A part b):** A common student error is to forget the Jury test and instead use the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Routh-Hurwitz is for the **s-plane (continuous systems)** — it is **NOT directly applicable** to the **z-plane**. Marks will be deducted. Always use **Jury or Schur-Cohn** for digital systems.

---

### **Module Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step discretization of a continuous-time PID controller to obtain the **velocity-form digital PID algorithm**. State the advantages of the velocity form over the position form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital controller is described by the difference equation:

$$ u(k) = 1.5 e(k) - 0.6 e(k-1) + 0.2 u(k-1) $$

Find the pulse transfer function $D(z)$ and determine its stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Continuous PID**

$$ u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$

**Step 2 — Discretization**

- Integral: $T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$
- Derivative: $\dfrac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$

**Step 3 — Position form**

$$ u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T}[e(k) - e(k-1)] $$

**Step 4 — Velocity form via $u(k) - u(k-1)$**

$$ \Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T}[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)] $$

**Step 5 — Advantages of the velocity form**

| Feature | Position Form | Velocity Form |
|---|---|---|
| Integral windup | Severe | Naturally avoided |
| Bumpless transfer | Difficult | Easy (computes $\Delta u$) |
| Actuator saturation handling | Poor | Excellent (stops integrating) |
| Computational cost | Higher (full sum) | Lower (recursive) |
| Use in industrial PLCs | Rare | **Standard** |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Take Z-transform of the difference equation**

$$ U(z) = 1.5 E(z) - 0.6 z^{-1} E(z) + 0.2 z^{-1} U(z) $$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to find $D(z) = U(z)/E(z)$**

$$ U(z) [1 - 0.2 z^{-1}] = E(z) [1.5 - 0.6 z^{-1}] $$

$$ \boxed{D(z) = \frac{1.5 - 0.6 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}} = \frac{1.5 z - 0.6}{z - 0.2} } $$

**Step 3 — Stability analysis**

The characteristic equation is the denominator: $z - 0.2 = 0 \implies z = 0.2$.

**Step 4 — Check the pole location**

$$ \vert z \vert = 0.2 < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Pole is inside the unit circle} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The digital controller $D(z)$ is **STABLE.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Trap:** Students sometimes substitute the unit circle boundary as $z = 1$ for all cases. The correct rule is: **if all poles satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$, the system is asymptotically stable.** A pole at $z = 1$ indicates marginal stability (sustained oscillation); $\vert z \vert > 1$ means instability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Digital Controllers)**

- A **digital controller** processes **discrete-time** signals using **difference equations** or equivalently, a **pulse transfer function** $D(z)$.
- The **Z-transform** is the discrete-time analog of the Laplace transform. The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ connects the $s$-plane to the $z$-plane.
- **Stability region in the z-plane:** the **interior of the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$).
- **Shannon-Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is mandatory. Violation causes **aliasing**.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** is a low-pass analog filter placed **before** the ADC.
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** transfer function: $G_{ZOH}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$. It holds each output constant for $T$ seconds and introduces an average delay of $T/2$.
- **Closed-loop pulse transfer function (unity feedback):**

$$ T(z) = \frac{D(z) G(z)}{1 + D(z) G(z)} $$

- **Jury stability test (2nd order, $a_0 > 0$):** three conditions — $P(1) > 0$, $P(-1) > 0$, $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$.
- **Digital PID (position form):**

$$ u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T \sum e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T}[e(k) - e(k-1)] $$

- **Digital PID (velocity / incremental form)** is **industry-standard** due to anti-windup and bumpless transfer benefits.
- **Pulse transfer function of $G_p(s)$ with ZOH:**

$$ G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \, \mathcal{Z}\left\{ \frac{G_p(s)}{s} \right\} $$

- The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** is for continuous systems; **never** apply it to discrete systems. Use **Jury** or **Schur-Cohn**.
- A first-order hold (FOH) **linearly interpolates** between samples; a ZOH holds them **constant**. Do not confuse the two.
- **Integral windup** is the accumulation of error in the integral term when the actuator saturates — solved by **clamping** or by using the **velocity form** of PID.

> **End of Module 4 — Digital Controllers Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program logic controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program logic controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, ruggedized, digital industrial computer specifically designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automated machinery in harsh industrial environments. It continuously monitors inputs from sensors, executes user-programmed logic instructions stored in non-volatile memory, and generates corresponding output signals to actuators — all within a deterministic scan cycle typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** Per the NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 scheme, a PLC is defined as *"a microprocessor-based controller that uses programmable memory to store instructions and to implement functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations in order to control industrial machines and processes through analog or digital input/output modules."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Brain of the Factory"

Imagine an **automatic washing machine**. You press "Start" (input), and the machine follows a strict sequence — fills water, agitates, drains, spins. The PLC works identically, but for an entire factory:

- **Sensors** (eyes/ears) → tell the PLC what is happening (temperature, pressure, position)
- **PLC** (brain) → decides what should happen next, based on a stored program
- **Actuators** (muscles) → motors, valves, heaters that physically do the work
- **Programming device** (teacher) → loads the decision rules into the PLC's memory

In short, if a **microcontroller is the brain of a smart toaster**, a **PLC is the brain of an entire assembly line** — built to survive dust, heat, vibration, and electrical noise.

## 1.3 Why PLCs Exist — The Historical Trigger

Before PLCs, industrial control used **electromechanical relay panels** — wall-sized cabinets of wires and relays. Changing logic meant rewiring physically. PLCs replaced this in 1968 (Bedford Associates, Modicon) by allowing logic changes through *software*, not wires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Whenever asked *"Why PLCs replaced relay logic?"*, answer with three points: (1) programmable, (2) reliable (no moving parts), (3) diagnostic and compact.

## 1.4 PLC in the Mechatronic Context

In a mechatronic system, the PLC sits at the heart of the **control layer** and interfaces with:

- **Sensors** (analog/digital) → via **Input Modules (I)**
- **Actuators** (motors, solenoids, drives) → via **Output Modules (O)**
- **HMI/SCADA** → via **Communication Ports**
- **Other controllers / PCs** → via **Industrial Networks (PROFIBUS, Modbus, EtherNet/IP)**

The figure below shows this positioning:

> **Mechanic (mechanical)** + **Electronic (sensors/actuators)** + **Control (PLC/Microcontroller)** + **Software (HMI/Logic)** = **Mechatronic System**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pearl:** The PLC is the *control kernel* of any discrete-event mechatronic system. Knowing its architecture, scan cycle, and ladder logic is essential for Module 4's ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a PLC — Component-Level Breakdown

A typical PLC consists of the following modular sub-systems:

### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- Contains the **microprocessor**, **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)**, and **control unit**
- Executes the user program, manages memory, handles communications
- Includes **status LEDs** (RUN, STOP, ERROR, BATTERY)

### 2.1.2 Memory System (Tiered)
- **ROM / Flash (Non-volatile):** Stores the operating system (firmware) and the *system program*
- **RAM (Volatile):** Stores the *user program*, *input/output image tables*, and *runtime data*
- **Battery-backed RAM / EEPROM:** Retains user logic during power loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Terminology Alert:** The *user program* lives in RAM, but is *backed up* to EEPROM/Flash so it survives a power cycle. This dual-storage is a frequent 3-mark question.

### 2.1.3 Power Supply Unit (PSU)
- Converts **230 V AC / 24 V DC** mains to the internal logic levels (**5 V DC** or **24 V DC**)
- Provides **isolated, regulated power** to CPU and I/O modules
- Must tolerate ±10% line voltage variation

### 2.1.4 Input/Output (I/O) Modules
| Type | Description | Example Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Input (DI)** | ON/OFF sensing | Limit switch, push button, proximity sensor |
| **Digital Output (DO)** | ON/OFF actuation | Relay coil, solenoid valve, indicator lamp |
| **Analog Input (AI)** | Variable sensing | 4-20 mA, 0-10 V from temperature/pressure sensor |
| **Analog Output (AO)** | Variable actuation | 4-20 mA to VFD, proportional valve |

### 2.1.5 Communication Interfaces
- **RS-232 / RS-485** (legacy, point-to-point)
- **Ethernet / PROFINET** (modern, high-speed)
- **PROFIBUS, Modbus RTU/TCP, DeviceNet** (fieldbus)
- **USB** (programming)

### 2.1.6 Programming Device
- A PC, laptop, or **Hand-Held Programmer (HHP)** loaded with vendor-specific software (e.g., TIA Portal for Siemens, RSLogix for Allen-Bradley, CX-Programmer for Omron)

## 2.2 The PLC Operating Cycle (Scan Cycle) — The Heart of PLC Behavior

The PLC operates in a **repetitive, sequential loop** called the *Scan Cycle*. This is THE most-tested concept in KTU Module 4.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Scan Cycle:

1. **Input Scan (Read):** The CPU reads the *physical state* of all input terminals and copies them into the **Process Image Input Table (PII)** in memory. This happens *once per cycle* and is the snapshot used during program execution.
2. **Program Scan (Execute):** The CPU executes the user program (ladder logic) *rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom*. It uses the PII values (not the live inputs) to compute results, which are written to the **Process Image Output Table (PIQ)**.
3. **Output Scan (Write):** After program execution completes, the CPU transfers the PIQ values to the *physical output terminals*, energizing/de-energizing the actuators.
4. **Housekeeping / Overhead:** Communication handling, self-diagnostics, timer/counter value updates, and memory checks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Input Image Table?** It ensures *consistency* — all inputs read at the same instant, even if a sensor changes during program execution. This prevents *race conditions* and is a classic **2-mark KTU question**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Scan Time $T_{scan}$ | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{ovh}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Sum of all four phases |
| 2 | Program Execution Time $T_{prog}$ | $T_{prog} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}$ | ms | $n$ = no. of instructions; $t_i$ = time per instruction (typically **µs**) |
| 3 | Throughput / Scan Rate | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | scans/second (Hz) | Indicates PLC speed |
| 4 | Memory Word Size | $1 \text{ Word} = 16 \text{ bits}$ | bits | Used for timers, counters, data registers |
| 5 | Analog Resolution | $Q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n}}$ | Volts or mA | $n$ = ADC bits; $FS$ = full-scale range |
| 6 | Input Signal Range (Industry Standard) | $4\text{-}20 \text{ mA}$ | mA | 4 mA = 0% (live zero); 20 mA = 100% |
| 7 | Output Actuator Drive Time | $t_{actuator} = T_{scan} + t_{propagation}$ | ms | Must be less than process time-constant |
| 8 | Reliability (MTBF) | $\text{MTBF} \approx 100{,}000$ to $400{,}000$ hours | hours | 10-50 years continuous operation |
| 9 | Response Time (Digital) | $T_{response} = T_{scan} + t_{module}$ | ms | Worst case = 2 × scan time |
| 10 | Memory Sizing | $M_{total} = M_{prog} + M_{data} + M_{image}$ | KB / MB | Vendor-dependent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall #1:** Students often forget the *housekeeping* term in $T_{scan}$. Examiners at KTU frequently deduct **1 mark** for omitting $T_{ovh}$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where PLCs Are Used

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Welding robots, paint shops, conveyor sequencing
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Pump control, chemical dosing, level monitoring
- **Packaging Industries:** Filling, capping, labeling, sorting
- **Power Plants:** Turbine sequencing, breaker control
- **Smart Factories (Industry 4.0):** PLCs act as *edge controllers* feeding data to MES/Cloud

## 2.5 Comparison Matrix: PLC vs. Other Controllers

| Criterion | PLC | Microcontroller | DCS | PC/SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Industrial harsh | Lab/embedded | Plant-wide | Office/control room |
| **Programming** | Ladder, FBD, ST | C, Assembly | Function blocks | GUI-based |
| **I/O Count** | Hundreds–thousands | Dozens | Thousands+ | HMI only |
| **Reliability (MTBF)** | Very high | Medium | Very high | Medium |
| **Real-time Guarantee** | Deterministic (scan-bound) | Depends on RTOS | Deterministic | Non-deterministic |
| **Cost (per I/O)** | Medium | Low | High | N/A |
| **Use Case** | Discrete control | Embedded product | Continuous process | Supervisory |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between PLC and DCS"* — memorize the 3-point difference: (1) PLC = discrete logic, single controller; (2) DCS = distributed, redundant, for continuous processes; (3) PLC is cheaper for small systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Scan Time Calculation

**Problem Setup (KTU ESE Pattern):** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC executes a user program containing:
- 200 logical AND instructions
- 80 timer instructions
- 50 counter instructions
- 40 math (addition) instructions

Given vendor datasheet instruction execution times:
- AND instruction: $t_{AND} = 0.1 \, \mu s$
- Timer instruction: $t_{T} = 1.2 \, \mu s$
- Counter instruction: $t_{C} = 1.0 \, \mu s$
- Math (ADD) instruction: $t_{M} = 1.5 \, \mu s$

Input scan time $T_{in} = 1.5 \, ms$
Output scan time $T_{out} = 1.0 \, ms$
Housekeeping time $T_{ovh} = 0.8 \, ms$

Find the total scan time and maximum scan frequency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The total scan time is the sum of the four phases:

$$T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{ovh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the program execution time $T_{prog}$.**

The program execution time is the weighted sum of the per-instruction time multiplied by the number of each instruction type.

$$T_{prog} = N_{AND} \cdot t_{AND} + N_{T} \cdot t_{T} + N_{C} \cdot t_{C} + N_{M} \cdot t_{M}$$

Substitute the values:

$$T_{prog} = (200)(0.1 \, \mu s) + (80)(1.2 \, \mu s) + (50)(1.0 \, \mu s) + (40)(1.5 \, \mu s)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product term individually.**

$$200 \times 0.1 \, \mu s = 20.0 \, \mu s$$

$$80 \times 1.2 \, \mu s = 96.0 \, \mu s$$

$$50 \times 1.0 \, \mu s = 50.0 \, \mu s$$

$$40 \times 1.5 \, \mu s = 60.0 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Sum the four products to obtain $T_{prog}$.**

$$T_{prog} = 20.0 + 96.0 + 50.0 + 60.0 = 226.0 \, \mu s$$

Convert to milliseconds for unit consistency:

$$T_{prog} = 226.0 \, \mu s = 0.226 \, ms$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all values into the scan-time equation.**

$$T_{scan} = 1.5 \, ms + 0.226 \, ms + 1.0 \, ms + 0.8 \, ms$$

**Step 6 — Perform the final addition.**

$$T_{scan} = 3.526 \, ms$$

**Step 7 — Compute the maximum scan frequency (throughput).**

$$f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{3.526 \times 10^{-3} \, s}$$

$$f_{scan} \approx 283.6 \, \text{scans/second} = 283.6 \, \text{Hz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A scan time of **3.526 ms** allows this PLC to process inputs/outputs roughly **283 times per second**. For most discrete-event applications (conveyors, packaging), this is more than adequate (industry benchmark: 10–20 ms is acceptable for discrete logic).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Digital Response Time

**Problem:** A limit switch is connected to a digital input of a PLC whose scan time is $T_{scan} = 3.526 \, ms$. The output module has a propagation delay $t_{module} = 0.5 \, ms$. Find the **worst-case** time between the switch closure and the corresponding output energization.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recognize the worst-case condition.**

The switch can close *just after* the input scan has finished, so the CPU will only see it on the *next* scan. This is the classic "missed-scan" scenario.

**Step 2 — Worst-case input latency.**

$$t_{input,\,latency} = T_{scan} = 3.526 \, ms$$

**Step 3 — Add the output module propagation delay.**

$$T_{response,\,worst} = t_{input,\,latency} + t_{module}$$

$$T_{response,\,worst} = 3.526 + 0.5$$

$$T_{response,\,worst} = 4.026 \, ms$$

**Step 4 — Best-case scenario (for comparison).**

If the switch closes *just before* the input scan, the input is read immediately, the program executes, and the output is written at the end of the same cycle:

$$T_{response,\,best} = t_{module} = 0.5 \, ms$$

**Step 5 — Average response time.**

$$T_{response,\,avg} = \frac{T_{best} + T_{worst}}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 4.026}{2} = 2.263 \, ms$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** When asked for "response time" of a PLC, default to the **worst-case = 1 scan + module delay** unless otherwise specified. State this assumption clearly in the answer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ladder Logic Implementation of a Classic Problem

**Problem:** A conveyor belt has a START pushbutton (I0.0), a STOP pushbutton (I0.1), and a thermal overload (I0.2). It drives a motor through output Q0.0. Implement the ladder logic and write the boolean equation.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Latch (Seal) Logic Design.**

The motor must be energized when START is pressed AND the overload is normal AND the motor is currently OFF. Once started, it should hold itself ON (latch) even after the START button is released. The STOP button must break the circuit.

**Step 2 — Boolean Equation (Sum-of-Products).**

$$Q0.0 = (I0.0 \cdot \overline{I0.1} \cdot \overline{I0.2}) + (Q0.0 \cdot \overline{I0.1} \cdot \overline{I0.2})$$

**Step 3 — Simplified Form (Factoring the Safety Conditions).**

$$Q0.0 = \overline{I0.1} \cdot \overline{I0.2} \cdot (I0.0 + Q0.0)$$

The terms $\overline{I0.1}$ (NOT STOP) and $\overline{I0.2}$ (NOT OVERLOAD) are **normally-closed (NC) safety conditions** in the ladder.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pearl:** Always implement STOP and OVERLOAD as **Normally-Closed** contacts in ladder logic. This way, a *broken wire* (fail-safe) automatically stops the motor — a critical industrial safety practice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Sizing for an Application

**Problem:** A bottling line PLC application requires:
- 4 KB of user program (ladder logic)
- 256 digital input points
- 128 digital output points
- 32 analog input channels (16-bit each)
- 16 analog output channels (16-bit each)
- 50 timer words
- 50 counter words

Find the total data memory requirement.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Digital I/O Image Memory.**

Digital I/O uses **1 bit per point**, grouped in 16-bit words.

$$\text{Digital Input Words} = \frac{256}{16} = 16 \text{ words}$$

$$\text{Digital Output Words} = \frac{128}{16} = 8 \text{ words}$$

**Step 2 — Analog I/O Image Memory.**

Each analog channel occupies **1 word (16 bits)**.

$$\text{Analog Input Words} = 32 \times 1 = 32 \text{ words}$$

$$\text{Analog Output Words} = 16 \times 1 = 16 \text{ words}$$

**Step 3 — Timer and Counter Memory.**

$$\text{Timer/Counter Words} = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ words}$$

**Step 4 — Total Data Memory in Words.**

$$M_{data,\,words} = 16 + 8 + 32 + 16 + 100 = 172 \text{ words}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Bytes (1 word = 2 bytes).**

$$M_{data,\,bytes} = 172 \times 2 = 344 \text{ bytes} = 0.344 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 6 — Add Program Memory.**

$$M_{total} = M_{prog} + M_{data} = 4 \text{ KB} + 0.344 \text{ KB} = 4.344 \text{ KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This small footprint confirms why modern PLCs (with MB-level memories) can handle huge applications, leaving ample headroom for future expansion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PLC

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a generic PLC, showing data, power, and signal flow between the major sub-systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V] --> B[Power Supply Unit PSU]
    B --> C[CPU Microprocessor ALU and Control Unit]
    D[ROM and Flash Firmware OS] --> C
    E[Battery Backed RAM User Program and Data] <--> C
    F[Programming Device PC or HHP] <--> C
    G[Communication Ports RS485 and Ethernet] <--> C
    C --> H[Input Module DI and AI]
    C --> I[Output Module DO and AO]
    H --> J[Field Sensors Limit Switch and Thermocouple]
    I --> K[Field Actuators Motor and Solenoid]
    C --> L[HMI and SCADA Interface]
    L --> M[Operator Display Panel]
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- The **PSU** receives mains AC and supplies isolated DC to the CPU, I/O modules, and field devices.
- The **CPU** is the central hub — it exchanges data with the **programming device** and **communication ports** for upload/download and networking.
- **Memory** is tiered: ROM holds firmware, battery-backed RAM holds the live user logic and image tables.
- **Input Module** digitizes/buffers sensor signals; **Output Module** drives actuators with required voltage/current.

---

## 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle Flowchart

This flowchart depicts the four-phase scan cycle, including the *decision branches* for STOP mode and the *continuous loop* in RUN mode.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLC Power ON] --> B[Initialization and Self Test]
    B --> C{Operating Mode}
    C -->|STOP| D[Hold Outputs OFF and Wait for RUN]
    D --> C
    C -->|RUN| E[Phase 1 Read Physical Inputs to PII]
    E --> F[Phase 2 Execute User Program Ladder Logic]
    F --> G[Phase 3 Write PIQ to Physical Outputs]
    G --> H[Phase 4 Housekeeping Comm and Diagnostics]
    H --> E
```

### Diagram Interpretation

- After power-on, the PLC performs a self-test (memory check, I/O handshake).
- If the mode selector is in STOP, the outputs are held off and the CPU idles.
- In RUN mode, the four-phase cycle loops indefinitely, with a total cycle time = scan time.

---

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Schematic — Motor Start/Stop with Overload

The diagram below shows a *ladder rung* in graphical form (left) and its equivalent ladder-logic schematic using the Mermaid flowchart notation (right).

**Boolean Equation:** $Q0.0 = \overline{I0.1} \cdot \overline{I0.2} \cdot (I0.0 + Q0.0)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Left Power Rail L] --> N1[NC STOP I0.1] --> N2[NC OVERLOAD I0.2] --> N3{NO START I0.0] --> O1[Output Coil Motor Q0.0] --> L2[Right Power Rail N]
    N2 --> P1[NO Auxiliary Contact Q0.0 Latch]
    P1 --> N3
```

### Rung Interpretation (Left-to-Right Scan)

1. Current flows from **L** (line/hot rail) to **N** (neutral rail) when all contacts in series are closed.
2. The **STOP** (NC) and **OVERLOAD** (NC) contacts must be closed (buttons not pressed, no fault).
3. The **START** (NO) contact closes when pressed, OR the **AUXILIARY** contact (feedback from Q0.0) keeps the rung latched.
4. The **OUTPUT COIL** Q0.0 energizes the motor contactor, which physically powers the motor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a Programmable Logic Controller. List any four advantages of PLCs over conventional relay-based control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A PLC is a microprocessor-based, solid-state, industrial digital computer that uses programmable memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control industrial processes through its I/O interfaces.

**Any four advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

1. **Programming flexibility** — Logic changes via software, not rewiring.
2. **High reliability** — Solid-state, no moving parts; MTBF > 100,000 hours.
3. **Compact size** — Replaces hundreds of relays in a small footprint.
4. **Ease of troubleshooting** — Built-in diagnostics, status LEDs, online monitoring.
5. **Lower long-term cost** — Cheaper to modify and maintain.
6. **Communication capability** — Easily interfaces with HMIs, SCADA, and other controllers.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Explain the four phases of the PLC scan cycle with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The PLC scan cycle consists of four sequential phases executed repetitively in RUN mode:

1. **Input Scan (Read):** The CPU reads the status of all physical input devices and stores them in the **Process Image Input (PII) table** in RAM. *(0.75 mark)*

2. **Program Scan (Execute):** The CPU executes the user program rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom, using the PII values. Results are stored in the **Process Image Output (PIQ) table**. *(0.75 mark)*

3. **Output Scan (Write):** The CPU transfers the PIQ values to the physical output terminals, energizing/de-energizing the actuators. *(0.75 mark)*

4. **Housekeeping:** The CPU performs communication tasks, timer/counter updates, and self-diagnostics. The cycle then loops back to Phase 1. *(0.75 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students write only *three* phases, omitting **housekeeping**. KTU examiners specifically check for the fourth phase — losing it costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a PLC and explain the functions of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the operation of a PLC scan cycle. A PLC program has 500 logic instructions, 100 timer instructions, and 50 counter instructions. Instruction times are: logic = 0.08 µs, timer = 1.0 µs, counter = 0.9 µs. Input scan = 2 ms, output scan = 1.5 ms, housekeeping = 1 ms. Calculate the total scan time and scan frequency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram and Functions — 7 Marks**

*(Block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 — 2 marks; Explanation of each block — 5 marks @ 1 mark each)*

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **CPU** | Microprocessor + ALU + control unit; executes user program, manages memory and I/O |
| **Memory (RAM/ROM)** | ROM stores OS; RAM (battery-backed) stores user program and image tables |
| **Power Supply** | Converts AC mains to regulated DC (5 V / 24 V) for internal electronics and field I/O |
| **Input Module** | Receives signals from sensors (digital/analog), provides isolation and signal conditioning |
| **Output Module** | Drives actuators (relays, solenoids, VFDs) with required voltage/current; provides isolation |
| **Programming Device** | PC/HHP with vendor software to write, download, monitor, and debug logic |
| **Communication Port** | Interfaces with HMIs, other PLCs, SCADA, and field devices over industrial networks |

**(b) Scan Cycle and Calculation — 7 Marks**

**Scan cycle sketch** *(Refer to Section 4.2 — 2 marks for neat diagram with all 4 phases)*

**Calculation — 5 marks:**

**Step 1 — Program execution time:**

$$T_{prog} = (500 \times 0.08) + (100 \times 1.0) + (50 \times 0.9) \, \mu s$$

$$T_{prog} = 40 + 100 + 45 = 185 \, \mu s = 0.185 \, ms$$

**Step 2 — Total scan time:**

$$T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{ovh}$$

$$T_{scan} = 2.0 + 0.185 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 4.685 \, ms$$

**Step 3 — Scan frequency:**

$$f_{scan} = \frac{1}{4.685 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 213.4 \, \text{Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Students commonly write $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ — **forgetting housekeeping** ($T_{ovh}$). In KTU 2024 scheme valuation, this omission typically costs **1 mark** out of 7 in part (b).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare PLC, microcontroller, and DCS based on any seven parameters. Explain the role of PLC in a mechatronic system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a ladder logic program for the following bottling plant: A conveyor (driven by motor M1) is started by a START pushbutton (PB1) and stopped by STOP (PB2) or by a bottle-jam sensor (S1). A green lamp (L1) indicates running state, and a red lamp (L2) indicates jam/fault. Show the ladder diagram and write the boolean equations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison and Role — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table — 5 marks** (any 7 parameters × 0.5 mark = 3.5 marks, plus 1.5 for explanation):

| Parameter | PLC | Microcontroller | DCS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Environment | Industrial harsh | Embedded product | Plant-wide |
| Programming | Ladder, FBD, ST | C, Assembly | Function blocks, SAMA |
| I/O Capability | Hundreds–thousands | Dozens | Thousands+ |
| Reliability (MTBF) | 100,000+ hrs | 50,000 hrs | 200,000+ hrs |
| Cost (per I/O) | Medium | Low | High |
| Real-time Behavior | Deterministic (scan) | RTOS-dependent | Deterministic |
| Scalability | Modular | Fixed | Highly scalable |
| Use Case | Discrete control | Consumer products | Continuous process |

**Role in Mechatronic System — 2 marks:**

The PLC acts as the **control kernel** that bridges the *mechanical* and *electronic* domains of a mechatronic system. It:
1. Acquires signals from sensors (electronic) monitoring mechanical parameters.
2. Executes stored control logic to compute decisions.
3. Sends commands to actuators (electronic/motor drives) that act on the mechanical system.
4. Communicates with HMIs and supervisory layers (computer/software domain).

> Thus, the PLC is the **central nervous system** of a mechatronic system, integrating all four core disciplines.

**(b) Ladder Logic Design — 7 Marks**

**Rung 1 — Motor Control with Latch and Safety (3 marks):**

$$M1 = \overline{PB2} \cdot \overline{S1} \cdot (PB1 + M1)$$

**Rung 2 — Green Lamp (Running Indicator) (2 marks):**

$$L1 = M1$$

**Rung 3 — Red Lamp (Jam/Fault Indicator) (2 marks):**

$$L2 = S1$$

**Ladder Diagram Representation:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1L[Left Rail L] --> R1N1[NC PB2 Stop] --> R1N2[NC S1 Jam Sensor] --> R1N3[NO PB1 Start] --> R1O[Motor Coil M1] --> R1N[Right Rail N]
    R1N2 --> R1AUX[NO M1 Auxiliary Latch]
    R1AUX --> R1N3
    R2L[Left Rail L] --> R2N[NO M1 Contact] --> R2O[Green Lamp L1] --> R2N2[Right Rail N]
    R3L[Left Rail L] --> R3N[NO S1 Contact] --> R3O[Red Lamp L2] --> R3N2[Right Rail N]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** Two common mistakes in ladder-logic design questions:
> 1. Drawing **STOP and jam sensor as NO** (Normally-Open) — they **must be NC** for fail-safe operation. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 2. Forgetting the **auxiliary latch contact** in parallel with the START button — without it, the motor cannot self-hold. *(Lose 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Must-Know Definitions
- ☐ PLC = solid-state, microprocessor-based, programmable industrial controller
- ☐ Scan cycle = Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping
- ☐ PII = Process Image Input table; PIQ = Process Image Output table
- ☐ Ladder logic = graphical programming language (rungs, contacts, coils)
- ☐ NC (Normally-Closed) vs NO (Normally-Open) contacts

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{ovh}$
- ☐ $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$
- ☐ $T_{prog} = \sum N_i \cdot t_i$
- ☐ $T_{response,\,worst} = T_{scan} + t_{module}$

### Must-Know Components of a PLC
- ☐ CPU, Memory (ROM/RAM/Flash), PSU, I/O Modules, Programming Device, Communication Port

### Must-Know Industrial Practices
- ☐ STOP and safety contacts drawn as **NC (fail-safe)**
- ☐ Standard signal = **4–20 mA** (live-zero for fault detection)
- ☐ Outputs held OFF in **STOP mode**; re-energized in **RUN mode**
- ☐ Inputs read into PII *once per cycle* (deterministic behavior)

### Must-Know Differences
- ☐ PLC vs Relay — programmable, reliable, compact, communicative
- ☐ PLC vs Microcontroller — industrial vs embedded, modular vs fixed
- ☐ PLC vs DCS — discrete vs continuous process, single vs distributed
- ☐ PLC vs PC — real-time, rugged vs general-purpose, office-grade

### Common KTU 3-Mark Question Patterns
- ☐ Define PLC + list advantages (1 + 2 marks)
- ☐ Explain scan cycle with diagram (3 marks)
- ☐ Draw block diagram + functions (3 marks)
- ☐ Compare PLC with another controller (3 marks)

### Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns
- ☐ Part (a): Block diagram + component explanation (7 marks)
- ☐ Part (b): Scan time calculation + frequency (7 marks)
- ☐ OR: Ladder logic design for industrial application (7 marks)

### Last-Minute Tips
- ☐ Always **state assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming worst-case response")
- ☐ Always **show unit conversions** (µs → ms)
- ☐ Always **label diagrams** clearly (PII, PIQ, NC, NO, etc.)
- ☐ Always **mention fail-safe** in safety-related design
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/output and communication systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Input/output and communication systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Input/Output (I/O) and Communication Systems** constitute the interfacing backbone of any mechatronic system. They are responsible for the **acquisition**, **conditioning**, **transformation**, and **transmission** of data between the physical (real-world) domain and the digital (controller/processor) domain.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, this module is formally defined as the study of:

> **"The hardware and protocol architecture that allows sensors (input), actuators (output), controllers, and human-machine interfaces to exchange information reliably, accurately, and in real-time."**

The four functional pillars of this module are:

| Pillar | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Conditioning** | Prepares raw transducer signals | Amplification, filtering |
| **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** | Converts analog → digital | ADC, sampling, quantization |
| **Wired Communication** | Transfers data over physical media | CAN, I2C, SPI, RS-485 |
| **Wireless Communication** | Transfers data over RF/Optical media | Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ZigBee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Examiners explicitly test the distinction between **synchronous** and **asynchronous** communication, the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, and the **OSI/7-layer model** mapped to industrial fieldbuses.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a mechatronic system as a **multilingual international office**:

- **Sensors** are like *foreign visitors* speaking different languages (voltage, current, resistance, frequency).
- **Signal conditioning** is the *interpreter* who makes their language uniform and clear.
- **The controller** is the *manager* sitting in a glass cabin who only understands a single digital dialect.
- **Communication buses** are the *courier networks* (postal service, telephone, email) carrying memos between the manager, visitors, and the outside world.

**Without proper I/O and communication**, the visitors cannot reach the manager, and the manager cannot issue commands — the entire office collapses. This is precisely why this module is the *"nervous system"* of mechatronics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Data Flow Direction:**
> - **Input (I):** Sensor → Controller (sensing the world)
> - **Output (O):** Controller → Actuator (acting on the world)
> - **Feedback:** Output result → Sensor (closing the loop)

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standards govern I/O and communication in industrial mechatronics:

- **Nyquist Minimum Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (where $f_{max}$ is the highest signal frequency component)
- **Standard Industrial Voltage Levels:** **0–10 V**, **4–20 mA** (current loop is preferred for noise immunity)
- **Standard Logic Voltage Levels:** **TTL (5 V)**, **CMOS (3.3 V / 5 V)**, **LVTTL (1.8 V / 2.5 V)**
- **CAN Bus Standard:** **ISO 11898** (up to **1 Mbps** at 40 m, **125 kbps** at 500 m)
- **RS-485 Standard:** Up to **10 Mbps** over **1200 m** with **32 nodes** per segment
- **TCP/IP Industrial Standard:** **EtherNet/IP**, **PROFINET**, **EtherCAT**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Logic Levels Table** (memorize for KTU):
> 
> | Standard | $\mathbf{V_{OH}(\text{min})}$ | $\mathbf{V_{OL}(\text{max})}$ | $\mathbf{V_{IH}(\text{min})}$ | $\mathbf{V_{IL}(\text{max})}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | TTL (5V) | 2.4 V | 0.4 V | 2.0 V | 0.8 V |
> | CMOS (5V) | 4.99 V | 0.01 V | 3.5 V | 1.5 V |
> | LVTTL (3.3V) | 2.4 V | 0.4 V | 2.0 V | 0.8 V |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Signals (Foundation Concept)

Before any I/O system is designed, the engineer must classify the input signal:

1. **Analog Signal** — Continuous in time and amplitude (e.g., thermocouple voltage, strain gauge).
2. **Digital Signal** — Discrete in both time and amplitude (e.g., 0/5 V from a switch).
3. **Discrete (Sampled) Signal** — Continuous in amplitude, discrete in time.
4. **Quantized Signal** — Discrete in amplitude, continuous in time.

The transformation **Analog → Digital** is the heart of the I/O subsystem and is performed by an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** via two stages: **Sampling** and **Quantization**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The I/O Subsystem — Operational Logic Flow

A mechatronic I/O chain executes a deterministic sequence:

**Step 1: Physical Sensing** — Transducer converts physical quantity (T, P, F, V) into an electrical signal (V or I).
*Why:* The processor cannot perceive heat, force, or light directly.
*How:* Sensors exploit physical effects (Seebeck, piezoelectric, photoelectric).

**Step 2: Signal Conditioning** — The raw signal is amplified, filtered, linearized, and isolated.
*Why:* Sensor outputs are typically low-amplitude (mV, µA) and noise-corrupted.
*How:* Instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128), active filters (Sallen-Key, Multiple-Feedback).

**Step 3: Multiplexing (Optional)** — Multiple channels share one ADC for cost efficiency.
*Why:* ADCs are expensive; sharing one reduces hardware count.
*How:* Analog multiplexer (MUX) such as CD4051, ADG708.

**Step 4: Analog-to-Digital Conversion** — Continuous analog → discrete binary word.
*Why:* Microcontrollers operate on binary logic.
*How:* Successive Approximation (SAR), Flash, Sigma-Delta ADCs.

**Step 5: Digital Processing** — The MCU/DSP/PLC interprets the binary word using algorithms.
*Why:* Useful information must be extracted (e.g., averaging, FFT, PID).

**Step 6: Digital-to-Analog Conversion (Output Path)** — Binary word → analog control signal.
*How:* R-2R Ladder, Weighted Resistor, PWM-filtered DACs.

**Step 7: Power Amplification & Actuation** — Low-power signal → high-power actuator drive.
*How:* H-bridge, servo driver, VFD, solenoid driver.

**Step 8: Communication** — Bidirectional data exchange with other subsystems, HMIs, or remote stations.

---

### 2.2 Communication Systems — Theoretical Classification

#### A) Parallel vs Serial Communication

| Feature | Parallel | Serial |
|---|---|---|
| Lines used | $n$ lines for $n$ bits | 1 (or 2) lines |
| Speed | High (short distance) | Slower (per bit) but high throughput possible |
| Distance | Short (< 1 m) | Long (km with RS-485) |
| Cost | High (many wires) | Low |
| Skew | Problematic at high speed | Negligible |
| Examples | PCI, ISA, IEEE-488 (GPIB) | UART, SPI, I2C, USB, CAN |

#### B) Synchronous vs Asynchronous Serial

- **Asynchronous (UART, RS-232):** No shared clock. Data framed with **start bit, data bits (5–9), parity bit, stop bit(s)**. Both ends must pre-agree on **baud rate** (bits per second).
- **Synchronous (SPI, I2C):** Shared clock line. Higher throughput, no start/stop overhead, requires an extra wire.

#### C) Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex

- **Simplex:** One-way only (e.g., radio broadcast, sensor → controller).
- **Half-Duplex:** Both ways, but not simultaneously (e.g., walkie-talkie, single-wire CAN).
- **Full-Duplex:** Both ways simultaneously (e.g., SPI with MOSI+MISO, RS-232 with TX+RX).

#### D) Master-Slave vs Multi-Master

- **Master-Slave (I2C, SPI):** One master initiates all transfers; slaves are passive.
- **Multi-Master (CAN, Ethernet):** Any node can initiate; collision detection/arbitration required.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is exam-critical. Every formula must be memorized with units.**

| # | Formula / Theorem | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling rate (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency (Hz) | Minimum ADC sampling rate to avoid **aliasing** |
| 2 | **Quantization Step Size** | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage (V), $n$ = ADC bits | Resolution of ADC |
| 3 | **Quantization Error (RMS)** | $e_{q,RMS} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}$ | $Q$ in V | Theoretical noise floor of ideal ADC |
| 4 | **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | $n$ = number of bits | SNR of an ideal N-bit ADC |
| 5 | **Signal Bandwidth from Rise Time** | $BW \approx \dfrac{0.35}{t_r}$ | $t_r$ = 10–90% rise time (s) | Estimate signal bandwidth for sampling |
| 6 | **Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = bit rate (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = power ratio | Max error-free data rate |
| 7 | **Baud Rate to Bit Rate** | $R_b = R_{baud} \times \log_2(M)$ | $M$ = number of signal levels | For multi-level encoding (e.g., M-ary) |
| 8 | **4–20 mA Loop Compliance Voltage** | $V_{compliance} = V_{supply} - I_{max} \cdot R_{load}$ | All in V and Ω | Verify loop can drive 20 mA |
| 9 | **Aliasing Frequency** | $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{in} \vert$ | All in Hz | Identify foldover components |
| 10 | **Propagation Delay (Bus)** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_p}$ | $d$ = distance (m), $v_p$ ≈ 2×10⁸ m/s in cable | Timing budget for long buses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Constant for Exam:**
> The propagation speed of signal in a typical copper cable is $v_p \approx 0.66\,c \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (because of the dielectric constant of insulation).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

I/O and communication systems are omnipresent in modern industry:

- **Automotive:** A modern car has **70+ ECUs** connected via **CAN bus** (engine, ABS, airbag, infotainment).
- **Factory Automation:** **PROFINET** and **EtherCAT** provide real-time Ethernet control of robotic arms.
- **Medical Devices:** **SPI** interfaces connect ECG ADCs to microcontrollers in patient monitors.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** **I2C** links temperature/humidity sensors to ARM Cortex-M processors.
- **Aerospace:** **ARINC 429** and **MIL-STD-1553** are dual-redundant serial buses for avionics.
- **Process Control:** **4–20 mA current loop** transmits process variables (pressure, flow) over kilometers of plant cabling with full noise immunity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why 4–20 mA and not 0–20 mA?**
> A 4 mA "live zero" allows the receiver to distinguish between a **true zero signal** and a **broken wire (0 mA)**. This is a fault-detection feature mandated in **ISA-50.1** and **IEC 60381** standards.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Error and SQNR

**Problem Setup:**
Consider an ideal $N$-bit unipolar ADC with full-scale input range $V_{FS}$ (volts). We wish to derive the **theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)**.

**Step 1 — Step Size (Resolution):**
The ADC divides the full-scale range into $2^N$ equally spaced levels. The voltage represented by one LSB (Least Significant Bit) is:

$$
Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}
$$

**Step 2 — Quantization Error Model:**
Assuming the input is uniformly distributed across the full range, the instantaneous quantization error $e_q(t)$ is a **uniform random variable** in the interval $\left[-\dfrac{Q}{2},\,+\dfrac{Q}{2}\right]$.

**Step 3 — Mean Square Value of Uniform Distribution:**
For a uniform distribution over width $Q$, the variance (mean square value) is:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{Q^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — RMS Quantization Noise:**
Taking the square root:

$$
e_{q,RMS} = \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

**Step 5 — RMS Signal Power:**
For a full-scale sinusoidal input $v(t) = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2}\sin(\omega t)$, the RMS value is $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}$. Its mean square (power into 1 Ω) is:

$$
v_{RMS}^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}
$$

**Step 6 — SQNR (Linear Ratio):**

$$
SQNR = \frac{v_{RMS}^2}{\overline{e_q^2}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{Q^2/12}
$$

Substituting $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$:

$$
SQNR = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8} \times \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{V_{FS}^2}
$$

Cancel $V_{FS}^2$:

$$
SQNR = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Decibels:**

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20N\log_{10}(2)
$$

Evaluating the constants: $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ dB and $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ dB.

$$
\boxed{\,SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02\,N + 1.76\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by **6.02 dB** (a doubling of voltage resolution). This is the famous "6 dB per bit" rule.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 12-bit ADC in a Temperature Logger

**Given:**
- ADC resolution: $N = 12$ bits
- Full-scale voltage: $V_{FS} = 5.0$ V
- Input signal: $v_{in}(t) = 2.5 \sin(100\pi t)$ V (50 Hz sine)

**Find:** (a) Quantization step size, (b) Quantization noise RMS, (c) SQNR, (d) Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.

**Step (a) — Step Size:**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5.0}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.0}{4096} \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 1.221\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step (b) — Quantization Noise RMS:**

$$
e_{q,RMS} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{1.221 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} \approx 3.524 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} = 352.4\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**Step (c) — SQNR:**

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\,\text{dB}
$$

(As a ratio: $10^{74/10} \approx 2.51 \times 10^7$.)

**Step (d) — Nyquist Minimum Sampling Rate:**
The signal frequency is $f_{max} = 50$ Hz, so:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 100\,\text{Hz}
$$

In practice, engineers use **oversampling** ($f_s \geq 10 f_{max}$) to leave margin against real-world filter roll-off. So $f_s = 1$ kHz is a robust choice.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Communication Protocols

This matrix is the **single most-examined comparative table** in the KTU ESE for this module.

| Protocol | Topology | Wires | Max Speed | Max Nodes | Max Distance | Duplex | Encoding | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UART / RS-232** | Point-to-point | 2 (TX,RX) + GND | 115.2 kbps (typ.) 1 Mbps (max) | 1 pair | $\leq$ 15 m | Full | NRZ | PC ↔ Microcontroller debug |
| **RS-485** | Multi-drop bus | 2 (A,B) twisted pair | 10 Mbps | 32 (256 w/ repeaters) | 1200 m | Half | Differential NRZ | Industrial, Modbus RTU |
| **I²C** | Multi-master bus | 2 (SDA,SCL) | 400 kbps (Fast) 3.4 Mbps (Hs) | 127 (8-bit addr) | $\leq$ 10 m (typ.) | Half | NRZ + clock | Sensor interconnects on PCB |
| **SPI** | Master-slave star | 4 (MOSI,MISO,SCK,CS) | $\geq$ 50 Mbps | Limited by CS pins | $\leq$ 3 m | Full | NRZ | High-speed ADC, Flash, Display |
| **CAN 2.0B** | Multi-master bus | 2 (CANH,CANL) | 1 Mbps | 2^29 identifiers | 5 km (10 kbps) | Half | NRZ + bit stuffing | Automotive, Industrial |
| **USB 2.0** | Host-device tree | 4 (VBUS,D+,D-,GND) | 480 Mbps | 127 (tiered) | 5 m | Half | NRZI + bit stuffing | PC peripherals |
| **Ethernet (100BASE-TX)** | Star / Switched | 4 (2 twisted pairs) | 100 Mbps | Unlimited (IP-based) | 100 m | Full | MLT-3 / 4B5B | Office / Industrial LAN |
| **Bluetooth LE** | Piconet (star) | Wireless 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps | 1 master + 7 slaves | $\leq$ 100 m | Half | GFSK | Wearables, IoT sensors |

---

### 3.4 Bit Timing Calculation for CAN Bus (Derivation Walk-Through)

**Given:** CAN bus bit rate $R = 500\,\text{kbps}$ (typical for industrial machinery).

**Step 1 — Bit Time:**
The duration of one bit is:

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2.0\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Nominal Bit Time Partition:**
CAN divides each bit into **4 non-overlapping segments**:
- Sync Segment: $T_{SYNC} = 1 \cdot T_Q$
- Propagation Segment: $T_{PROP} = 2 \cdot T_Q$
- Phase Segment 1: $T_{PS1} = 3 \cdot T_Q$
- Phase Segment 2: $T_{PS2} = 3 \cdot T_Q$

Total Time Quanta: $N_{TQ} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 3 = 9$

**Step 3 — Time Quanta Duration:**

$$
T_Q = \frac{T_{bit}}{N_{TQ}} = \frac{2.0\,\mu\text{s}}{9} \approx 222.2\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Required CAN Clock Frequency:**

$$
f_{CAN\_CLK} = \frac{1}{T_Q} \approx 4.5\,\text{MHz}
$$

This is typically derived from a higher system clock (e.g., 72 MHz APB1 in STM32) through a **Baud Rate Prescaler (BRP)** of 16.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks *"Calculate the CAN bit timing for a given bus speed"* or *"Why does CAN use non-return-to-zero with bit stuffing?"* — the bit stuffing prevents long runs of identical bits, which would otherwise defeat the **resynchronization** mechanism.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Mechatronic I/O Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the full signal path from physical world to controller and back to actuator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph physicalWorld["Physical World"]
        env["Environment Variable (T, P, F, V)"]
        actuator["Mechanical Actuator (Motor, Valve, Heater)"]
    end

    subgraph inputChain["Input / Acquisition Chain"]
        sensor["Sensor / Transducer"]
        sigcon["Signal Conditioner (Amp + Filter + Linearize)"]
        mux["Analog Multiplexer MUX"]
        adc["Analog-to-Digital Converter ADC"]
    end

    subgraph ctrlCore["Controller Core"]
        mcu["Microcontroller / DSP / PLC"]
        daq["DAQ Firmware (Sampling, Averaging)"]
        commsTx["Communication TX Module"]
    end

    subgraph commBus["Communication Bus"]
        bus["RS-485 / CAN / I2C / SPI / Ethernet"]
    end

    subgraph outputChain["Output / Actuation Chain"]
        dac["Digital-to-Analog Converter DAC"]
        driver["Power Driver (H-Bridge, SSR, VFD)"]
        pwm["PWM Generator"]
    end

    subgraph remoteEnd["Remote End / HMI"]
        hmi["HMI / SCADA / Cloud"]
    end

    env --> sensor
    sensor --> sigcon
    sigcon --> mux
    mux --> adc
    adc --> daq
    daq --> mcu
    mcu --> commsTx
    commsTx --> bus
    bus --> hmi
    bus --> mcu

    mcu --> pwm
    pwm --> driver
    mcu --> dac
    dac --> driver
    driver --> actuator
    actuator -.Feedback.-> env
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Communication Protocol Stack Mapping (OSI)

This diagram maps the **7-layer OSI model** to industrial mechatronic protocols. Useful for KTU 14-mark comparative answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Layer 7: Application\nModbus, CANopen, PROFINET, EtherCAT"]
    L6["Layer 6: Presentation\nData encoding (ASCII, JSON, OPC-UA)"]
    L5["Layer 5: Session\nConnection management (TCP)"]
    L4["Layer 4: Transport\nTCP, UDP, CAN TP"]
    L3["Layer 3: Network\nIP addressing, routing"]
    L2["Layer 2: Data Link\nCAN MAC, Ethernet CSMA/CD"]
    L1["Layer 1: Physical\nVoltage levels, cables, connectors"]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

**Interpretation:** Different protocols implement only specific layers:
- **CAN 2.0B:** Implements **Layer 1 + Layer 2** (physical + data link). Higher layers built by **CANopen (CiA 301)** or **DeviceNet**.
- **EtherCAT / PROFINET:** Full OSI stack with real-time extensions.
- **Modbus RTU over RS-485:** Layer 1 (RS-485) + Layer 7 (Modbus application layer).

---

### 4.3 Schematic — 4–20 mA Current Loop Transmitter (Conceptual)

A standard 2-wire current loop used in process control instrumentation:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor["Sensor / Transducer\n(Variable Resistance or Voltage)"]
    xtr["XTR105 / XTR111\nV-to-I Converter IC"]
    loopA["Wire A (Loop +)"]
    resistor["250 Ω Sense Resistor\nat Receiver"]
    adcR["Receiver ADC / 1–5 V Input"]
    loopB["Wire B (Loop Return)"]
    supply["24 V DC Power Supply"]

    supply --> loopA
    loopA --> xtr
    xtr --> sensor
    xtr --> loopB
    loopB --> supply
    loopA --> resistor
    resistor --> adcR
```

**Operation:** The XTR (or equivalent) IC forces a current of **4 mA** at sensor minimum and **20 mA** at sensor maximum, regardless of wire resistance. The receiver drops $1\text{ V}$ to $5\text{ V}$ across the 250 Ω resistor, suitable for direct ADC input.

| Topology | Cable Use | Fault Tolerance | Cost | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | High (each node → hub) | Excellent (single cable break OK) | Highest | Ethernet LAN |
| **Bus** | Low (shared cable) | Poor (single break disables all) | Lowest | RS-485, CAN, I²C |
| **Ring** | Moderate | Moderate (one break disables chain) | Moderate | Token Ring (legacy), Fieldbus |
| **Mesh** | Highest (redundant) | Excellent | Highest | Industrial wireless, ZigBee |
