## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
> **Q1. Define the term "Signal Conditioning" and list any four operations performed on a raw sensor signal in a mechatronic system.**

**Model Answer:**
Signal conditioning is the process of manipulating a raw transducer output into a form suitable for the next stage of processing (typically an ADC or transmission line). The four most common operations are:

1. **Amplification** — Increasing low-amplitude signals (e.g., mV-level thermocouple output → 0–5 V).
2. **Filtering** — Removing high-frequency noise (low-pass) or 50/60 Hz mains interference (notch).
3. **Linearization** — Correcting non-linear sensor response (e.g., thermistor exponential curve).
4. **Isolation** — Galvanic isolation using optocouplers or transformers to protect downstream electronics from high-voltage transients.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any 2 operations + 1 mark for any 2 more operations = 3)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex communication. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Description | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | Data flows in **one direction only** | TV broadcasting, sensor → controller telemetry line |
| **Half-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions, but not simultaneously** | Walkie-talkie, **CAN bus** (single twisted pair) |
| **Full-Duplex** | Data flows in **both directions simultaneously** | **SPI** (MOSI + MISO), RS-232 (separate TX and RX lines), Ethernet |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Question A]**
> **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Data Acquisition System (DAQ)**. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 16-bit ADC is used to digitize a 0–10 V analog input signal. Calculate: (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the RMS quantization noise, and (iii) the theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

**Part (a) — DAQ Architecture (7 marks):**

The Data Acquisition System converts real-world analog signals into digital form for processing. The block diagram is:

```
Sensor → Amplifier → Low-Pass Filter → Sample-and-Hold → Multiplexer → ADC → Buffer/Memory → Controller (PC/MCU)
```

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Converts physical quantity (T, P, V) into electrical signal (V or mV) |
| **Amplifier** | Boosts low-amplitude signal (e.g., INA128 instrumentation amplifier) |
| **Low-Pass Filter** | Anti-aliasing filter; restricts input bandwidth to $f_{max} \leq f_s/2$ (e.g., 4th-order Butterworth) |
| **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** | Holds the input voltage constant during ADC conversion to avoid aperture error |
| **Multiplexer** | Switches multiple analog channels sequentially to a single ADC (e.g., CD4051) |
| **ADC** | Converts held analog voltage to binary word (SAR, Flash, or Sigma-Delta) |
| **Buffer Memory** | Stores digital data temporarily (FIFO) for transfer to the controller |
| **Controller** | Receives digital data, processes it (display, logging, PID control) |

*(Marking: 1 mark block diagram + 1 mark functions table → 7 marks distributed)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 16$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V

**Step (i) — Quantization Step Size:**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{16}} = \frac{10}{65{,}536} \approx 1.526 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} = 152.6\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**Step (ii) — RMS Quantization Noise:**

$$
e_{q,RMS} = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{1.526 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4641} \approx 4.405 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{V} = 44.05\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**Step (iii) — SQNR in dB:**

$$
SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\,\text{dB}
$$

**Final Answer Set:**
- $Q \approx 152.6\,\mu\text{V}$
- $e_{q,RMS} \approx 44.05\,\mu\text{V}$
- $SQNR \approx 98.08$ dB

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Question B]**
> **(a)** Compare the features of **CAN bus, I²C bus, and RS-485** with respect to: physical medium, number of wires, maximum number of nodes, maximum speed, maximum distance, and typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**. A speech signal has frequency components up to 4 kHz. Determine the minimum sampling rate. If the signal is sampled at 7 kHz, what is the aliasing frequency when reconstructed? Comment on the choice of anti-aliasing filter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

**Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | **CAN 2.0B** | **I²C** | **RS-485** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical medium | Twisted pair (CANH, CANL) | PCB traces (SDA, SCL) | Twisted pair (A, B) |
| Number of wires | 2 + GND | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 2 + GND (differential) |
| Max nodes | Unlimited (2²⁹ IDs) | 127 (8-bit address) | 32 (256 with repeaters) |
| Max speed | 1 Mbps (40 m) | 400 kbps (Fast) / 3.4 Mbps (Hs) | 10 Mbps |
| Max distance | 5 km @ 10 kbps | < 10 m (typical) | 1200 m @ 100 kbps |
| Duplex | Half | Half | Half (4-wire = full) |
| Application | Automotive ECUs, industrial | On-board sensors, EEPROM | Modbus RTU, factory automation |

---

**Part (b) — Nyquist Theorem (7 marks):**

**Statement:**
The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components higher than $f_{max}$ Hz can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$
\boxed{\,f_s \geq 2 f_{max}\,}
$$

The minimum rate $2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Step 1 — Minimum sampling rate for 4 kHz speech:**

$$
f_{s,\min} = 2 \times 4\,\text{kHz} = 8\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Aliasing frequency at $f_s = 7$ kHz:**
Since $f_s = 7$ kHz $< 2 f_{max} = 8$ kHz, the **Nyquist condition is violated**, and aliasing occurs. The input frequency 4 kHz is above the folding frequency $f_s/2 = 3.5$ kHz.

The aliased (apparent) frequency is:

$$
f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - f_s \vert = \vert 4000 - 7000 \vert = 3000\,\text{Hz} = 3\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Anti-aliasing filter:**
An analog **low-pass filter** must be placed **before** the ADC. Its specifications:
- **Cut-off frequency:** $f_c = f_s/2$ (i.e., 3.5 kHz at $f_s = 7$ kHz)
- **Roll-off:** Steep (≥ 4th-order Butterworth or Bessel) to attenuate out-of-band noise.
- **Practical choice:** Use $f_s \geq 10 f_{max}$ (i.e., 40 kHz) and design filter with cut-off at 4 kHz, giving substantial transition band margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly **lose 1–2 marks** in these ways:
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $f_s$, $Q$, and $e_{q,RMS}$ — examiners deduct strictly.
> 2. **Confusing SQNR with SNR.** SQNR is **theoretical** (ideal ADC); practical SNR is lower due to thermal noise, jitter, and non-linearity.
> 3. **In the aliasing question**, students write $f_{alias} = f_s - f_{in}$ (3 kHz) but **fail to comment** that this means **information is irreversibly lost** — the original 4 kHz component cannot be recovered.
> 4. **In protocol comparisons**, students list I²C, SPI, CAN, RS-485 but **omit columns** like duplex, encoding, or arbitration. Always use a **6–8 column table** for full marks.
> 5. **Formula** $SQNR = 6.02 N + 1.76$ is written as $6N$ (missing the **1.76 dB** correction) — examiners do check the constant.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Facts for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ **Nyquist rate** = $2 f_{max}$; always state both **minimum** and **practical (oversampled)** value.
- ✅ **Quantization step** $Q = V_{FS}/2^N$ — memorize formula and units.
- ✅ **SQNR (dB)** = $6.02 N + 1.76$ — this is the "**6 dB per bit**" rule.
- ✅ **4–20 mA** current loop: live-zero enables **broken-wire detection**; max 20 mA = sensor maximum; max wire length limited by compliance voltage.
- ✅ **I²C**: 2-wire (SDA, SCL), 7-bit address space (128 devices), **open-drain** with pull-up resistors, **multi-master** with arbitration.
- ✅ **SPI**: 4-wire (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS/SS), full-duplex, **master-slave**, very high speed, short distance (PCB-level).
- ✅ **CAN bus**: 2-wire differential, multi-master, **CSMA/CA + arbitration by message ID**, dominant bit = 0, recessive bit = 1, bit stuffing prevents long DC runs.
- ✅ **RS-485**: 2-wire differential, **half-duplex** (4-wire = full), up to 32 nodes, 1200 m max, terminated with **120 Ω** at both ends to match characteristic impedance.
- ✅ **Baud rate vs Bit rate**: Bit rate = Baud rate × $\log_2(M)$ where $M$ = signal levels (for binary NRZ, they are equal).
- ✅ **UART frame**: Start bit (0) + Data (5–9 bits) + Parity (optional) + Stop bit(s) (1) — **LSB first**.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is **analog**, placed **before** the ADC; it must be at least **2nd order** in practice (4th-order Butterworth preferred).
- ✅ **OSI model** — CAN implements only Layers 1+2; Modbus is a Layer-7 protocol that can ride on RS-485, TCP, or even CAN.
- ✅ **Fieldbus** = industrial network for distributed I/O; **PROFIBUS, PROFINET, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet** are common standards.
- ✅ **Wireless IoT protocols**: **Wi-Fi (high BW, high power)**, **Bluetooth LE (low power, short range)**, **ZigBee (mesh, low power)**, **LoRa (long range, very low data rate)**.
- ✅ **Industrial Wireless standards**: **WirelessHART**, **ISA 100.11a** for process automation.
- ✅ **HMI** = Human-Machine Interface (touchscreen / SCADA panel) — the *output* device where the operator sees the system state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Protocol Distinctions:**
> **"I Can Speak Powerfully"** →
> **I**²C — **C**hip-to-chip (PCB level)
> **S**PI — **P**eripheral (4-wire, fast)
> **P**rotocols like CAN, RS-485 — for **industrial** fields
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault findings mechatronics designs and case studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET833 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault findings mechatronics designs and case studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fault Finding in Mechatronic Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Finding (Fault Detection and Diagnosis — FDD)** in mechatronics is the systematic process of **identifying, isolating, and characterizing** deviations (faults) in the integrated mechanical, electronic, and control sub-systems of a mechatronic product so that the system can be restored to its intended operational state with minimal downtime.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, fault finding in mechatronic design is not merely "repairing broken parts" — it is a **holistic engineering discipline** that combines:
- **Hardware-level diagnostics** (sensor signal integrity, actuator response)
- **Software-level diagnostics** (model-based observers, parity checks)
- **System-level reliability engineering** (FMEA, FTA, MTBF/MTTR analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 expects students to (a) understand the *types* of faults, (b) apply *structured fault-finding methodologies*, and (c) appreciate *design-for-diagnosability* through real mechatronic case studies such as CNC machines, automotive ABS, and robotic arms.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body

Think of a mechatronic system as a **human body**:

| Body Component | Mechatronic Equivalent | Symptom | Diagnostic Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nerves (sensory) | Sensors, transducers | Numbness in finger | Continuity test, multimeter |
| Muscles (actuators) | Motors, solenoids, valves | Weak grip | Current/voltage measurement |
| Brain (controller) | Microcontroller / PLC | Confusion / lag | Logic analyser, debugger |
| Skeleton (structure) | Mechanical frame, bearings | Joint stiffness | Vibration analysis |

Just as a **doctor** first takes a *symptom report*, then a *physical examination*, then a *scan/report*, a mechatronics engineer follows a **layered fault-finding protocol**:
1. **Symptom observation** (alarm logs, HMI messages)
2. **Boundary test** (input/output checks at module pins)
3. **In-depth test** (oscilloscope, signature analysis, model-based residual)

### 1.3 Key Metrics in Fault Engineering (Mandatory Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **exam-favourite** in KTU valuations and must be memorized:
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = reliability indicator
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** = maintainability indicator
> - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
> - **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** = $S \times O \times D$ (Severity × Occurrence × Detection)
> - **Failure Rate** $\lambda(t)$ — measured in *failures per hour*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Faults in Mechatronic Systems

Faults are categorised along **three orthogonal axes**:

**A) By Physical Location**

- **Sensor faults**: bias, drift, complete failure, noise corruption
- **Actuator faults**: stuck-at, loss of effectiveness, saturation
- **Controller / processor faults**: program crash, memory corruption, I/O failure
- **Communication faults**: bus-off (CAN), packet loss (Ethernet/IP), EMI-induced bit flips
- **Mechanical faults**: bearing wear, backlash, lubrication failure, fatigue cracks

**B) By Time Behaviour**

- **Abrupt (step) fault**: sudden parameter jump — *e.g., a wire snapping*
- **Incipient (drift) fault**: slow parameter deviation — *e.g., thermistor ageing*
- **Intermittent fault**: appears sporadically — *e.g., loose connector*

**C) By Additivity**

- **Additive fault** $f_a$: sensor reading becomes $y_s = y_{true} + f_a$
- **Multiplicative fault** $f_m$: $y_s = (1 + f_m) \, y_{true}$

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" this classification matters:** KTU 14-mark questions often test whether you can **diagnose the fault type from a given symptom**. Incipient faults are the *hardest* to catch and form the core of *model-based* diagnostics.

### 2.2 Fault-Finding Methodologies — The Five Pillars

| # | Methodology | Core Idea | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Self-Test / Built-In Test (BIT)** | System tests itself at power-on | Consumer electronics, automotive ECUs |
| 2 | **Hardware Redundancy** | Multiple identical sensors/actuators vote | Aerospace, nuclear |
| 3 | **Model-Based FDD** | Compare actual output with model prediction (residual) | High-end CNC, robotics |
| 4 | **Knowledge-Based / Expert Systems** | Rule-base (IF–THEN) reasoning | Complex process plants |
| 5 | **Data-Driven (ML / statistics)** | Train on historical failure signatures | Industry 4.0, predictive maintenance |

### 2.3 FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)

A *bottom-up* inductive method. For every component, list:
- **Failure Mode** — *how* it fails
- **Effect** — consequence on subsystem/system
- **Cause** — *why* it fails
- **Severity (S)** — 1 to 10
- **Occurrence (O)** — 1 to 10
- **Detectability / Detection (D)** — 1 to 10 (lower = easier to detect)
- **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often gives a small table and asks: *"Compute the RPN and rank the failure modes."* Memorise the formula and the **1–10 scale** ranges.

### 2.4 FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)

A *top-down* deductive method. Uses **logic gates (AND, OR)** to combine basic events leading to a top-level undesired event. Probability of top event computed via Boolean algebra on the tree.

### 2.5 Reliability Block Diagrams & Series–Parallel Formulas

For a system of $n$ components with reliabilities $R_i$:

- **Series system** (all must work): 
$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

- **Parallel system** (at least one must work): 
$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

- **Exponential reliability** for constant failure rate $\lambda$: 
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | dimensionless | Cumulative failure prob. |
| Failure rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | per hour | Constant for exponential |
| Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Memory-less property |
| RPN (FMEA) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | unitless | Each factor 1–10 |
| Series system reliability | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | dimensionless | Independent components |
| Parallel system reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | dimensionless | Independent components |
| AND gate (FTA) | $P_{top} = \prod P_{basic}$ | probability | All must occur |
| OR gate (FTA) | $P_{top} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_{basic})$ | probability | Any one occurs |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar in $\vert x \vert$ (modulus) is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` so as **not to break the markdown table syntax**. This rule is enforced for all tables above and below.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ABS**: uses *hardware-redundant* wheel-speed sensors with voting logic.
- **Aerospace flight control**: *triplex* redundancy with mid-value selection.
- **CNC machine tools**: *model-based* thermal-error compensation.
- **Industrial robots**: *vibration-signature* analysis for bearing fault prediction.
- **Smart manufacturing**: *data-driven* anomaly detection on IoT edge devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: System Availability vs Component Reliability

**Statement:** Show that for a system composed of two independent components in *series* with constant failure rates $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, the system availability $A_s(t)$ is the product of individual reliabilities when repair is instantaneous (i.e., $MTTR \to 0$).

**Step 1 — Start with the reliability of component 1.**

For a component with constant failure rate $\lambda_1$ and zero repair time, the reliability (probability of *not failing* in time $t$) follows the exponential distribution:

$$R_1(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t}$$

**Step 2 — Write the same for component 2.**

By the same exponential-decay reasoning applied to the second component:

$$R_2(t) = e^{-\lambda_2 t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events.**

For a *series* system, the system functions only if **both** components function. Since the components fail independently, we multiply the survival probabilities:

$$R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the exponentials.**

Plugging the expressions from Steps 1 and 2 into the multiplication:

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t}$$

**Step 5 — Combine exponents using the power-addition rule $e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$.**

$$R_s(t) = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)\, t}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

The system behaves as a *single equivalent component* with an effective failure rate:

$$\lambda_{eq} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$$

So the mean time between failures of the series system is:

$$MTBF_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_{eq}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to availability in the limit $MTTR \to 0$.**

Using the availability definition $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, and letting $MTTR \to 0$:

$$A_s \to \frac{MTBF_s}{MTBF_s + 0} = 1$$

But in the **realistic case** with finite MTTR per component $r_1, r_2$, the availability is:

$$A_s = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda_1 r_1 + \lambda_2 r_2}$$

> This is the standard textbook form and a frequent KTU 14-mark derivation target.

### 3.2 Derivation: Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mechatronic assembly uses a DC motor with the following FMEA ratings:
- Severity $S = 8$ (high — safety concern if motor stalls)
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (moderate — happens occasionally)
- Detection $D = 4$ (moderate — current monitoring helps)

Compute the RPN and interpret the priority.

**Step 1 — Write down the FMEA formula.**

The Risk Priority Number is defined as the product of three ordinal ratings:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

Plugging $S = 8$, $O = 5$, $D = 4$ into the expression:

$$RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the multiplication in sequence.**

$$8 \times 5 = 40$$

$$40 \times 4 = 160$$

**Step 4 — Final result and interpretation.**

$$\boxed{RPN = 160}$$

**Interpretation:** KTU's typical FMEA threshold considers RPN > 150 as **high priority**, requiring *immediate corrective action* — e.g., adding a stall-detection algorithm or upgrading to a more reliable motor.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fault-Finding Toolkit

| Symptom Observed at HMI | First-Check Subsystem | Tool / Test | Most Likely Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Sensor out of range" | Sensor + wiring | Multimeter, scope | Open circuit, EMI, sensor failure |
| "Actuator not responding" | Driver + power supply | Voltmeter, current clamp | MOSFET blown, PSU droop |
| "Controller reset loop" | Watchdog + power | Oscilloscope on $V_{cc}$ | Brown-out, software bug |
| "Position error grows" | Encoder + mechanical | Backlash test, encoder check | Coupling slip, encoder drift |
| "Overheating alarm" | Thermal sensor + fan | IR thermometer, tachometer | Fan failure, blocked vent |
| "Communication timeout" | Bus + termination | Logic analyser, TDR | Cable damage, missing resistor |

### 3.4 Worked Problem: Parallel System Reliability

**Problem:** A mechatronic safety interlock uses *two* redundant limit switches wired in parallel. Each switch has reliability $R = 0.99$ over a mission time of 1 year. Find the system reliability.

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration.** A *parallel* configuration means the system works if **at least one** switch works. This is the standard *k-out-of-n* with $k=1$ and $n=2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the unreliability of a single switch.**

$$F = 1 - R = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unreliability of *both* failing simultaneously.**

For independent events, the joint failure probability is the product:

$$F_{both} = F \times F = 0.01 \times 0.01 = 0.0001$$

**Step 4 — System reliability = 1 minus the probability that all fail.**

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - F_{both} = 1 - 0.0001$$

$$R_{parallel} = 0.9999$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Parallel redundancy raised the system reliability from **0.99 to 0.9999** — a 100-fold improvement, demonstrating why safety-critical mechatronic systems (e.g., emergency stops, brake-by-wire) use parallel redundant sensors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fault-Finding System

The diagram below captures the **hierarchical interaction** between the physical mechatronic system, the diagnostic layer, and the human/operator interface — the canonical structure taught in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechatronic Physical System] --> B1[Sensor Layer]
    A --> B2[Actuator Layer]
    A --> B3[Mechanical Subsystem]
    B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Controller / Processor]
    D --> E1[Model-Based Residual Generator]
    D --> E2[Rule-Based Expert Engine]
    D --> E3[Statistical Anomaly Detector]
    E1 --> F[Fault Decision and Classification]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G1[HMI Display and Alarm Log]
    F --> G2[Safe-State Actuator Command]
    F --> G3[Maintenance Data Recorder]
    G1 --> H[Operator / Maintenance Engineer]
    H --> I[Corrective Action Loop]
    I --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows represent **information flow**, not necessarily physical wiring. The three diagnostic blocks (E1, E2, E3) correspond to the three pillars of modern FDD: *analytical*, *knowledge-based*, and *data-driven*. The dashed corrective loop closes back to the physical system, embodying the engineering philosophy of *continuous improvement*.

### 4.2 FMEA Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Identify System Functions] --> S2[List Potential Failure Modes]
    S2 --> S3[Assign S, O, D Ratings]
    S3 --> S4[Compute RPN]
    S4 --> S5{RPN Above Threshold?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Recommend Corrective Action]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Monitor and Document]
    S6 --> S8[Re-rate After Action]
    S8 --> S5
    S7 --> S9[FMEA Register Closed]
```

### 4.3 Fault Tree for a CNC Spindle "Fails to Start"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Top Event: Spindle Fails to Start]
    Top --> G1[Gate OR: Power Issue]
    Top --> G2[Gate OR: Control Issue]
    Top --> G3[Gate OR: Mechanical Issue]
    G1 --> P1[No Mains Supply]
    G1 --> P2[Fuse Blown]
    G1 --> P3[Contactor Failure]
    G2 --> P4[Controller Output Fault]
    G2 --> P5[E-Stop Active]
    G2 --> P6[CNC Program Error]
    G3 --> P7[Motor Winding Open]
    G3 --> P8[Bearing Seizure]
    G3 --> P9[Drive Belt Broken]
```

> **Reading the Fault Tree:** The top event propagates downward through OR gates. For an AND gate (not shown here, but used in safety analyses), *all* branches must fail simultaneously to propagate the failure upward.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students confuse **RPN (multiplicative)** with simple addition — RPN is *always* a product.
> 2. For series systems, students often add reliabilities — *wrong*. Always *multiply*.
> 3. Availability ≠ Reliability. Reliability is a *probability over time* with *no repair*; availability includes *repair* in the steady-state.
> 4. In FTA, an OR gate gives $1 - \prod(1-P_i)$, **not** $\prod P_i$.
> 5. When asked for "design recommendations," students forget to mention *both* hardware and software diagnostics — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for *design-for-diagnosability* principles.

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Risk Priority Number" (RPN) as used in FMEA. State the typical rating scale used for S, O, and D.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
RPN is a numeric indicator used in FMEA to rank failure modes by their relative risk. It is computed as:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where **S** = Severity (1 = no impact, 10 = catastrophic), **O** = Occurrence (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent), and **D** = Detection difficulty (1 = easily detected, 10 = undetectable). Higher RPN implies greater priority for corrective action.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for scale description.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between an "abrupt fault" and an "incipient fault" in a mechatronic sensor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Abrupt fault** appears as a sudden step change in the sensor output, typically caused by a discrete failure event such as wire breakage or connector loosening. Detection is comparatively easier.
- **Incipient fault** develops slowly over time as a gradual drift, typically caused by ageing, contamination, or calibration loss. Detection requires model-based or statistical techniques and is significantly harder.

*(1.5 marks each for the two definitions + 0 mark reserved for distinction clarity. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Solve either Option A or Option B in full.**

---

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **five major categories of faults** encountered in a mechatronic system with two examples each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sensor faults** — e.g., bias in a strain gauge, drift in a thermocouple.
2. **Actuator faults** — e.g., loss of effectiveness in a hydraulic cylinder, stuck valve in a pneumatic actuator.
3. **Controller / processor faults** — e.g., watchdog timer expiry, program memory corruption.
4. **Communication faults** — e.g., CAN bus-off condition, Ethernet packet collision overrun.
5. **Mechanical faults** — e.g., bearing pitting, gear-tooth wear, fatigue crack in a linkage.

*(1.4 marks per category — 0.7 for description + 0.7 for two examples.)*

**(b)** A mechatronic pick-and-place robot uses three identical servo motors in *parallel* (voting logic: takes the median). Each motor has reliability $R = 0.95$ per 1000 hours. Compute the system reliability and comment on the effectiveness of triple modular redundancy. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute unreliability of each motor.**

$$F = 1 - R = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — For triple modular redundancy (TMR), the system fails only if 2 or more motors fail.**

Number of ways to choose 2 out of 3 failing = $\binom{3}{2} = 3$.

Probability of exactly 2 failing:

$$P_{2F} = \binom{3}{2} R^1 F^2 = 3 \times 0.95 \times (0.05)^2 = 3 \times 0.95 \times 0.0025 = 0.007125$$

Probability of all 3 failing:

$$P_{3F} = F^3 = (0.05)^3 = 0.000125$$

**Step 3 — Total system failure probability.**

$$P_{fail} = P_{2F} + P_{3F} = 0.007125 + 0.000125 = 0.00725$$

**Step 4 — System reliability.**

$$R_{sys} = 1 - P_{fail} = 1 - 0.00725 = 0.99275$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** TMR raised reliability from **0.95 to ~0.9928**, an improvement of ~4.4 percentage points. TMR is highly effective when individual component cost is low and failure consequences are high (e.g., aerospace actuators).

*(2 marks for setup, 3 marks for computation, 2 marks for interpretation.)*

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supple Exam]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the architecture of a model-based fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) system. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A model-based FDD system has the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4 diagram):

1. **Plant (Physical System)** — the actual mechatronic system being monitored. *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Sensors** — measure physical variables (position, velocity, temperature). *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Reference Model** — a mathematical replica of the plant, implemented in software. It predicts what the sensor outputs *should be* under nominal conditions. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Residual Generator** — computes the difference (residual) between actual sensor output and model-predicted output. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Residual Evaluator / Threshold Logic** — decides whether the residual is "small noise" or "indicates a fault." Techniques: simple threshold, CUSUM, or statistical test. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Decision and Classification Module** — identifies the *type* and *location* of the fault and triggers alarms. *(1.0 mark)*
7. **HMI / Safe-State Output** — communicates to the operator and may switch the system to a safe state. *(0.5 marks)*

**Key advantage:** The method detects faults *without* additional hardware redundancy, using only *analytical redundancy*.

---

**(b)** A mechatronic assembly station has the FMEA data given below. Compute the RPN for each failure mode, rank them, and recommend an action plan. *(7 marks)*

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1: Belt slippage | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| F2: Sensor cable break | 8 | 3 | 6 |
| F3: PLC I/O card failure | 9 | 2 | 7 |
| F4: Pneumatic seal leak | 5 | 6 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute each RPN using $RPN = S \times O \times D$.**

- F1: $6 \times 7 \times 5 = 210$
- F2: $8 \times 3 \times 6 = 144$
- F3: $9 \times 2 \times 7 = 126$
- F4: $5 \times 6 \times 4 = 120$

**Step 2 — Rank in descending order of RPN.**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F1: Belt slippage | 210 | Highest |
| 2 | F2: Sensor cable break | 144 | High |
| 3 | F3: PLC I/O card failure | 126 | Moderate |
| 4 | F4: Pneumatic seal leak | 120 | Lowest |

**Step 3 — Action Plan.**

- **F1 (RPN 210):** Highest priority. Recommend installing a *tension-monitoring sensor* and a *vision-based slip detection* algorithm. Add scheduled preventive maintenance every 500 hours. *(2 marks)*
- **F2 (RPN 144):** Use *armoured cable* with *strain relief* at connectors; add a *self-test* routine that injects a known signal at power-on. *(2 marks)*
- **F3 (RPN 126):** Maintain a *spare I/O card on-site*; use a *redundant card* in critical lines. *(1.5 marks)*
- **F4 (RPN 120):** Below threshold, but still monitor via *pressure-transducer trending*. *(0.5 marks)*

*(2 marks for correct computation, 2 marks for ranking, 3 marks for justified action plan.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- [x] **Fault** = any deviation of a system parameter from its nominal value; classify by *location*, *time behaviour*, and *additivity*.
- [x] **FDD** has three pillars: *model-based*, *knowledge-based*, *data-driven*.
- [x] **BIT (Built-In Test)** is the simplest on-line diagnostic, run at every power-on.
- [x] **Hardware redundancy** (parallel sensors with voting) gives high reliability at the cost of weight, cost, and complexity.
- [x] **FMEA** is *bottom-up*; **FTA** is *top-down*; both are mandatory tools in mechatronic design reviews.
- [x] **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$, with each factor rated **1 to 10**; threshold of **150** is the de-facto industry cutoff.
- [x] **Series system** $R_s = \prod R_i$; **Parallel system** $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- [x] **Exponential reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ holds for *constant* failure rate (memory-less property).
- [x] **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — distinguish clearly from *reliability*.
- [x] **FTA AND gate**: $P_{top} = \prod P_{basic}$. **OR gate**: $P_{top} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_{basic})$.
- [x] **TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy)** uses median voting and is the standard for safety-critical mechatronics.
- [x] **Case studies to revise**: CNC spindle (FTA shown above), ABS braking (sensor voting), pick-and-place robot (TMR), washing machine (BIT), industrial robot arm (vibration analysis).
- [x] **Design-for-diagnosability** = designing hardware and software *together* so that faults are *easy to detect* — this is the KTU Module 4 capstone theme.
